To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department is assisting with plans for new cricket facilities in the Belfast South and Mid Down area.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any steps his Department takes to ensure the consistent application of the Adoption Agencies Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1989 across adoption agencies, and (ii) whether his Department monitors or reviews variation in access decisions.
answer_text: The Adoption Agencies Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1989 (the 1989 Regulations) provide the framework for the operation of adoption agencies.  Implementation within HSC Trusts is monitored as part of the established organisational management structures and the accountability arrangements for arm’s length bodies of the Department of Health.  Within voluntary sector adoption agencies, implementation is monitored by the Regulation, Quality and Improvement Authority.The 1989 Regulations also allow adoption agencies to provide access to the information held in adoption case records, as they think fit for the purposes of carrying out their functions as an adoption agency.  Following concerns about how agencies were providing access to adoption records, in November 2023, the Department issued Circular FCPD 03/2023 and the ‘Access to Birth Records and Adoption Agency Case Records for Adults Impacted by Adoption Practice Guidance’.The Department provided training for all Adoption Agencies to support implementation of the ‘Access to Birth Records and Adoption Agency Case Records for Adults Impacted by Adoption Practice Guidance’, monitored implementation of the guidance for 12 months, and committed to reviewing the guidance after one year.		A substantial amount of work has been carried out already on the review of guidance.  Due to unplanned staff vacancies, this work was temporarily paused, but has now been resumed.Decisions on the release of information from adoption records continue to be complex, and require consideration of specific circumstances by experienced social work practitioners, on a case-by-case basis.  In addition to monitoring of the implementation of the guidance, monitoring of access to adult adoption records was carried out from January 2021 to September 2024 through the provision of a monthly data return to the then Health and Social Care Board on the number and source of referrals. Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCTs) have continued to collate this activity internally. My Department has recently reviewed the monitoring template with HSCTs, and it has been agreed that a revised template is required to more accurately capture service demand and strengthen the quality and assurance value of the data collected. The most appropriate future reporting arrangements have yet to be determined, including potential alignment with sixmonthly statutory function reporting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when he will commence overarching contractual and funding negotiations with Community Pharmacy NI.
answer_text: The current community pharmacy contractual arrangements in Northern Ireland centre around a funding envelope determined by the Department in the context of the overall funding available for Health and Social Care (HSC), and services commissioned by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group, in line with a three-year commissioning plan developed in collaboration with Community Pharmacy NI (CPNI). The Community Pharmacy Commissioning Plan 2025-2028 is based on the previously agreed community pharmacy contractual framework that is comprised of: (1) Practice Quality System; (2) Core Services; (3) Additional Services; and (4) Locally Required Services.Community Pharmacy in NI has benefitted from these arrangements, with yearly increases to recurrent funding, annual pay uplifts aligned with DDRB and exceptional investments such as for National Insurance increases. This has included uplifts to the non-global sum, the application of which is determined through extensive engagement with Community Pharmacy NI. Since April 2018, there has been an increase in Government funding for community pharmacy HSC service provision of almost £60m (57%). I recently approved a 4% inflationary uplift for 2025/26 which equates to £4.72m. Furthermore, in acknowledgement of recent concerns raised by CPNI regarding financial pressures arising from current price fluctuations for a number of medicines, and in order to support the network, I have approved an increase in the special advance payable to contractors. I trust this will be welcomed by the sector. This continuous and substantial investment has been made over many years, and I propose no change to the current arrangements at this time. My Department is facing severe financial constraints due to the growing demand for healthcare from an ageing population, soaring inflationary costs and a major deficit. Despite these challenges, there is an ambition to optimise community pharmacy’s contribution to population health through delivery of the Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan 2030 and the three-year Commissioning Plan.My Department remains committed to continued engagement with Community Pharmacy NI through the established fora, to oversee the delivery of commissioned HSC community pharmacy services in line with the available financial envelope and against the agreed Commissioning Plan, in order to ensure the provision of best possible services to patients and the public.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration he has given to suspending clawback to support the stabilisation of community pharmacy during the 2026-27 financial year.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department will publish progress on securing budget allocation for the Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2 project, to ensure accountability and transparency during each phase.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given that her Department is not currently allocating funding to the construction of roads or bridges, to detail the timescale for making decisions concerning diverting funding to public transport projects, such as the development of the Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2 project.
answer_text: I have not taken any budget decisions for future years as these are subject to equality impact considerations and a final budget outcome for my Department.However, I want to be clear that I remain committed to progressing key infrastructure and public transport projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given that the majority of the £124.5 million funding for the Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2 project is currently unsecured, to detail any steps her Department is taking to bridge the shortfall.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any budgetary contingency plans she has made to ensure the Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2 project is operational, no later than 2033.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has identified any further planning issues which may inhibit the delivery of any phase of the Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2 project.
answer_text: The Outline Business Case for the project was submitted to DoF on 25 November 2025 with an estimated delivery cost of £124.5m and this was approved on 22 December 2025.The current funding position means the project is being planned on a phased-delivery basis. Whilst a significant funding shortfall remains to deliver the full scheme, the funding envelope of £35m from our Belfast Region City Deal partners along with £13m, which I have committed from my Department’s budget, will allow commencement of the first phase of this project with an expected completion by 2030.At this stage, no further planning issues have been identified that would inhibit the delivery of any phase of the BRT2 project. This will continue to be monitored, as the complexity of work may necessitate change. Delivering the full BRT2 programme will require the collective support of Executive colleagues, particularly as resolving the funding shortfall depends on securing additional resources through a future multi-year budget, to ensure this strategically important project can proceed in full, enabling the scheme to deliver its intended benefits for connectivity, economic growth, and sustainable travel across Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how the current £100 million funding shortfall for the Belfast Rapid Transport project is to be addressed, beginning from the 2026-27 financial year.
answer_text: The Outline Business Case for the project was submitted to DoF on 25 November 2025 with an estimated delivery cost of £124.5m and this was approved on 22 December 2025.The current funding position means the project is being planned on a phased-delivery basis. Whilst a significant funding shortfall remains to deliver the full scheme, the funding envelope of £35m from our Belfast Region City Deal partners along with £13m, which I have committed from my Department’s budget, will allow commencement of the first phase of this project with an expected completion by 2030.At this stage, no further planning issues have been identified that would inhibit the delivery of any phase of the BRT2 project. This will continue to be monitored, as the complexity of work may necessitate change. Delivering the full BRT2 programme will require the collective support of Executive colleagues, particularly as resolving the funding shortfall depends on securing additional resources through a future multi-year budget, to ensure this strategically important project can proceed in full, enabling the scheme to deliver its intended benefits for connectivity, economic growth, and sustainable travel across Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any additional bus services to be provided on the route from Belfast City Centre to Cairnshill, given the delay to the Belfast Rapid Transport Phase 2 project.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink operates a network of services to connect communities and optimise journey options to best meet the overall demand, within its existing resource levels and a challenging funding position. Recognising the growth of communities, Translink would welcome opportunities to further align its bus services with emerging demand to aid modal shift and to increase the numbers of customers choosing public transport. However, as Translink continue to provide the network within a very challenging financial context there are no immediate plans to provide additional services on the Cairnshill route. At present Translink provide the following combined service level from Cairnshill (via service 652) and Carryduff (via services 520 and 515):Days Service Number Outbound Inbound Mon - Fri 652 15 18 Mon - Fri 520/515 37 32   Total for Mon - Fri 52 50 Sat 520/515 36 35 Sun 520/515 18 18   Translink will continue to monitor and review its existing service provision to Cairnshill and identify and implement timetable adjustments to improve the existing service where funding and resource constraints permit.

answer_text: The Outline Business Case for the project was submitted to DoF on 25 November 2025 with an estimated delivery cost of £124.5m and this was approved on 22 December 2025.The current funding position means the project is being planned on a phased-delivery basis. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any legislative updates that will be made to the Race Relations Order, arising from the Framework for Racial Equality.
answer_text: A comprehensive review and public consultation on reform of the Race Relations (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 have been completed. Resulting policy proposals are currently under consideration, the outworking of which will provide the basis of updated legislation for consideration by the Executive and the Assembly. The enactment of these legislative updates will form part of the delivery of the Race Relations Framework.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department has carried out any assessment of the overlap between the Refugee Integration Strategy and the draft Framework for Racial Equality.
answer_text: The development of a Refugee Integration Strategy (RIS) was a recommendation in the previous Racial Equality Strategy and acknowledges some of the specific needs of those who arrive here seeking sanctuary. It is intended to sit alongside and support the vision of the draft Framework for Race Relations and assist delivery of its outcomes. We recognise that there are opportunities to align work across both strategies where there are shared needs and actions which can be delivered collaboratively to maximise outcomes for all, without negating the need to address bespoke issues facing refugees and those seeking asylum. This is an approach we are taking in delivering council hubs; a commitment in the RIS Delivery Framework. As set out in the draft Framework for Race Relations, we are seeking to expand the model to facilitate the provision of support, advice and signposting for all those who need it rather than solely refugees and those seeking asylum.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether any assessment has been carried out on the security of using Palantir as a provider of software, particularly for databases.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on implementation of the Refugee Integration Strategy.
answer_text: The Refugee Integration Strategy and associated Delivery Framework acknowledge the importance of successful integration, particularly when forced migration globally has increased significantly with more people seeking sanctuary from war and persecution. Work to deliver this year’s four priority actions in the Refugee Integration Strategy is ongoing with progress as follows:The establishment of resource hubs across council areas to provide support, advice and signposting for refugees and asylum seekers is well advanced;A skills and employment survey which sought to know more about the skills, qualifications and employment experience of refugees and asylum seekers, including any barriers to employment has been completed. Over 200 responses were received and analysis is underway;Funding was committed to provide regional immigration advice and information for asylum seekers and refugees; andThe Executive Office led, multi-agency Community Cohesion Group has been developing a range of interventions to help address the root causes of tensions. This includes interventions to enhance communications which we expect to receive for consideration shortly.Work has also commenced on the development of the first annual report on the progress of the Strategy alongside engagement with other departments to ensure we build on this work and agree further priorities for the year ahead.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any governance, transparency and ethical oversight arrangements currently in place to support the responsible use of artificial intelligence across the Civil Service.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on (i) the work; (ii) the priorities; and (iii) any projects underway, within the Office of AI and Digital, since its establishment in June 2025.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps their office is taking to ensure effective coordination of artificial intelligence policy and implementation across Departments.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the status of the development and implementation of an Artificial Intelligence Strategy and Action Plan for Northern Ireland, including how it aligns with the Programme for Government commitments on public service transformation.
answer_text: We established the Office of AI and Digital as a key commitment within our Programme for Government. The Office has been supporting the development of an Artificial Intelligence Strategy aimed at guiding responsible and ethical adoption of AI in the public sector, to contribute to modern, efficient and citizen focused public services.The drafting stage incorporated structured input from all nine departments, as well as contributions from an external advisory panel consisting of experts from the Artificial Intelligence Collaboration Centre (AICC), industry partners, and academic institutions. We will shortly launch a public consultation on the Strategy to invite broader stakeholder and public feedback on the proposed framework. This consultation will help us refine and finalise the Strategy to ensure it is evidencebased, robust, and aligned with our needs. Development of our AI capability is a key driver of wider public sector transformation, so this work is closely aligned to the work of the Public Sector Transformation Board. Our officials continue to engage across the NICS to map the interdependencies, opportunities, risks, and capacity requirements that will help shape a detailed AI Action Plan.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any plans their office has to develop the digital and artificial intelligence skills base within the Civil Service to support public service reform; and (ii) any measurable targets, timelines and indicators that have been set to assess progress.
answer_text: Developing digital and artificial intelligence capability across departments is a key commitment throughout our Programme for Government and is being taken forward as part of a wider, coordinated approach to workforce development.The Office of AI and Digital is exploring opportunities to collaborate with Digital Workplace Enablement Team (Department of Finance) digital and AI initiatives, such as promoting awareness through overviews / demos, Community Channels and drop-in clinics across the NICS.  The Digital Workplace Enablement Team also provides digital and AI support and guidance.  The combined approach contributes positively to improving digital confidence and capability across the workforce.Current activity is focused on establishing a baseline understanding of skills and capability across the Civil Service, supporting the development of targeted learning pathways, and promoting shared learning through cross-departmental networks and communities of practice. This approach recognises the need to build capability progressively, alongside the safe and responsible introduction of AI into public service delivery.As the AI Strategy moves through public consultation and towards implementation, this work will inform the development of clearer delivery plans, including priorities, sequencing, and appropriate indicators to support sustained capability-building over time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any amendments being considered to the Education Authority's whistleblowing procedures, arising from the Northern Ireland Audit Office report Raising Concerns in the Northern Ireland Public Sector, published on 19 March 2026.
answer_text: My Department adheres to the Raising Concerns at Work Policy (Public Interest Disclosure / Whistleblowing) available online and is currently considering all elements of the report including any implications for Arm’s Length Bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any current procedures, existing prior to the publication of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report Raising Concerns in the Northern Ireland Public Sector, for ensuring independent investigation when concerns are raised, and the Education Authority itself is potentially implicated.
answer_text: My Department adheres to the Raising Concerns at Work Policy(Public Interest Disclosure / Whistleblowing) available on the Departmental website. Section 9 ‘Concerns received regarding Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs) linked to the Department’ enables my Department to retain ownership of the investigation where Whistleblowing concerns relate to senior members of ALB staff or Board members. Consideration is also given to the role of the ALB Board (and Audit Committee) in dealing with any such cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department and the Education Authority have to respond to the Northern Ireland Audit Office report Raising Concerns in the Northern Ireland Public Sector, published on 19 March 2026, particularly with reference to ensuring there are pathways to ensuring investigations on issues raised by whistleblowers are independent.
answer_text: My Department adheres to the Raising Concerns at Work Policy (Public Interest Disclosure / Whistleblowing) available online. Officials are currently considering all elements of the report, including ensuring that pathways are available to facilitate independence and impartiality and will respond to the Northern Ireland Audit Office in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any guidance his Department has given to the Education Authority with reference to enabling staff to raise concerns about behaviour in special education settings.
answer_text: The Education Authority has a Raising Concerns at Work (whistleblowing) policy which aims to ensure that employees and the general public have a mechanism, in which to raise concerns, identify wrongdoing, risk, or malpractice.https://www.eani.org.uk/publication/raising-concerns-ea-whistleblowing-policy-and-procedureSchools will also have individual policies and procedures in place to raise any concerns at work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any advice his Department has provided to the Department of Education and the Education Authority in relation to updating guidance in schools around allergy safety, in line with Benedict's Law.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any progress made on the implementation of Benedict's Law in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 41034/22-27 published in the Weekly Answer Booklet for week ending 6th March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department has completed with the Public Health Agency to update procedures and advice around access to emergency medication in public spaces for allergies.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any progress made on updating staff training with reference to allergies in schools, following the case of Benedict Blythe in 2021.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) Special Education Needs Inclusion Service Medical Needs team operates a central point of contact for schools and parents who have questions around inclusion of children with medical needs including those with allergies and anaphylaxis and provides a range of resources on its website to support teachers, non-teaching staff and parents in allergy management.  Since 2022 all EA catering staff receive general Food Safety training, alongside separate Allergen Management training. In addition to this, a Special Diets Management training module was introduced for all catering staff in August 2025. Each module is delivered to new staff upon appointment to their job, and the training is refreshed annually for all catering staff. Special diets and allergens have also been the subject of recent awareness-raising initiatives, including toolbox talks and quality assurance assessments. Individual pupils with an Anaphylaxis Care Plan or Individual Healthcare Plan will have specific support and training identified in these plans.   The pupil and their family should be known to the Paediatric Anaphylaxis Nursing Specialists team who can provide specific support, if required. Schools currently arrange completion of the relevant training via the respective Health and Social Care Trust.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any discussions they have had with the Minister for the Economy and other Ministers, in considering whether to mirror the Scottish Government's eligibility criteria for British National (Overseas) Hong Kongers, residing in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Executive Office has no remit in relation to decisions on eligibility criteria for student home fee status.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether any assessment has been made of their Department's report, Lived Experiences of Hong Kong BN(O) Visa Holders Living in Northern Ireland: A Short Research Report.
answer_text: We welcome the publication of this independent research report. Its findings provide valuable insights into the lived experiences of Hong Kong British National (Overseas) (BN(O)) visa holders and will help to inform approaches to integration under the auspices of the Refugee Integration Strategy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any progress made to provide a welcome pack for British National (Overseas) Hong Kongers living in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Welcome Hub for Hong Kong British National (Overseas) Visa holders is available on NI Direct website, it provides guidance and signposts to key services and support organisations.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any work that has been undertaken to bring together British National (Overseas) Hong Kongers, resident in Northern, since February 2024.
answer_text: Our Department’s engagement with the Hong Kong British National (Overseas) Visa holders has primarily been via the Welcome Hub available on NI Direct and through liaison with community groups such as the Chinese Welfare Association and Wah Hep.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister of Health concerning the impact on child health of the TinyStart programme.
answer_text: The Department of Health has confirmed that they do not fund TinyLife to take forward the TinyStart programme.  Therefore, I have had no discussions with the Minister for Health on the impact of the programme.  However, as a condition of receiving funding TinyLife are required to monitor and assess their programme with quarterly and annual reports submitted to my Department for review against objectives.The Budgets for 2026/27 have not yet been set by the Executive.  I am therefore not presently in a position to provide any commitments or timescales to funding. On that basis, no formal assessments or discussions of the impacts on children and families of any potential reduction or withdrawal of funding for the TinyStart programme has been undertaken at this stage.Any decision regarding future funding will be considered in the context of my Department’s 2026/27 budget allocation, the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's work to stop caravans staying permanently in public car parks, including any proposals that exist to update regulations concerning this matter.
answer_text: The Department’s approach is generally to seek removal through co-operation rather than by legislative means. Achieving such co-operation may require the involvement of local representatives and, on occasion, the police.Where agreement cannot be reached, Article 91 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 (1993 No. 3160 (N.I. 15)) provides that any person who, without reasonable excuse, encamps or maintains an encampment on a road, including with a caravan, is guilty of an offence.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in assessing the value of a Delivery Unit based on the Welsh model, to detail any consideration that was given to the structural reality that the Welsh Government has a single Permanent Secretary, unlike Northern Irelands system of fully independent Departments.
answer_text: In establishing the Delivery Unit, we have drawn on the principles and learning from a range of models, including but not limited to that adopted by the Welsh Government, while designing an approach that reflects our own distinctive circumstances. The Unit is intentionally flexible and its approach will continue to evolve as lessons are learned through evidence and experience, including what most effectively supports departments to deliver complex, crosscutting priorities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given it comprises of only four people, to detail any specific skills the Delivery Unit add to the Department of Health, which are not already available to it, in order to bring down waiting lists.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given that it comprises of only four people, to detail any specific skills the Delivery Unit adds, that are not already available within the Strategic Investment Board, given its role in championing reform.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any role the Strategic Investment Board had, or has, in the development of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategy.
answer_text: A range of experienced Strategic Investment Board (SIB) staff have worked on the EVAWG programme, to provide expertise across several key areas.   The integration of a full time Strategic Communications and Engagement Specialist assisted in establishing the EVAWG identity, fostering meaningful stakeholder engagement, and managing the successful co-design process. This includes delivering on the communications and campaigns elements of the Delivery Plan, raising awareness and providing information at a societal level, whilst ensuring that there is strong consistent messaging.       Other temporary support has been provided to support the strategic communications and engagement work-strand, as well as the development of the Change Fund.   Additionally, a part time Data Scientist has worked with the EVAWG team to develop a comprehensive and robust framework for measuring impact. EVAWG engaged SIB to provide specialist support in developing the suite of Outline Business Cases relating to preparatory works and to the first Delivery Plan. These documents have been crucial in establishing the programme’s foundation, with clear, robust and well supported evidence to support delivery. SIB also supported EVAWG on an ad-hoc basis including the development of the Equality Impact Assessment, a Policy Landscape Review, General Frontline Services Landscape Review, and the Evaluation of Belfast and Lisburn Women’s Aid Victims Support worker project.          The Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) process is being managed on behalf of The Executive Office by the Strategic Investment Board, who are the appointed Centre of Procurement Excellence for SBRIs here.SIB support to the EVAWG team has decreased during this financial year and we can confirm that no further embedded support will be provided from 1 April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the condition of the school hall roof at Rosetta Primary School, including (i) when the issue was first formally identified; (ii) any interim safety measures that have been put in place; (iii) any engagement that has taken place between his Department, the Education Authority and the school regarding remedial works; (iv) whether a time-bound programme of works has been agreed; and (v) when he expects the hall to be fully reinstated and returned to use.
answer_text: The assembly hall at Rosetta Primary School was closed in March 2024 on the basis of a structural engineer’s report. On the engineer’s advice, internal steel props have been erected to make safe the roof structure. While the roof works are being planned and delivered, the Education Authority (EA) has established a licence agreement with the neighbouring church to enable the school to use the church hall, with EA covering this cost. The EA has also installed a gate in the boundary wall to enable pupils to access the church grounds directly from the school site.	In parallel, a planning application has been submitted for a bespoke, standalone modular toilet block and storage provision at the school. EA officials are engaging with the school and will continue to liaise with them throughout the project.(iv) & (v) The EA appointed an external architect-led design team in January 2025 to review the works required to the roof of the school’s assembly hall. Following an initial structural survey, the scope of the proposed works was extended and a more extensive condition survey was initiated. The EA received the formal condition report from the design team on 4 March 2026 and this will now be incorporated into a feasibility report, to inform the business case. It is anticipated that the feasibility report will be completed during March 2026.A timeline for the work will be determined when the business case is completed and the preferred option identified. The EA will liaise closely with the school on a timeline for completion of the work.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they regard the specific definition of national and cultural identity in the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 as consistent with existing equality law, including at Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Office brought forward this legislation and carried out a Section 75 Equality Screening.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the definition of national and cultural identity in the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 should be regarded as specific to that Act and specific to national and cultural identity, rather than other types of identity.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of whether the term national and cultural identity in the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 is defined specifically with reference to the three offices established under the Act.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the operation of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: We are pleased to say that the Director of the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression, the Irish Language Commissioner and the Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition, all provided for under the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022, have been in post since the 13th November 2025. The NIO commenced further provisions of the Act on the 27th January 2026 to enable the Offices to carry out their statutory duties including the Act’s due regards duty on public authorities with regards to the National and Cultural Identity Principles. We met with the three successful candidates individually on the 9th February 2026 and we wish them well during their time in post.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the engagement it has had with other bodies on the issue of abuse and harassment of elected representatives.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on delivery of the Animal Welfare Pathway - Advancing Animal Welfare in Northern Ireland 2025-27.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on any work her Department has carried out since November 2024 on residents' parking schemes, in the Belfast City local council area.
answer_text: I have considered my officials’ recommendations in relation to residents’ parking schemes that will provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for schemes going forward, including those within the Belfast City local council area. On the 10th of March I announced the criteria that will be applied to these schemes, and that applications will open in April 2026. I remain committed to continuing to develop the schemes where work has already commenced such as the Iveagh area of Belfast where my officials are currently completing the preparatory work necessary to undertake a formal consultation, and for my Department to give consideration to a scheme that could address the emerging on-street parking pressures in the vicinity of the Ulster University campus in Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any revisions she is making to her Department's governance arrangements around active travel, following the Northern Ireland Audit Office report on active travel in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the NIAO report on active travel which recognised that the introduction of the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 significantly changed the active travel landscape in the North. My Department has accepted all recommendations contained within the report and the NIAO were formally notified of this on 27 November 2025. A copy of the Department’s response to each of the recommendations, including target implementation dates in 2026/27, has been deposited in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's assessment of the Northern Ireland Audit Office report on active travel in Northern Ireland, which shows that her Department did not meet its own Bicycle Strategy targets.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the NIAO report on active travel which recognised that the introduction of the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 significantly changed the active travel landscape in the North. My Department has accepted all recommendations contained within the report and the NIAO were formally notified of this on 27 November 2025. A copy of the Department’s response to each of the recommendations, including target implementation dates in 2026/27, has been deposited in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether her Department will be amending its stakeholder communication around active travel, in the light of a recent Northern Ireland Audit Office report on active travel in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the NIAO report on active travel which recognised that the introduction of the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 significantly changed the active travel landscape in the North. My Department has accepted all recommendations contained within the report and the NIAO were formally notified of this on 27 November 2025. A copy of the Department’s response to each of the recommendations, including target implementation dates in 2026/27, has been deposited in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on any work underway to reduce gynaecology waiting times, particularly colposcopies referrals; (ii) to detail any support that is available to patients awaiting these procedures.
answer_text: There is significant work underway across the health and care system to reduce waiting times and develop pathways for gynaecology-specific conditions within each Trust across Northern Ireland.Primary and Secondary Care teams are working collaboratively to ensure patients are seen in the right place, at the right time, by the right professional.  In addition to accepting referrals from primary care, a pilot initiative is underway to identify patients on secondary care waiting lists who could be managed within the Enhanced Primary Care Service.  Eligible patients will be assessed and treated by one of the 16 specialist GPs with enhanced skills in gynaecology.  This service currently manages referrals for pessary fitting, HRT management, initial assessment and treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding, and contraceptive management including coil fitting.  Secondary Care services are also developing action plans to address waiting times and implement relevant GIRFT recommendations within each Trust.  Between April and December 2025, additional non-recurrent funding has been approved of £3.9m to provide waiting list initiatives—both in-house and via the independent sector to address red flag or other time-critical assessments and treatments.  As well as Trusts carrying out administrative validation exercises, to review and cleanse current outpatient waiting lists, clinicians are also carrying out enhanced clinical validation of outpatient lists.  Additional outpatients are also in place to provide one stop clinics for colposcopy, hysteroscopy, smears and pessaries that cannot be provided in primary care.   Working through the Provider Collaborative my Department is seeking to build stakeholder confidence and momentum in the short term by maximising capacity and tackling the longest gynaecology waiters, while in parallel developing a sustainable, evidence-based, gynaecology service model at Trust-level and regionally.  The Elective Care Framework has provided additional recurrent funding to address service gaps in gynaecology.  My officials and Trusts are continuing to explore options to strengthen pathways and improve care for women with gynaecological conditions.  Some recommendations will require investment, while others can be achieved through modernisation and reform. With regards to Colposcopy Services, my Department has been working with Trusts to develop business cases to provide two Colposcopy and Hysteroscopy Specialist Nurses in each Trust across Northern Ireland. Once trained, these specialist nurses with be able to assess, diagnosis and treat women with several gynaecology conditions and address the waiting time for patients waiting on colposcopies. When trained, it is anticipated that these nurses will provide a minimum of 2 new patient colposcopy clinics per week, seeing 8 new outpatients per clinic.   This will support the current clinical capacity in Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on when abiraterone, used for prostate cancer treatment, will be routinely available in Northern Ireland, as it is in the rest of the UK and Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions. In practice this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also routinely available in Northern Ireland.Where NICE has recommended the use of abiraterone (Zytiga®) in the treatment of prostate cancer, this medicine is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, in line with the current NICE guidelines. Further information on this treatment is available at the following links:https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta259https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta387https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta951https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta1110In circumstances where NICE is yet to complete a full appraisal, or does not recommend a particular drug, the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) does not routinely commission that therapy for use in Northern Ireland. Northern Ireland does not have its own health technology assessment body or equivalent to NICE, Scottish Medicines Consortium or All Wales Medicine Strategy Group, that could make a bespoke determination for Northern Ireland patients in the absence of NICE guidance.My Department has recently published a review of HSC medicines access arrangements which recognises the need to take action to ensure continued access to clinically and cost-effective medicines for the Northern Ireland population on an equitable basis, including in cases where NICE have not made a determination, but use is supported by clinical evidence and best practice. Informed by the review’s recommendations, my Department plans to review the existing workforce infrastructure underpinning medicines access pathways to identify potential resource requirements needed to strengthen existing access arrangements, including for use of off-label and off-patent medicines where use is supported by clinical evidence and best practice. Any additional resource requirements for medicines access infrastructure, and additional costs associated with provision of access for off-label or off-patent medicines, will need to be considered within the context of my Department’s challenging financial position and competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any role the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill will have in amending the role of recruitment agencies.
answer_text: My Good Jobs Employment Bill does not include any direct amendments to the role of recruitment agencies. However, many of the provisions within the Bill will apply to recruitment agencies as they do to other employers.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the Good Relations Strategic Framework, which was due for publication in mid-2025.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any role his Department is undertaking in opening up, and enhancing, school hockey facilities for community use.
answer_text: Under my Curriculum-Led Capital Investment Programme one of the strands available to post primary schools is the provision of synthetic pitches to schools who do not currently have access to one.The intention of my Programme is to assist schools in delivering the Physical Education element of the Curriculum. However, there will be the possibility for schools to consider opening their facilities to the wider community and departmental officials will be working in conjunction with SportNI’s Your School, Your Club initiative to promote community use of these facilities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the preparations for the redress scheme as provided for in the Inquiry (Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses) and Redress Scheme Bill (NIA Bill 15/22-27).
To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQO3125/22-27, to detail any hockey pitches currently being funded under the Your School Your Club programme.
answer_text: Two hockey pitches will be funded through the Your School Your Club programme, with 2G synthetic surfaces to be installed at Belfast High School and Castlederg High School.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any guidance that exists for childcare providers on managing the timing of payments in a way which does not unfairly penalise parents, while enabling the provision to continue.
answer_text: Private childcare providers are independent businesses. My Department has no authority to intervene in their commercial decisions, including invoicing practices and billing cycles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the regulation of childcare providers, since he took up office.
answer_text: Under the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 HSC Trusts have responsibility for the registration and annual inspection of childcare providers. To ensure a regional approach and a consistent level of quality, my department developed and published the Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under 12 in 2012.The department is currently carrying out a comprehensive review of the Minimum Standards, which began in 2025 with a public consultation on two key issues for the sector: adult to child ratios and the requirement for all prospective childcare workers to provide a health declaration countersigned by their GP before taking up employment.  Analysis of the 495 responses received from a wide range of stakeholders has now been completed, and I will be publishing the department’s response in the coming weeks in advance of my officials carrying out the wider review of the Standards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQO3125/22-27, whether his Department has updated its list of hockey pitches which are available for play, with floodlights.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail when the last appointment to the board of the Strategic Investment Board was made.
answer_text: Two Non-Executive Directors were reappointed for a second term in 2020.  An Interim Chair was appointed in 2025.  Proposals for a public appointment competition are currently under consideration.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the original purpose of the Strategic Investment Board upon its establishment; and (ii) when this purpose changed.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how the value of the Strategic Investment Board is assessed.
answer_text: A review of SIB was carried out in 2023-2024. The draft review report concludes that SIB continues to be a very important organisation to the NICS and the wider public sector.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how the work of the Delivery Unit is distinct from the work of the Strategic Investment Board on transformation of public services.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any advice his Department has given to other departments concerning (i) a collective approach towards workplace ventilation audits; (ii) monitoring carbon dioxide levels in workplaces; and (iii) putting measures in place to improve ventilation, since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: My Department has not provided any advice on these matters. Responsibility for workplace health and safety and minimum standards for ventilation in buildings resides with the Department for Economy and Department of Finance respectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any advice his Department has provided to schools or education authorities arising from the joint union report on Good ventilation in schools and colleges and how to achieve it.
answer_text: The Department has noted the joint union report on ventilation in schools and colleges. The Education Authority (EA) continues to use the ventilation guidance it provided to schools in December 2021 which accompanied the rollout of carbon dioxide (CO2) monitors. Although this guidance was developed as part of the Covid-19 response, it is fundamentally about improving ventilation and remains relevant to school settings.Both the EA and the Department rely on the expert advice of the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI) when considering matters such as ventilation and the potential role of air filtration systems. All actions taken by the EA to date have been guided entirely by this expert public health and safety advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration her Department has given to providing advice to workplaces, based on the joint unions' report, Good ventilation in schools and colleges and how to achieve it.
answer_text: Employers responsible for schools and further education are required to comply with the health and safety duties outlined within the Health and Safety at Work (Northern Ireland) Order 1978 and subordinate regulations. HSENI is the health and safety regulator for such premises. HSENI is supportive of the aims of the joint union guide for union representatives / health and safety representatives entitled ‘Good ventilation in schools and colleges and how to achieve it’, namely, to promote the importance of good ventilation in schools and colleges, and to help educate trade union representatives to work together to improve ventilation across settings to the benefit of all staff and pupils. Guidance, in the form of an Approved Code of Practice, on how employers can comply with the regulations relating to the matters covered by the report was published by the Health and Safety Executive in Britain in 2013 and is applicable to the north of Ireland.  Further relevant information is also available on the Health and Safety Executive’s website.  HSENI is satisfied that no further guidance needs to be issued at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department has taken to improve ventilation across regional colleges and other providers of further and higher education, since the Covid-19 pandemic.
answer_text: My Department does not hold responsibility for college or higher education estates, as each manages its own operations.  Officials have liaised with both sectors and Higher Education institutions have indicated that no additional departmental investment has been made in this area, however its Estates teams continue to uphold all relevant legal and environmental standards.Further Education colleges have advised that pre and post the Covid-19 pandemic, all sector-wide ventilation and indoor air quality requirements had been implemented in accordance with government and industry guidance. Moreover, beginning in 2021, a more stringent planned and preventative maintenance regime was introduced across the entire sector estate, fully aligning with industry-standard maintenance specifications and guidance provided by the Chartered Institute of Building Service Engineers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any advice his Department has provided to other departments and agencies arising from the September 2025 joint unions' report Good ventilation in schools and colleges and how to achieve it.
answer_text: My Department has not provided any advice on these matters. Responsibility for workplace health and safety and minimum standards for ventilation in buildings resides with the Department for Economy and Department of Finance respectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any discussions that have taken place between his Department and the UK Government regarding the new £10 million Young Cancer Patient Travel Fund; and (ii) whether his Department intends to introduce an equivalent travel support scheme for cancer patients under 25 and their families.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have engaged with Young Lives vs Cancer on the issue of travel cost support for children and young people undergoing cancer treatment. I also met with Young Lives vs Cancer to hear their concerns directly. Health is a devolved responsibility in Northern Ireland, and decisions on the design and funding of local support schemes must therefore be taken within the context of the Northern Ireland Executive’s budget and priorities.While my Department has considered the charity’s proposals, given the significant pressures on the Department’s budget, it has not been possible to consider any additional financial support other than that which is currently available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment his Department has made of Young Lives vs Cancer’s proposed model for a Young Cancer Patient Travel Fund in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have engaged with Young Lives vs Cancer on the issue of travel cost support for children and young people undergoing cancer treatment. I also met with Young Lives vs Cancer to hear their concerns directly. Health is a devolved responsibility in Northern Ireland, and decisions on the design and funding of local support schemes must therefore be taken within the context of the Northern Ireland Executive’s budget and priorities.While my Department has considered the charity’s proposals, given the significant pressures on the Department’s budget, it has not been possible to consider any additional financial support other than that which is currently available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any estimates his Department has made as to the amount required to establish a dedicated young cancer patient travel fund; and (ii) any consideration his Department has given to accepting Young Lives vs Cancer’s recommendations.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have engaged with Young Lives vs Cancer on the issue of travel cost support for children and young people undergoing cancer treatment. Health is a devolved responsibility in Northern Ireland, and decisions on the design and funding of local support schemes must therefore be taken within the context of the Northern Ireland Executive’s budget and priorities.While my Department has considered the charity’s proposals, given the significant pressures on the Department’s budget, it has not been possible to consider any additional financial support other than that which is currently available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any steps will be taken to ensure that children and young people under 25 with cancer in Northern Ireland do not face additional challenges caused by the cost of travel to treatment compared to their counterparts in England, given that the UK Government has now accepted Young Lives vs Cancer's proposal for a £10 million Young Cancer Patient Travel Fund.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have engaged with Young Lives vs Cancer on the issue of travel cost support for children and young people undergoing cancer treatment. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of staff working in the Delivery Unit; and (ii) their pay bands.
answer_text: The Delivery Unit is comprised of four full time staff: one Grade 5, two Grade 7s, and one EO2. The unit is intentionally small and agile, with capacity to adjust its workplan and approach as its remit develops.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on how any imminent legislation may address human trafficking and the exploitation of workers, who have recently arrived in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Strategy 2024-2027 includes commitments to ensure that appropriate assistance and support is available to all victims of slavery and trafficking identified in the North and to support understanding of the need for supply chain transparency in respect of exploitation.	My officials and colleagues from the Department of Justice maintain regular engagement with counterparts in Britain to monitor emerging policy and legislative changes concerning labour exploitation, and there is regular engagement with the Gangmasters and Labour Abuse Authority via the Organised Crime Task Force Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Sub-group.		Looking forward, we will consider how best to work collaboratively across enforcement fields with the Fair Work Agency established by the British Government.		My Good Jobs Bill will also discourage exploitative employment practices through, for example, establishing enhanced rights of trade union access and increasing restrictions on the use of zero hours contracts.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 38323/22-27, to detail the statutes they have referred to.
answer_text: The Equality legislation referred to in AQW 38323/22-27 includes:Employment Equality (Age) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2006 regulation 37Disability Discrimination Act 1995Special Educational Needs and Disability (Northern Ireland) Order 2005Race Relations (Northern Ireland) Order 1997Fair Employment and Treatment (Northern Ireland) Order 1998Equal Pay Act (Northern Ireland) 1970Sex Discrimination (Northern Ireland) Order 1976Employment Equality (Sexual Orientation) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003Equality Act (Sexual Orientation) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2006Sections 75 and 76 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the provision of sports pitches, for sports with predominantly female participation.
answer_text: Under the Recreation and Youth Service (Northern Ireland) Order 1986, district councils hold the statutory responsibility for ensuring that adequate local sports and recreational facilities are provided.Sport NI has undertaken a review of sporting infrastructure across Northern Ireland, and the Chief Executive has advised as follows:-“Sport NI’s Infrastructure Review considers the supply-demand analysis of existing infrastructure stock, and the reported peak time demand in densely populated areas for pitches (including female pitch-based sports).  The Review also considers comparative supply analysis with other countries and the ownership (across club, community, school estate, district council and other organisations) of existing pitches. It analyses the potential availability of additional existing pitches for community use to address demand. The Infrastructure Review will form an evidence base that will initiate the engagement and consultation process for the Infrastructure Strategy. This will include female pitch-based participation needs and issues”.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans she has to ensure that training for solicitors, in both immigration and Northern Irish law, is available from academic institutions in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: While my Department is responsible for the oversight and funding of the Higher Education sector here, as autonomous institutions the universities are responsible for their course offerings and content. The professional training and development of solicitors is not a matter for my Department.The Higher Education Institutions may be able to provide the member with information regarding what is currently available in their course offerings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any information provided to people (i) leaving Northern Ireland; or (ii) entering Northern Ireland from Great Britain, concerning the transfer of benefits, such as the Personal Independence Payment.
answer_text: (i) A person moving from Northern Ireland to another part of the UK can generally transfer their benefit record but must report their change of address to the relevant benefit paying office. People in receipt of a disability benefit who move to Scotland must inform the relevant office of this change and must make an application for the equivalent Scottish disability benefit. More information about the effect on benefits of moving within or outside of the UK can be found under the relevant individual benefit pages on the NI Direct website or in NI Direct's "Going Abroad" section.(ii) Information on the transfer of benefits for those people moving to Northern Ireland from Great Britain can be found using the “Benefits: report a change in your circumstances” section of the GOV.UK website. This directs benefit recipients to the relevant Department for Work and Pensions office that should be notified. NI Direct's "Moving back to Northern Ireland" checklist also provides a summary of the necessary action required. Scottish disability benefit recipients planning to move to Northern Ireland should contact Social Security Scotland about for advice on claiming the equivalent benefit in Northern Ireland. More information on moving from Scotland can be found on the mygov.scot website.Finally, the Department’s Make the Call service can also be contacted for further advice about any moves to or from GB, or abroad, and to ensure a person is receiving all eligible benefit support.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the (i) business case; and (ii) projected costings, for the redevelopment of the former School of Music on Donegall Pass in Belfast, through the Urban Villages Initiative.
answer_text: Fitzroy Presbyterian Church plan to redevelop the derelict former School of Music building as an Arts and Creative Hub. The new build will provide studio accommodation for circa 20 visual, performing and mixed media artists, and will also provide a large room to run arts-based in-house learning programmes and events.The business case for the project is at an advanced stage. A range of options have been developed to repurpose the building with the cost of the options ranging from c£1.7m up to £3.6m. Urban Villages has allocated an indicative capital contribution of £2m against the project and it is likely that the eventual preferred option will require a capital funding contribution from at least one partner funder. It is anticipated that the business case approval will be secured by May 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions ongoing to implement improved hospital throughput.
answer_text: My Department continues to work closely with Health and Social Care Trusts to improve hospital throughput through a comprehensive programme of actions, recognising the significant pressures across emergency departments, ambulance handovers and wider patient flow.Improving throughput requires a whole-system approach. Each Trust has an unscheduled care improvement plan that brings together acute care, community services and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service. This is supported by daily operational oversight from the Regional Co-ordination Centre, which monitors pressures such as ambulance handover delays, repatriation between Trusts and discharge blockages.To further stabilise unscheduled care, £10 million has been repurposed regionally.  This is supporting short-stay and intermediate care beds, alongside the expansion of Same Day Emergency Care and ambulatory pathways, enabling earlier assessment and treatment and helping to avoid unnecessary admissions. Frailty at the Front Door services are now operational across acute sites facilitating early clinical decision-making, supporting admission avoidance and reducing pressure on inpatient beds.Trusts have strengthened discharge processes by ensuring timely senior decision-making, and expanding discharge coordinators and allied health professional capacity to accelerate safe discharge and maintain patient flow through wards. Improving social care flow is a key component of hospital throughput. Early Review Teams have now reviewed more than 4,100 individuals, with almost half requiring reduced or no home-care support, releasing over 12,000 weekly hours of capacity regionally. In 2025-26 alone, revised Early Review criteria have released an additional 4,961 hours per week.Regional protection of care packages during hospital admission was implemented in Quarter 3, ensuring packages are immediately available once patients are medically fit for discharge. Digital solutions are also improving capacity management: all five Trusts have now implemented Careline Live, which has delivered significant efficiency gains. For example, over 2,100 hours were saved in the South Eastern Trust between April and September 2025. A regional digital care-home bed availability platform is under development and is scheduled for delivery in 2026–27.The Trusted Assessor model is in place across all Trusts to reduce delays associated with care-home assessments. Work is progressing to develop a fully consistent regional model and will be completed in Quarter 1 of 2026–27, with the longer-term aim of moving to a seven-day service subject to resources. Engagement with care-home providers is ongoing, including a focus on the growing need for dementia capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when the economic benefit of the arts, was last assessed.
answer_text: This would be a matter for the Department for Communities to confirm, given its policy responsibilities for arts and culture.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he expects any additional consequential funding arising from the recently announced £1.5billion investment in arts, culture and heritage in England.
answer_text: The Department for Culture, Media and Sport’s £1.5billion investment is from its Spending Review settlement.The Executive receives Barnett consequentials from the changes to Whitehall Department budgets. However, the Barnett consequentials at a Spending Review are identified at Whitehall department level rather than individual programme level and therefore it is not possible to identify the amount of Barnett consequentials received by the Executive as a result of Whitehall spending on arts, culture and heritage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement he has had with the Minister of Finance to discuss a comprehensive audit designed to demonstrate the social value and economic benefit of the arts, for use not just by the Executive, but also by Councils in assessing funding priorities.
answer_text: I have not engaged with the Minister of Finance on this matter. The development of a research programme, to include issues around social value and economic impact of cultural activity, is one of the emerging actions of my Department’s Heritage, Culture and Creativity Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment he has made of the value of delivering equivalence in investment in the arts, to sustain sectors in Northern Ireland, which provide clear value.
answer_text: It is my assessment that equality of opportunity is a core principle that underpins the work to develop the new arts policy.   I wrote to the Arts Council setting out my expectations that younger people from more deprived areas, those living in rural areas and areas distant from Belfast; and those not currently engaged in cultural activities are given clear priority in the funding decisions the Council makes.I met with the Arts Council Chair, along with board members and I have been reassured by their response.  I will continue to work with the Arts Council to ensure that the arts are for everyone.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) which Department holds overall responsibility for consumer protections and quality standards for purchasers of new-build homes; and (ii) the respective roles of (a) his Department; and (b) any other Department, or public body, in this area.
answer_text: I have arranged for the information you requested to be placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any responsibilities that Trading Standards currently holds, in relation to (i) protections; and (ii) quality standards, for new build homes.
answer_text: My Department’s Trading Standards Service has local enforcement responsibilities for consumer protection under the Estate Agents Act 1979 and the Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 (DMCCA); however, neither of these Acts deals with quality standards.The DMCCA prohibits traders, including estate agents, builders and property developers, from providing false or misleading information to consumers, omitting material information, engaging in aggressive practices or breaching the requirements of professional diligence. The Trading Standards Service is responsible for enforcing the DMCCA here. My Department does not have responsibility for quality standards in building new homes. This is primarily enforced through Building Regulations, overseen by the District Councils’ Building Control services and supplemented by industry schemes like the New Homes Quality Board (NHQB) and 10-year warranty providers such as the New Homes Building Council (NHBC).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any responsibilities his Department currently holds in relation to (i) protections; and (ii) quality standards, for purchasers of new-build homes.
answer_text: My Department does not hold responsibility for quality standards for purchasers of new-build homes.Protections for homeowners provided for by my Department arise from the Defective Premises (Northern Ireland) Order 1975 as recently strengthened by the Defective Premises Act (Northern Ireland) 2024. The Act provides for protections for homeowners and tenants where dwellings are defective or unsafe, leaving them uninhabitable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any responsibilities his Department currently holds in relation to (i) financial; or (ii) legal, protections for purchasers of new-build homes.
answer_text: My Department has no responsibility for legal or financial protection for purchasers of new homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the current status of Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2, including anticipated timelines for completion.
answer_text: On the 25th February 2025 I was pleased to announce the next steps for the Belfast Rapid Transit 2 project which will see implementation of this innovative service across the North and South of the city, bringing the benefits already established by the Glider service to communities living along this route.The Outline Business Case for the project was submitted to DoF on 25th November 2025 with an estimated delivery cost of £124.5m and this was approved on 22nd December 2025.Funding of £35m has been allocated from our Belfast Region City Deal partners, and I have committed an additional £13 million from my Department which will enable the commencement of a phased delivery. While a significant shortfall funding remains, I am committed to working with my colleagues in the executive and our Belfast Region City Deal partners to identify opportunities to address this.Following approval of the Outline Business Case, my officials are now in the process of appointing engineering consultants to progress the detailed design phase.A detailed delivery programme will be developed following the appointment of consultants however it is currently expected that, subject to securing the additional funding required, the BRT2 project could be completed by 2033.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the recently published Individual Wellbeing in Northern Ireland Report 2024-2025, to detail how the effectiveness of interventions to improve wellbeing among disabled people will be assessed over the next 12 months.
answer_text: Interventions are monitored at both population and programme levels. The Executive Office uses official statistics such as the Individual Wellbeing in Northern Ireland report to track wellbeing at a population level and to examine inequalities among different groups in our society. For programmes delivered in The Executive Office, departmental statisticians work closely with policy colleagues at a programme and project level to provide guidance on how the effectiveness of interventions can be measured, supporting on the development of suitable metrics, advising on reliable data collection methods and ensuring robust analysis and reporting of results. Where it is relevant and possible to do so, data is broken down by disability to help assess the effectiveness of any interventions among people with and without disabilities. This approach is ongoing and will continue over the next 12 months and beyond.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the recently published Individual Wellbeing in Northern Ireland Report 2024-2025, to detail how the effectiveness of interventions to improve wellbeing in people living in deprived communities will be measured over the next 12 months.
answer_text: Interventions are monitored at both population and programme levels.The Executive Office uses official statistics such as the Individual Wellbeing in Northern Ireland report to track wellbeing at a population level and to examine inequalities among different groups in our society.For programmes delivered in The Executive Office, departmental statisticians work closely with policy colleagues at a programme and project level to provide guidance on how the effectiveness of interventions can be measured, supporting on the development of suitable metrics, advising on reliable data collection methods and ensuring robust analysis and reporting of results.  In relation to deprivation, data is generally collected based on the location of the project being delivered, rather than for individual participants.  As a proxy, this supports assessment of the effectiveness of interventions to improve wellbeing for people living in the most deprived areas.   This approach is ongoing and will continue over the next 12 months and beyond.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the Individual Wellbeing in Northern Ireland Report 2024/25.
answer_text: We welcome the publication of this report on 21 January. It provides important data on areas that are key to the wellbeing of our population, such as loneliness, levels of life satisfaction, happiness and anxiety, and whether or not people think the things they do are worthwhile.Our Programme for Government highlights our commitment to working together to improve the wellbeing of everyone living and working here and this data will inform how we can best deliver a real and positive difference to people's lives.We have committed to reviewing the Programme for Government supported by a published Annual Report.  Progress is monitored throughout the year, and our Wellbeing Framework ensures we understand progress not just in pounds and projects, but the real-life impact it has on quality of life. All of the data is drawn from official statistics, published independently, and is available on the Wellbeing Dashboard.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any assessment made of the impact of funding reductions to the Arts Council of Northern Ireland on musicians; and (ii) whether funding will be restored and indexed to inflation.
answer_text: A new 10-year policy for the arts is currently under development and will seek to develop the evidence for appropriate support  and funding for the sector.Funding decisions for 2026/27 have yet to be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any support available for (a) emerging musicians; and (b) grassroots venues; and (ii) whether he will consider (a) targeted funding; (b) rates relief; and (c) a review of licensing frameworks.
answer_text: The Arts Council, as the body responsible for funding the arts, lists funding support available on its website https://artscouncil-ni.org/funding/. (a) Decisions on rates relief provisions are not a matter for my Department. (b) My Department is responsible for setting the legislative framework for entertainment licensing. Councils are responsible for issuing and regulating entertainment licences for indoor and outdoor venues. My officials are continuing to engage with local government counterparts in relation to the effectiveness of the current licensing framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether he has considered adopting a Fair Work model for the creative industries; and (ii) any steps being taken to improve income and job security for musicians.
answer_text: The Arts Council is aware of and is reviewing the Fair Work principles. It has published its guidance to funding recipients on Rates of Pay for Artists, with remuneration standards for artists, including musicians.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any care pathway that is in place for patients whose referrals to Primary Care and Talking Therapies services are declined on the basis that their needs exceed the duration or scope of commissioned short-term interventions.
