answer_text: Across Northern Ireland, Tier 2 Talking Therapies services provide short term, evidence based psychological interventions for individuals with mild to moderate mental health needs. Referrals whether via GP, self-referral, or community pathways are screened or triaged to determine suitability for Tier 2 intervention. When a referral falls within the scope of Tier 2 services, the individual is offered assessment and/or matched to an appropriate community, voluntary, or statutory provider.Where a referral exceeds the clinical scope, duration, or intensity of Tier 2 Talking Therapies, established pathways ensure that individuals are directed to the most appropriate higher tier service. These typically include Primary Care Liaison Services, Community Mental Health Teams (CMHTs), Psychological Therapies Services, Trauma Services, or Mental Health Assessment Centres, depending on presenting need. In all cases, onward referral is made with the service user’s consent, and the original referrer is updated to ensure continuity of care.Tier 3 and higher tier services across the region undertake their own triage or assessment processes. If an individual’s needs exceed the remit of the receiving service, clinical discussion determines the most appropriate alternative pathway, and the referral is redirected accordingly.If a person’s needs fall outside the scope, duration, or complexity of Tier 2 Talking Therapies, the referral is redirected rather than refused, ensuring the individual is placed on the correct pathway for the level of care required.Within primary care, the NI Local Enhanced Service (LES) supports access to counselling for adults with mild to moderate depression, delivered by practice-based counsellors using low intensity interventions (e.g., guided self-help informed by CBT principles). Where needs exceed what the LES can safely deliver, GPs refer the individual into the mental health assessment and treatment system through established Trust pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the trauma-informed psychological therapy services currently commissioned by his Department that provide treatment for complex post-traumatic stress disorder in line with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidance.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Clinical Guidelines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here.  Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. DoH Circular HSC (SQSD) 13/22 sets out process for endorsement, implementation, monitoring and assurance of Clinical Guidelines in Northern Ireland.  As there is no dedicated funding source to support the implementation of NICE Clinical Guidelines in Northern Ireland, any resources required are considered against other competing priorities. Nevertheless, HSC organisations are expected to implement guidance as far as possible within existing resources.NICE have published a range of guidance relevant to mental health. There are a significant number of recommendations contained within each NICE guideline and the extent of implementation varies across Trusts.In line with NICE guideline NG116, which covers both PTSD and CPTSD, individuals with post-traumatic stress disorder will typically be offered, after full assessment and as indicated, an appropriate psychological therapy, most often trauma-focused cognitive behaviour therapy or EMDR (eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing therapy).If Complex Post Traumatic Stress Disorder (C-PTSD) has resulted from a Troubles/conflict-related trauma care may be provided by the Regional Trauma Network (the RTN). The RTN brings together services provided by both the statutory (through the Health and Social Care Trusts) and community and voluntary sector organisations. (funded through the Victims and Survivors Service).   Most Adult service users seen by Psychologists will have complex trauma presentations in their personal experiences, even if it isn’t the initial reason for referral. Within Adult Psychological Therapy Services, service users often have a background of traumatic childhood experiences or of traumatic experiences in adulthood, such as domestic violence/sexual violence). Psychologists working within Early Intervention & Rehab services and within Inpatient MH services will also support service users with complex trauma presentations.It should also be noted that complex trauma can present outside Mental Health services. This can include Clinical Health Psychology Services (in relation to trauma related to healthcare diagnosis or experiences; Maternity Psychological Services (in relation to experience of birth trauma or a delayed impact of childhood traumatic experiences) and Neuropsychology Services (where understanding a diagnosis of Functional Neurological Disorder can include the impact of historical traumatic events). Psychologists working within all these areas have expertise and training in working with complex trauma as a presenting challenge.Complex trauma can also be present for children and young people. Psychologists working across all childrens services have expert

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has undertaken on the impact of incentives for domestic electric vehicle uptake, including (i) home charging provision; (ii) air quality; and (iii) public health.
answer_text: Decarbonising our Transport System is a core element of both the Executive’s Energy Strategy and the NI Climate Change Act (NI) 2022.  Road transport is responsible for a range of pollutant emissions, and the north has 17 Air Quality Management Areas declared because of NO2 emissions from road traffic. These primarily occur in urban areas experiencing high volumes of traffic.  The transition from diesel and petrol vehicles towards zero-emission vehicles is recognised as one of the key measures in reducing emissions from the transport sector. Increased utilisation and uptake of incentives represent a positive step towards lowering carbon emissions, improving air quality and delivering positive public health benefits. The Department works closely with key Stakeholders to encourage the uptake of all incentives that support home charging. Charging at home is the most cost effective measure for EV owners and by ensuring information about suitable grants is readily available on NI Direct and our own web sites and launching our new off street charging solutions pilot (which will allow ongoing monitoring and evaluation), we hope to make the switch to EV as easy as possible for those who live here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has undertaken of the potential environmental and public health benefits of domestic solar energy generation.
answer_text: Individual project proposals for domestic solar energy generation are subject to planning authority approvals, including the completion of any relevant environmental and public health impact assessments as appropriate.My Department keeps the wider impacts of energy technologies under review as part of ongoing policy development. Any future policy on domestic solar energy generation will include the completion of all statutory impact assessments.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the impact of the absence of a single equality Act on Northern Ireland’s compliance with Windsor Framework Article 2.
answer_text: Compliance with Windsor Framework Article 2 is a UK Government commitment. It is only for Departments where implementation falls within the devolved space. Whether the relevant equality law provisions are contained in a single act or several pieces of legislation has no impact on compliance with Article 2 of the Windsor Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any plans for new or expanded business incubation units and cluster zones to promote economic development.
answer_text: Developing a network of world leading business incubation units and innovation clusters is central to my Economic Vision. Through the City and Growth Deals programme, we are establishing dedicated innovation centres that will serve as clustering hot spots for our priority sectors. One example is the £70 million Momentum One Zero initiative led by Queen’s University Belfast, which showcases the scale of ambition and investment across the region.I recently attended the official event marking the start of construction of the new Momentum One Zero facility, which will cement the North’s reputation as a global destination for digital innovation, directly supporting economic development. Using funds to be provided for an Enhanced Investment Zone, we’re targeting a long-standing and significant gap in specialist wet-lab incubation space. This will provide flexible lab space, co-working areas and shared specialist equipment to help early-stage businesses start, grow and scale here in the North.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any progress made on extending age discrimination protections when accessing goods, facilities and services, to children under 18.
answer_text: We understand Clare Sugden MLA is taking forward a private members bill in relation to age discrimination.  Junior Ministers met with Ms Sugden MLA to discuss her proposed Age Goods, Facilities and Services Bill and the challenges in bringing forward legislation on this issue. Development of any age discrimination laws will require careful consideration and work to inform their scope, as well as required exemptions.No one in our society should be discriminated against because of their age.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether any work has commenced on the development of a single equality Bill for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There is no agreement on a single Equality Bill.However, a number of statutes have been enacted here over recent decades which provide protection against discrimination on the grounds of age, disability, race, religion and political opinion, sex and sexual orientation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any progress made in ensuring the effective delivery of English as a Second Language classes for displaced people.
answer_text: My Department, through the six Further Education colleges, provided ‘English for Speakers of Other Languages’ teaching to over 2,000 students in the 2024/25 academic year. The six Further Education colleges offer free ‘English for Speakers of Other Languages’ provision to a number of displaced groups, including refugees and asylum seekers.Furthermore, my Department has provided funding to Southern Regional College to deliver online learning tools, which are free to access for the public, and to create a new ‘English for Speakers of Other Languages’ Hub in Belfast Metropolitan College.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans their Department has to make the recognition of qualifications, and re-accreditation processes, more accessible, and affordable, for displaced Ukrainians.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy’s Careers Service has paid membership with UK National Information Centre (UK ENIC), the official organisation for the recognition and comparison of international qualifications and skills. This valuable service provides clients from other countries with proof of qualification equivalency in relation to the British education system, enabling them to access education and employment whilst they are living in the North of Ireland.Careers staff can provide a free letter of comparability to clients with qualifications from other countries, including Ukraine.The Department for the Economy is represented on the UK ENIC stakeholder reference group. This platform provides discussion and learning to improve services for all users, including displaced persons.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any milestones she has set for the drafting, introduction and passage of the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill insofar, as it provides for neonatal care leave and pay; and (ii) any risks that have been identified that could delay commencement of this entitlement, beyond 2027.
answer_text: The Employment Rights Bill will be introduced to the Assembly following agreement by the Executive. As I announced on 28 April 2025, I intend the right to Neonatal Care leave and pay to form part of this Bill.  Its introduction and its timing are dependent on the passage into law of this Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration her Department has given to supporting parents of babies, requiring neonatal care, prior to the planned commencement of statutory neonatal care leave and pay in 2027.
answer_text: In advance of the commencement of Statutory Neonatal Care Leave and Pay, a range of existing family-related employment rights—such as maternity leave and pay, paternity leave and pay, the right to time off for dependents, parental leave, and shared parental leave—remain available to support parents whose babies require neonatal care.I encourage all employers to treat parents whose children require neonatal care flexibly and with consideration of the stressful circumstances they are facing and to view the forthcoming statutory neonatal care leave and pay as a base level of entitlements.  Ahead of the commencement of the statutory provisions, I also encourage those employers who can do so to offer these supports at the earliest opportunity, to provide valuable assistance to families experiencing neonatal care.  Many employers already offer support above this level for their staff and I would encourage more to do so.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether, since April 2024, any Executive discussions or decisions relating to the Education Authority’s finances, budget allocations, or governance, included a recorded recusal by the deputy First Minister.
answer_text: Ministers are not required to recuse themselves from discussion, and as such there is no recorded information in relation to recusals. Ministers are required to declare an interest where they are proposing or supporting an action which is likely to, or perceived to, have a direct benefit to themselves or to another person or organisation with whom they have a close personal relationship or interest.  Such an interest was declared by the deputy First Minister in relation to these circumstances.Ministers have, as required, completed the Ministerial Register of Interests, a copy of which can be found at the Department of Finance website.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether, since April 2024, the deputy First Minister has made any formal declaration of interest in relation to her spouse’s position as Chief Executive of the Education Authority, when participating in Executive discussions or decisions concerning the Education Authority.
answer_text: The deputy First Minister declared an interest in relation to her spouse’s position as Chief Executive of the Education Authority during a discussion on the Education Authority’s budget at the Executive meeting held on 16 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps they intend to take in response to the recommendations arising from the independent expert reports on Historical Clerical Child Abuse, including whether (i) an action plan; (ii) budgetary provision; and (iii) a delivery timetable, are being developed.
answer_text: We are carefully considering the findings of the research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse and have not yet taken any decisions.Officials remain committed to keeping victims and survivors informed throughout this process.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether any decision has been taken on the publication of the report containing the findings of three independent research projects on Historical Clerical Child Abuse, and the agreed recommendations.
answer_text: We are currently carefully considering the findings of the research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse.  No decision has been taken on the publication of the research reports.Officials remain committed to engaging with victims and survivors to ensure they are kept informed of developments.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether the Executive Office has now received (i) a report containing the findings of three independent research projects on Historical Clerical Child Abuse, commissioned by their Department; and (ii) the six recommendations agreed by the inter-departmental working group.
answer_text: The Interdepartmental Working Group (IDWG) on Historical Clerical Child Abuse commissioned three independent research projects, all of which reported by summer 2025.  Based on the findings of the research projects, the IDWG developed six high level recommendations, in consultation with victims and survivors.  We are carefully considering the findings of the research and the IDWG recommendations.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any guidance or protocols on conflicts of interest, that apply to Ministers when participating in Executive discussions, or decisions, affecting a public body where a close family member holds a senior leadership role, including (i) how declarations of interest and any recusals are (a) recorded; and (b) made available; and (ii) when this guidance was last reviewed or updated.
answer_text: The Ministerial Code of Conduct requires Ministers to “ensure that no conflict arises, or could be reasonably perceived to arise, between Ministers’ public duties and their private interests, financial or otherwise, and comply with rules concerning conflicts of interest.”  Ministers are required to make a full declaration of all interests which might be thought to give rise to a conflict.  The Department of Finance collates and publishes this Register of Ministers’ Interests.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the current delivery status of active travel schemes, including Belfast Cycling Network routes, in South Belfast.
To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on referrals to the Hate Crime Advocacy Service.
answer_text: ANSWERReferrals to the Hate Crime Advocacy Service (HCAS) have risen steadily over the past three years, in part driven by the unrest in August 2024 and the racist attacks in Ballymena in June 2025. In 2024/2025 HCAS received 1369 referrals, an increase of 356 referrals over the previous year.  With three months remaining in the accounting period, referrals for 2025/2026 are on course to surpass this figure. HCAS plays an important role in supporting victims of hate crime through the criminal justice process, offering a safe and confidential space and signposting to other services such as housing, health and psychological support. I recently had the opportunity to visit the service in person, hearing first-hand from the advocates, and I would like to take this opportunity to thank them and recognise the vital work they do in supporting victims of hate crime.  While it is disappointing that our society requires this service, it is encouraging to see the increasing use of, and confidence in, a service which provides such critical support for victims of hate crime at what is, undoubtedly, a very upsetting time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any evidence base for her Department’s position that, removing unauthorised flags may cause community tensions; (ii) the number of (a) complaints; and (b) requests, her Department has received for the removal of flags in South Belfast, in the last 12 months; and (iii) any actions that were taken in response.
answer_text: When a complaint is received about unauthorised attachments to my Department’s equipment, my Officials will ask for advice from PSNI and other partner agencies, who may have contacts in local communities, to determine if removal action is likely to increase community tensions.A total of 36 requests were received for the removal of flags in South Belfast in the last 12 months. Of the 36 requests, in 27 cases, PSNI advised that removal was likely to increase community tensions. In the remaining 9 cases, they did not give an opinion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) what constitutes as clear community support, for the removal of unauthorised flags and emblems from Departmental property, as used in a recent response from her Department, including (a) any evidence that is required; (b) how such evidence is gathered and assessed; and (c) whether her Department has established any formal threshold or criteria for determining community support.
answer_text: My Department does not have staff trained or experienced in community engagement. Consequently, it relies on the expertise of partner organisations, such as the PSNI, to determine community support or if removal action is likely to increase community tensions.On all occasions when an enquiry is received about flags on our equipment, the Department will contact PSNI for advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that the outstanding adoption regulations are completed without further delay, given the impact this is having on vulnerable individuals, including victims and survivors of Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses who require access to their records in advance of the public inquiry.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment that has been made of teacher and further education lecturer pay in Northern Ireland since 2010, including (i) any impact on recruitment and retention; and (ii) any steps currently being taken, within the Minister’s remit, to address pay disparity with the rest of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: There has been no comparative assessment made of teacher and further education lecturer pay in Northern Ireland since 2010. Teachers' pay is negotiated through the Teachers' Negotiating Committee (TNC). The pay for further education lecturers is not within the remit of the Minister of Education.High-quality teachers are the most important feature of a successful education system, and I am determined to make teaching in Northern Ireland an attractive, high-status profession.In relation to pay for teachers and education in general, the sector remains highly competitive with teaching being popular amongst our young people and the profession held in high esteem.Pay Agreements have ensured that teachers pay has increased significantly from 2021 to date.  In 2021 teachers pay increased by 1%, with further increases year on year from 5% in 2022, 4.1% in 2023 with a consolidated £1000 bonus and 5.5% in 2024.The current offer to teachers of 4% from September 2025 will, if accepted, bring teachers starting salary in Northern Ireland level with their counterparts in England, with leadership salary levels being slightly higher in Northern Ireland. Teachers in Northern Ireland will be on broadly comparable scales to their counterparts in England and Wales.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any alternative options being considered by her Department for the relocation of Forge Integrated Primary School, in the event that planning approval is not granted to relocate to the former Knockbreda High School site.
answer_text: My Department acts as a statutory consultee to the Planning process.  No alternative options for the relocation of Forge Integrated Primary School have been submitted for our consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any oversight his Department has of care providers who employ Health and Care Worker visa holders, including any inspection or compliance measures specific to sponsored workers.
answer_text: Responsibility for any oversight of Health and Care Worker visa holders’ rests with the home care providers, as the employer, and the Home Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the anticipated timetable for reaching a decision on alternative accommodation options for Forge Integrated Primary School; and (ii) whether her Department has formally discussed contingency arrangements with the Department of Education and the Education Authority.
answer_text: My Department acts as a statutory consultee to the Planning process.  No alternative options for the relocation of Forge Integrated Primary School have been submitted for our consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any (i) criteria; and (ii) data, being used to evaluate the current night-time bus services operated by Translink, including how (a) passenger usage; (b) service reliability; and (c) daytime performance on the relevant routes, are taken into account when informing decisions on future provision.
answer_text: The current night-time bus services are part of a one-year pilot scheme being operated by Translink on Friday and Saturday nights. Although historically, late-night services only operated in the run up to the Christmas period, the 52-week pilot will provide a base to develop the consistency and dependability for these services in order to fully evaluate the demand and sustainability for late night bus provision.  The criteria and data being used to evaluate success of the pilot scheme includes:  Quality of service delivery (punctuality and reliability)Level of patronage and revenue generation; and (passenger volumes and fare income)Running costsIt will also consider how the pilot has supported the Strategy for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls. Translink routinely uses a range of available data when making decisions on service provision including all available route information such as daytime performance on specific routes.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any formal mechanisms currently in place to ensure timely information sharing with the UK Government, on developments relevant to Windsor Framework Article 2.
answer_text: Formal bi-monthly meetings are held with the Northern Ireland Office to discuss developments relevant to Windsor Framework Article 2 implementation in the devolved space.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether all Bills and subordinate legislation are now accompanied by documentation assessing compliance with Windsor Framework Article 2.
answer_text: It will be implicit in the Statement of Legislative Competence signed by a Minister before the introduction of a Bill to the Assembly, that the Bill is compatible with Article 2(1) of the Windsor Framework. The enhanced scrutiny procedures for subordinate legislation introduced on 5th January by the Chairpersons’ Liaison Group include an updated SL1 template to accompany all subordinate legislation. This requires Departments to set out the assessment carried out to ensure compliance with section 24 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, which includes compatibility with Article 2(1) of the Windsor Framework.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any progress that has been made in rolling out training for (a) Executive Office; and (b) Civil Service, officials on Windsor Framework Article 2; and (ii) when updated guidance for policy and legislative development will be published.
answer_text: Officials across the Executive Office and the wider Civil Service identify their training needs with support from line management and where gaps are identified undertake the necessary learning.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the anticipated timeline for the development and publication of the new Racial Equality Strategy; and (ii) any (a) key targets; or (b) priority actions, proposed for 2026.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any oversight his Department has of care providers who employ Health and Care Worker visa holders, including any inspection or compliance measures specific to sponsored workers.
answer_text: Responsibility for any oversight of Health and Care Worker visa holders’ rests with the home care providers, as the employer, and the Home Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with the Home Office, regarding the proposed changes to a fairer pathway to settlement for migrant workers.
answer_text: The Department has engaged with the Home Office on the outworkings from the Immigration White Paper, including an online session on the 20th November 2025 regarding the consultation paper, A Fairer Pathway to Settlement. Officials were subsequently supplied with an external stakeholder information pack, which has been shared with the relevant stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any safeguards currently in place to protect workers in Northern Ireland, who are employed under a visa sponsorship for employers, including any mechanisms for reporting exploitation or intimidation.
answer_text: Workers employed under visa sponsorship arrangements, which are determined by the Home Office, have access to the same protections as all workers here. These include protections relating to the minimum wage, health and safety, whistleblowing and unlawful discrimination. Information on employment rights can be sought from the Labour Relations Agency and breaches of employment law which cannot be resolved can be raised with an industrial tribunal or the Fair Employment Tribunal.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have undertaken any assessment of the potential impact on Northern Ireland of proposed changes to Indefinite Leave to Remain qualification periods.
answer_text: Immigration is an excepted matter, and the Executive has no decision-making powers in respect of immigration policy or reforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any stakeholder groups and organisations his Department has engaged with (a) in relation to; and (b) since the publication of, the University of Stirling's review of alcohol licensing in February 2025; and (ii) the form that engagement took.
answer_text: My Department has met with:Representatives from the Society of Independent Brewers,Hospitality Ulster and Binder Dijker Otte (BDO) NI,Free the Night.My Department has met and corresponded with:The Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service,The Lady Chief Justice’s Office.My Department has corresponded with:The PSNI,The Law Society NI,Members of the Campaign for Real Ale.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department has engaged with organisations representing local brewers, small producers and consumer groups in relation to alcohol licensing reform in the 11 months since the publication of the Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing system in Northern Ireland including the Surrender Principle.
answer_text: Yes. My Department has met with the Society of Independent Brewers and Associates and written to members of the Campaign for Real Ale.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any progress her Department has made on the development of the Belfast to Carryduff Greenway, connected to the St Ita’s site; (ii) the timelines for any planned papers or proposals; and (ii) how her Department intends to ensure that the redevelopment of the Carryduff Household Recycling Centre site, is incorporated into the surrounding public realm plans.
answer_text: Greenways are generally council led projects and Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council is leading on the development of the Carryduff Greenway, including the section of the proposed path between the boundary of Cairnshill Park & Ride and Alderwood Hill near St Ita’s primary school. My Department can provide up to 50% of the capital funding required to support delivery of approved greenway projects, and officials will continue to engage with the Council to support delivery of their active travel priorities as plans advance through the various stages of scheme development. Council is best placed to provide timelines on their planned papers or proposals in relation to this but I can confirm that they have not sought funding in my Department’s latest Greenways grant funding round.I understand that proposals for redevelopment of Carryduff Household Recycling Centre are currently at Pre-Application Notice stage, and therefore my department has not yet been invited to comment on any proposals. As the proposed public realm scheme is led by Lisburn & Castlereagh City Council, they are best placed to ensure that the schemes are co-ordinated effectively.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 35452/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 35452/22-27 was answered on 16 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made of the impact of existing alcohol licensing laws, including the surrender principle, on market access and affordability for small businesses such as micropubs, microbreweries and other community-focused venues.
answer_text: AnswerThe Licensing and Registration of Clubs (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 (“the Act”) amended the Licensing (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 to introduce a new licence permitting local alcohol producers to sell alcohol directly to the public. This amendment was introduced to address market access issues highlighted by local producers during the preceding policy development. Further assessment will be undertaken as part of the  Department's forthcoming review of the Act, anticipated to be launched early next year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 35450/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 35450/22-27 was answered on 16 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when he will answer AQW 35451/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 35451/22-27 was answered on 3 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any eligibility criteria used by (a) nidirect; and (b) HMRC, to determine when a child ceases to qualify for Tax-Free Childcare.
answer_text: HMRC is responsible for administering the Tax-Free Childcare scheme. Their published guidance, which can be found here, indicates that eligibility depends on a child’s age and circumstances, alongside other factors relating to the applicant’s employment, household income and immigration status. The guidance further states that a child ceases to be eligible on 1 September after their 11th birthday, or 1 September after their 16th birthday if they have a disability. In addition, nidirect (link) signposts those seeking information on Tax-Free Childcare to the HMRC guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the reason why access to the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme is restricted to families who remain eligible for Tax-Free Childcare; and (ii) whether his Department will consider amending this requirement so that families whose Tax-Free Childcare ends, solely due to the child’s birth month, are not excluded.
answer_text: Eligibility for Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) is aligned with Tax Free Childcare (TFC) to ensure administrative simplicity and integration with existing UK-wide supports.  By aligning with TFC, my department can streamline eligibility checks; avoid duplication with Universal Credit childcare support; deliver assistance quickly without a new administrative infrastructure; and target funding at families most likely to face higher childcare costs.While I have no plans to review the eligibility for NICSS at this stage, considerations on the future of the scheme will feature in the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy. The draft Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy for public consultation issued to my Executive colleagues on Friday 14 November, however formal sign-off has not yet been received.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale for, following three years of engagement through the Pre-Application Discussion process, DfI Roads has not issued its statutory consultee response within the required timeframe, for the planning application for the new build of Forge Integrated Primary School on the former Knockbreda High School site.
answer_text: My officials engaged fully with the pre-application discussions for the planning application submitted by the Department for Education for the development of a new primary school building for Forge Integrated Primary School.  The full planning application was lodged in 2025, and the Department’s pre-application advice in relation to access, movement and parking was not reflected within this submission. My officials are currently undertaking a robust analysis of the documentation submitted in support of full planning application for this site and will respond to the planning authorities consultation in the coming weeks. The Department are supportive of the development of the site, however, road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools and this must be integral to our assessment and response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure that patients presenting with complex post-vaccination symptoms are granted access to diagnostic testing and subsequent treatment and care.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care service treats individuals based on their need, regardless of how they were injured.I would encourage anyone who wants access to diagnostic testing and treatment to maintain contact with their own GP, who will be best placed to consider their individual clinical needs and tailor the most appropriate pathway for their specific requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures his Department is taking to ascertain the total number of people affected by a serious adverse reaction to the COVID-19 vaccine.
answer_text: All vaccines and medicines have some side effects, although not everybody gets them, and the vast majority of side effects are normally mild and short lasting.  Unfortunately, in some rare cases, an individual may be injured due to receiving a vaccine.  The number of actual injuries proven to be linked to vaccination is extremely small.  The monitoring of vaccine safety including adverse vaccine reactions is the responsibility of the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA).  My Department follows the advice of MHRA and would take immediate action if the MHRA advised there were indications that a vaccine (or any medicine) was causing an unusual number of serious adverse reactions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any training implemented across the health and social care workforce to ensure all clinicians are aware of known and less well-recognised post-vaccination syndromes.
answer_text: The UKHSA publication ‘Immunisation Against Infectious Disease’, also known as ‘The Green Book’, provides the latest vaccine related information for all health professionals and immunisation practitioners. The Green Book details side effects and adverse effects of individual vaccines as well as a section on how to report any reactions, see attached link for further information: Green Book: Chapter 9 Surveillance and monitoringSpecific e-Learning resources are routinely used for training all vaccinators for both the influenza and COVID-19 vaccination programmes and include mandatory vaccine-specific modules which include content on recognising adverse reactions to vaccinations and advice on reporting these incidents through the Yellow Card Scheme. Modules include links to Summary of Products Characteristics which also instruct that any adverse reactions should be reported via the Yellow Card Scheme. Vaccinators are also required to complete Patient Group Direction and anaphylaxis training as per the Resuscitation Council UK (RCUK) guidance:   Anaphylaxis guidance for vaccination settings | Resuscitation Council UK, and advised to complete the COVID-19 vaccinator Competency Assessment Tool: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/covid-19-vaccinator-competency-assessment-tool.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether (i) she has considered the findings of her Department’s evaluation report on the Local Links pilot; and (ii) any recommendations or next steps are planned as a result of the report.
answer_text: I welcome the findings of the Local Links Pilot evaluation report, which highlighted significant positive outcomes for asylum seekers, including improved mental health, better access to essential services, and enhanced community integration. Over 3,000 travel cards were issued during the course of the pilot, which provided six months free travel for participants, and survey results showed that 98% of participants felt free travel improved their mental health, while 97% felt that free travel helped them feel part of the local community.As the pilot was funded through the British Home Office dispersal funding, the Department was unable to continue the scheme beyond its initial term. However, the positive outcomes will inform future decisions, including potential changes to the Concessionary Fares Scheme. A 2023 public consultation on proposed changes to the eligibility criteria for the Scheme included an option to extend the Scheme to all asylum seekers and victims of human trafficking. Due to the significant financial pressures facing the Department, it was not possible at that time to introduce any changes which would increase the costs of the Scheme. This option to provide concessionary travel to asylum seekers will be reconsidered should the financial position change.The full evaluation report is available at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/local-links-pilot-evaluation-report.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether any actions arising from the new Refugee Integration Strategy, will include measures to address transport affordability for asylum seekers, in light of the Mental Health Foundation report, The Journey to Wellbeing, which indicated that 97 per cent of participants were unable to access transport due to cost.
answer_text: We are pleased that the Refugee Integration Strategy and its associated Delivery Framework were published on 29 May this year. Delivery will enhance and support our wider work on racial equality and work is ongoing with partners to progress priority actions this year and commence work on longer term actions.Under the auspices of the draft Strategy, asylum dispersal funding provided by Home Office was allocated to the Department for Infrastructure for the Local Links Pilot. It commenced on 1 November 2023 to provide asylum seekers here with free travel on Translink services for a 6-month period. The full evaluation report for the pilot is available on the Department for Infrastructure website and whilst it demonstrated positive outcomes, as the Pilot was Home Office funded, the Department for Infrastructure was unable to continue the scheme beyond its initial term. However, its positive outcomes will inform future decisions, including potential changes to the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department (i) is considering options to support asylum seekers with transport costs; and (ii) has undertaken any assessment of the findings of the Department for Infrastructure’s evaluation of the Local Links Pilot, which utilised Home Office dispersal funding to meet this need.
answer_text: The Executive Office is not responsible for the provision of transport for asylum seekers; however, we facilitated the provision of Home Office dispersal funding to the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) for its Local Links Pilot. Whilst its main purpose was to support efforts to disperse asylum seekers outside of Belfast, one of the pilot’s objectives was to also inform long-term strategic planning on the needs of dispersed asylum seekers in relation to access to transport. We are aware the of the positive outcomes within the Local Links Pilot evaluation and that DfI will use this to inform future decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any dedicated (i) mental health; and (ii) practical support, provided to applicants to the Vaccine Damage Payment Scheme.
answer_text: There is no dedicated mental health or practical support provided to applicants to the Vaccine Damage Payment Scheme.  The health and social care service treats individuals based on their need, regardless of how they were injured.I would encourage anyone who has applied to the Vaccine Damage Payment Scheme to maintain contact with their own GP, who will be best placed to consider their clinical needs and tailor the most appropriate pathway for their specific requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any representation made to the UK Government in relation to the reform of the Vaccine Damage Payment Scheme criteria, given that the scheme currently pays only for permanent damage with a 60 per cent disability threshold and does not currently cover compensation for temporary injuries or loss of earnings.
answer_text: My Department contacted the UK Government in July 2025 to ask if there were any plans to reform the VDPS, and to highlight concerns the Vaccine Injured and Bereaved Support (VIBS) NI members had raised about the scheme, including difficulties with the application process and the 60% disablement threshold.  We await a response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how his Department ensures that families who may require social work support are assessed consistently and communicated with sensitively; and (ii) any guidance in place to ensure parents are not discouraged from seeking support for a child with additional needs.
answer_text: Families in Northern Ireland who may require Social Work support are assessed consistently through the Understanding the Needs of Children in Northern Ireland (UNOCINI) Assessment Framework. Social Workers and other professionals contributing to the assessment must comply with the UNOCINI Standards and guidance which ensures regional consistency. Social workers are governed by the Northern Ireland Social Care Council (NISCC) codes of conduct which includes how Services users are communicated with sensitively. The Children (NI) Order 1995 provides the key legislative framework for supporting children with additional needs. The Order places a general duty on authorities to provide a range of social care services for children in need, including those with additional needs. At the point of initial assessment Health and Social Care Trusts signpost parents to appropriate services or develop a child in need plan or family support plan which includes services to meet the assessed needs of families seeking support for a child with additional needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that community nursing services are adequately staffed and available to families with complex health needs at all times, including during periods of annual leave, so that parents are not left without clinical support when a child becomes acutely unwell.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any pathways available for parents of children with complex needs who require urgent clinical advice outside of normal staffing arrangements; and (ii) how his Department monitors whether these pathways are functioning reliably.
answer_text: (i) In relation to any pathways available for parents of children with complex needs who require urgent clinical advice outside of normal staffing arrangements, Trusts have advised the following:-Western Trust: Parents access generic emergency services such as A&E; Out of Hours GP or Regional Emergency Social Work service.Southern Trust: Children with complex health care needs have open access to Acute paediatrics Wards in CAH and DHH.  These patients are well known to the nursing and medical teams.  Children can also be referred into the ward by the Children’s Community Nursing teams and Community Paediatric colleagues.  Families with children with complex needs also will have the ward details and contact numbers and can be reviewed at any time.  The families are aware that they should ring the ward in the first instance to alert the team of their concerns/worries. This allows staff to quickly triage the patient’s condition and determine the best area to attend based on clinical need at that time.  This arrangement is in place for acute Paediatric wards and works very well for families and the service.South Eastern Trust: Community Children’s services are available within South Eastern Trust and are provided Monday to Friday 9am-5pm. Additional scheduled support can be provided to meet the assessed need of a small number of children/young people and their families.  There is no funded service outside of normal staffing arrangements.  Parents are advised to contact the GP out of hour’s service or acute hospital services. Where a child or young person moves towards end of life care, the South Eastern Trust Community Children’s Nurses work closely with NI Children’s Hospice and Regional Palliative Care colleagues to provide care and support for the child and family.The Paediatric Physiotherapy service, through Children’s Complex Health will also provide support to those who are approaching end of life, out of contracted hours. The Child Health Partnership through the Regional Paediatric Palliative Care Network NI; are working to identify needs for Paediatric end of life care provision and agree how this need can be supported and developed across NI.Belfast Trust: The clinical teams will have formed good relationships with families and parents of children with complex needs and the children will be well known to the service.   The specialist nurses are a point of contact by agreement for families and they will organise an admission to the hospital if deemed required/appropriate through patient flow.Northern Trust: a number of children with complex physical healthcare needs will have open consultation to the acute paediatric wards both in Antrim and Causeway hospitals.  The children for direct consultation are determined by the consultant paediatrician managing their care and a flag is put onto Encompass.  These children are well known to the nursing and medical teams.  They can also be referred in by the Children’s Community Nursing teams and Co

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the (a) number; (b) location; and (c) capacity, of Learning Support Units in post-primary schools serving South Belfast, broken down by school management type; and (ii) the number of places currently available within the Controlled sector.
answer_text: The Education Authority is responsible for the development of specialist provisions. The location of these provisions is determined by the level of identified support required for pupils. Based on this assessment, a need for behavioural and Autism/social communication specialist provisions was identified within the South Belfast area.There are currently two schools with these types of provisions in South Belfast.  There is behavioural specialist provision in St Joseph’s College, a catholic maintained school, and an Autism/social communication provision in Breda Academy, a controlled school.  In the 2024/25 academic year, enrolment in these units was 29 and 8 pupils respectively.The Education Authority is aware of the high demand for places in the Belfast area and continues to engage with schools to discuss establishing provision to create further capacity for September 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the projected demand for post-primary Specialist Learning Support placements, in the South Belfast area, between 2026 and 2028, including the number of pupils with a Statement of Special Educational Needs identifying Specialist Learning Support as the appropriate placement; and (ii) to detail the current shortfall of places within the Controlled sector.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:(i)The EA has undertaken comprehensive planning for specialist provision, compiling demand projections based on historical data, expert insights, and anticipated trends. These projections are reviewed regularly as key milestones are reached, and actual pupil mapping is completed.Planning operates at Council Area level rather than specific localities. Current projections indicate that demand for Year 8 specialist provision places across all types within the Belfast City Council Area for September 2026 is approximately 130 pupils. For the period extending to 2028, the EA anticipates demand will remain consistent with 2026 levels. This expectation is based on the number of pupils currently attending specialist provision classes in primary schools across the Belfast City Council area. Detailed projections are carried out annually, informed by the individual needs of children and young people identified through annual review processes.The EA continues to actively engage with all schools regarding this priority issue, ensuring provision planning responds appropriately to identified need.(ii)The Education Authority does not segregate specialist provision projections by sector, as capacity planning is needs-based rather than sector-specific. However, current data indicates that fewer than 10 specialist provision places remain available within the controlled post-primary sector for the Belfast area for September 2026, representing significant capacity pressure within this sector. EA are committed to working with all schools to ensure children and young people are supported to attend a school within their local community with their siblings and peers and continue to work across all sectors to accomplish this vision.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 32852/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps (i) his Department; and (ii) the Education Authority, plan to take to ensure pupils with a Statement of Special Educational Needs who require a Learning Support placement can access appropriate post-primary provision, within a reasonable distance of home, in line with statutory duties and equality obligations.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) are committed to ensuring that pupils with a Statement of Special Educational Needs can access appropriate educational provision close to their homes.Since 2020, approximately 6,350 additional SEN places have been created across Northern Ireland. However, we recognise that significant challenges remain, with only 26% of our mainstream schools currently offering Specialist Provision classes. This has resulted in many children with SEN being unable to attend local schools alongside their siblings.To address growing demand, current assumptions show an additional 5,000 SEN places will be needed over the next decade in mainstream schools. The EA is actively working to establish new Specialist Provision classes in mainstream schools, including post-primary settings, to ensure pupils can access education in line with their needs identified within a reasonable distance of home.EA's SEN Capacity Team continues to engage directly with schools to discuss provision requirements for 2026, with a view to increasing capacity further. In November 2025, over 300 schools were invited to engagement events, with 64 schools attending.  EA have now offered the 236 schools that who did not/could not attend these events, 4 additional dates in December to engage directly on this important issue for our children and young people.We remain committed to meeting our statutory duties and equality obligations by ensuring access to education in line with their individual needs for all pupils with Special Educational Needs in their local communities with their peers and siblings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail whether consideration has been given to establishing a Digital Bill of Rights for Northern Ireland, to strengthen privacy and data protection standards.
answer_text: Privacy and data protection standards are governed by the General Data Protection Regulation (UK GDPR), which was adopted following the UK's withdrawal from the EU. This regulation ensures that personal data is handled securely and in accordance with strict data protection principles.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether the Executive Office plans to develop ethical guidance for public bodies on the use of biometric and facial recognition technologies.
answer_text: Public bodies are encouraged to follow best practice guidelines such as those from the Information Commissioner’s Office and industry standards, focusing on human rights, accountability, and proportionality in deployment.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail whether the Executive Office has engaged with the Information Commissioner’s Office regarding the use of facial recognition technology, by private and public bodies.
answer_text: The Executive Office has not engaged with the Information Commissioner’s Office on the use of facial recognition technology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps being taken to ensure that departmental digital procurement standards regulate the use of facial recognition technology in public buildings and services.
answer_text: Procurement policy does not itself set the standards for products or regulate their use.  I have no plans to amend procurement policy to seek to regulate the use of these technologies. The usage of facial-recognition technologies is governed by data protection legislation, which is reserved to Westminster.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any assessment that has been made of the human rights and equality implications of the increasing use of facial recognition technology in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: International standards apply and the Information Commissioner’s Office have produced guidance that is applicable to its use that all users must adhere to.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the next steps for their Department following receipt of research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps she is taking to encourage more motor insurance companies to operate in the Northern Ireland market.
answer_text: For motor insurance issues, my Department has the responsibility for Part VIII of the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1981, Insurance against Third-party liabilities. Part VIII mandates that all motor vehicles on roads or public places must have valid third-party insurance coverage in place. My Department establishes the regulations governing the issuance and validity of insurance certificates, ensuring compliance with statutory insurance requirements.I understand your advocacy for engagement with motor insurance companies; however, my Department has no legislative tools to compel these companies to provide services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any analysis her Department has undertaken regarding the factors contributing to higher average motor insurance premiums in Northern Ireland, compared with other parts of the UK.
answer_text: The evidence provided by the Association of British Insurers (ABI), based on Comparethemarket data, shows that the average comprehensive motor insurance quote here was £650.93 in March 2025—the seventh lowest of the 14 UK regions. This also reflects a notable decrease from £767.09 in November 2024.My Department has no direct legislative influence over the prices set by insurance companies, which are regulated by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA). Nevertheless, my Department has considered the ABI’s written evidence and continues to focus on work aligned to four steps of their ten-step plan, aimed at reducing costs for all drivers: improving road safety and infrastructure; supporting new and novice drivers; advocating for safety-focused vehicle technology; and working with partners to minimise uninsured driving.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had with the Financial Conduct Authority, in relation to motor insurance affordability in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Insurance is a reserved matter, with its legislation and regulation overseen by UK authorities, including HM Treasury, in London.I have engaged with the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) through correspondence shared with the Motor Insurance Taskforce – of which the FCA is a member. The correspondence reiterated local insurance affordability concerns raised at a bilateral meeting with the Minister for Finance and the British Government’s Minister for the Future of Roads. My Department remains focused on measures within its remit to help reduce costs for motorists, including: improving road safety and infrastructure, supporting new and novice drivers, advocating for safety-focused vehicle technology, and working with partners to minimise uninsured driving.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact of Northern Ireland’s personal injury compensation framework, on the cost of motor insurance premiums.
answer_text: My Department has no direct legislative influence over the prices set by insurance companies, which fall under Financial Conduct Authority regulation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions she has had with the Department of Finance, regarding potential regulatory or legislative measures to increase transparency and competition in the motor insurance market.
answer_text: Insurance is a reserved matter, with its legislation and regulation overseen by British Government authorities, including Treasury.My officials and I have engaged with the Department of Finance (DoF) and industry stakeholders on issues relating to motor insurance throughout the year.Finance Minister O’Dowd and I met with Lilian Greenwood, the British Government’s Minister for the Future of Roads, to discuss local motor insurance matters. During the discussion, we provided our input for consideration by the British Government’s wider Motor Insurance Taskforce and received a comprehensive update from Minister Greenwood on the Taskforce’s work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any measures his Department is taking to ascertain the number of people affected by a serious adverse reaction to the COVID-19 vaccine; and (ii) the number of people who have applied to the Vaccine Damage Payment Scheme.
answer_text: All vaccines and medicines have some side effects, although not everybody gets them, and the vast majority of side effects are normally mild and short lasting.  Unfortunately, in some rare cases, an individual may be injured due to receiving a vaccine.  The number of actual injuries proven to be linked to vaccination is extremely small.  The monitoring of vaccine safety including adverse vaccine reactions is the responsibility of the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA).  My department follows the advice of MHRA and would take immediate action if the MHRA advised there were indications that a vaccine (or any medicine) was causing an unusual number of serious adverse reactions. In relation to the Vaccine Damage Payment Scheme (VDPS), my Department (along with the other devolved health departments) plays no role in the VDPS, or any of the decisions reached in respect of individual cases.   Requests for the number of people from Northern Ireland who have applied to the Scheme should be directed to the Business Services Authority, which is part of NHS England, who administer the scheme on behalf of the whole of the United Kingdom.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any representation made to the UK Government regarding the reform of the Vaccine Damage Payment Scheme criteria, given that the scheme currently pays only for permanent damage with a 60 per cent disability threshold and does not currently cover compensation for temporary injuries or loss of earnings.
answer_text: My Department contacted the UK Government in July 2025 to ask if there were any plans to reform the VDPS, and to highlight concerns the Vaccine Injured and Bereaved Support (VIBS) NI members had raised about the scheme, including difficulties with the application process and the 60% disablement threshold.  We await a response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure that patients presenting with complex post-vaccination symptoms are granted access to diagnostic testing and subsequent treatment and care.
answer_text: The health and social care service treats individuals based on their need, regardless of how they were injured.I would encourage anyone who wants access to diagnostic testing and treatment to maintain contact with their own GP, who will be best placed to consider their individual clinical needs and tailor the most appropriate pathway for their specific requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what dedicated mental health and practical support is provided to applicants to the Vaccine Damage Payment Scheme.
answer_text: There is no dedicated mental health or practical support provided to applicants to the Vaccine Damage Payment Scheme.  The health and social care service treats individuals based on their need, regardless of how they were injured.I would encourage anyone who has applied to the Vaccine Damage Payment Scheme to maintain contact with their own GP, who will be best placed to consider their clinical needs and tailor the most appropriate pathway for their specific requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any training implemented across the health and social care workforce to ensure all clinicians are aware of known and less well-recognised post-vaccination syndromes; and (ii) how they are being advised to support patients to report to the yellow card adverse reporting system.
answer_text: The UKHSA publication ‘Immunisation Against Infectious Disease’, also known as ‘The Green Book’, provides the latest vaccine related information for all health professionals and immunisation practitioners. The Green Book details side effects and adverse effects of individual vaccines as well as a section on how to report any reactions, see attached link for further information: Green Book: Chapter 9 Surveillance and monitoringSpecific e-Learning resources are routinely used for training all vaccinators for both the influenza and COVID-19 vaccination programmes and include mandatory vaccine-specific modules which include content on recognising adverse reactions to vaccinations and advice on reporting these incidents through the Yellow Card Scheme. Modules include links to Summary of Products Characteristics which also instruct that any adverse reactions should be reported via the Yellow Card Scheme. Vaccinators are also required to complete Patient Group Direction and anaphylaxis training as per the Resuscitation Council UK (RCUK) guidance:   Anaphylaxis guidance for vaccination settings | Resuscitation Council UK, and advised to complete the COVID-19 vaccinator Competency Assessment Tool: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/covid-19-vaccinator-competency-assessment-tool. Both resources reference the Yellow Card Scheme and detail how to access. The Yellow Card Centre for Northern Ireland was launched in September 2023, and it has focused on promoting the MHRA Yellow Card Scheme through virtual training sessions, alongside a range of other promotional events.   Further information on awareness sessions and promotional events is available on the Yellow Card Centre Northern Ireland website at: https://belfasttrust.hscni.net/services/yellow-card-centre-northern-ireland/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail a timeline for access by women to three full cycles of fertility treatment.
answer_text: I remain fully supportive of the New Decade, New Approach commitment to provide up to three funded cycles of IVF. You may be aware it was included following a request by my party.Although the expansion in 2024 to one full cycle was welcomed, it was not possible to expand the service further due to funding constraints.My Department is continuing to work with the Regional Fertility Centre to explore the feasibility of expanding current capacity from within available resources. This is extremely challenging, as any expansion must be both affordable and deliverable without detriment to current provision and waiting times, which the team has worked hard to maintain after the disruption of the Covid pandemic. While I am cautious about raising expectations at this stage, I am assured that all feasible options for increasing provision are being actively considered. While planning work is ongoing, it is not possible to give a definitive implementation date at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of whether the Trading Standards Service has sufficient powers to (i) investigate; and (ii) take enforcement action against, retailers who sell fireworks illegally without the appropriate licence.
answer_text: The Department of Justice is responsible for the control of fireworks in Northern Ireland.  The Trading Standards Service does not have a role in investigating or taking enforcement action against retailers who sell fireworks illegally without the appropriate licence.  Any concerns about illegal sale of fireworks should be reported to the Police Service of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for his assessment of whether local councils have adequate powers to investigate, and take enforcement action, against retailers who sell fireworks illegally without the appropriate licence.
answer_text: The Department of Justice is responsible for the control of fireworks in Northern Ireland and has a service level agreement in place with each local Council.  Council Environmental Health Officers are warranted by my department, under the Health and Safety at Work (Northern Ireland) Order 1978, and can carry out enforcement activity associated with fireworks retailing, consistent with the council’s enforcement policy, and in accordance with any instructions from my Department.  Council officers work closely with my Department and the PSNI to ensure appropriate enforcement action against retailers who sell fireworks illegally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the financial protections in place for those purchasing new-build homes, in terms of quality standards.
answer_text: This would be a matter for the Department for Communities which has responsibility for housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the financial and legal protections available for those purchasing new-build homes, in terms of quality standards.
answer_text: My Department has no remit or powers in relation to financial or legal protections for those purchasing new-build homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has any plans to strengthen protections for those purchasing new-build homes (i) during the buying stage; and (ii) in the first year after purchase, in terms of quality standards.
answer_text: This would be a matter for the Department for Communities which holds policy responsibility for housing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to strengthen protections for those purchasing new-build homes (i) during the buying stage; and (ii) in the first year after purchase, in terms of quality standards.
answer_text: My Department has no remit or power in relation to protections for those purchasing new-build homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the powers of the Northern Ireland Trading Standards Service to support those purchasing new build housing, in terms of its engagement with the developer on quality standards.
answer_text: The responsibility for quality standards in building new homes here is primarily enforced through Building Regulations, overseen by the District Councils’ Building Control services and supplemented by industry schemes like the New Homes Quality Board (NHQB) and 10-year warranty providers such as the New Homes Building Council (NHBC). The NHQB is an independent, non-profit organisation that aims to improve the quality of new homes and customer service. It operates the New Homes Quality Code - New Homes Quality Code published, which sets high standards for registered developers and provides access to the independent New Homes Ombudsman Service for dispute resolution if a customer is not satisfied with the developer's after-sales service. Registration with the NHQB is currently voluntary, but widespread among major developers.Most new homes are covered by a 10-year warranty (e.g. NHBC's Buildmark policy), which is often a requirement for mortgage lenders. These providers have their own technical standards (e.g. NHBC Standards) that builders must adhere to, in addition to Building Regulations.My Department’s Trading Standards Service has local enforcement responsibilities under the Estate Agents Act 1979 and the Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 (DMCCA); however, neither of these Acts deals with quality standards.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any steps being taken to improve transparency, and visibility of communications and social media activity, relating to the work of the Northern Ireland Executive’s Bureaux in Brussels, Washington DC and Beijing; and (ii) an update on the communications plan for the Bureaux, including a timeframe for its publication.
answer_text: The Executive’s overseas offices have recently completed a refresh of their webpages and are enhancing their use of social media to share the ongoing work to showcase Northern Ireland as a great place to live, work, study, invest and visit. Regular reporting highlighting this ongoing work is also well advanced and will be available in due course.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how the Executive ensures consistency in applying human rights standards across its overseas work, including in jurisdictions that maintain trade, or military, links with Israel, during the conflict in Gaza.
answer_text: Devolved Governments have responsibility for the implementation of human rights obligations within their jurisdiction. Where there is a concern about the actions of other governments on human rights issues this falls within the field of international relations which is not a devolved matter. The aim of our international engagement is to promote our region as a place where people want to live and work, to visit, to study and invest, and to build the relationships that can help us deliver the Executive’s Programme for Government.When we engage with our international partners, we promote the values that are important to us and demonstrate how our experience of building an accountable political and democratic system can support business growth, strengthen the economy, and generate many wider benefits for all in society.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any (i) economic; (ii) educational; or (iii) cultural, benefits delivered by the Northern Ireland Bureau in Beijing, since 2020.
answer_text: The NI Bureau China works to promote our region as a place where people want to live and work, to visit, to study and invest, and to build the relationships that can help us deliver the Executive’s objectives.In the past five years the Northern Ireland Bureau China has delivered benefits to Northern Ireland through:Supporting local authorities in developing their friendship and sister city relationships and to explore opportunities for co-operation, educational engagement, cultural exchange, trade and investment.Hosting high level delegations to engage with senior Chinese officials to promote economic, education and tourism growth opportunities.Supporting our universities and further education colleges through representing their interests with education policy makers and delivery arms of key Partners Across Government in China. The Bureau supports NI universities and further education colleges through in person representation at partnership formal meetings, education fora, China based conferences, and alumni events.The Bureau promotes Northern Ireland’s culture and tourism offering through a broad range of settings and activities through events, conferences and broader speaking opportunities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any steps being taken to ensure that the work of the Northern Ireland Bureau in China aligns with the Executive’s human rights obligations, including in relation to reports of religious and ethnic persecution.
answer_text: The role of the Bureau in China is to promote our region as a place where people want to live and work, to visit, to study and invest, and to build the relationships that can help us deliver the Executive’s objectives. The Northern Ireland Bureau China noted the concerns regarding human rights violations raised at The Executive Committee in May 2025 with the UK Embassy in China officials with responsibility for human rights policy and advocacy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how the objectives, and spending, of the Northern Ireland Bureau in Beijing aligns with the Programme for Government, and the Executive’s Investment Strategy.
answer_text: The overseas bureaux in Washington, Brussels and Beijing support all the Executive’s Ministers and Departments and promote a consistent approach across government to maximise the opportunities of our international relationships and by promoting NI as a great place to live, visit, study and invest in.
