Our international teams work closely with overseas embassies and partners across government, including InvestNI and Tourism Ireland, to ensure policy and operational development considers the Northern Ireland trade circumstances, capabilities, and international market opportunities globally, to deliver on the Programme for Government objective to grow a globally competitive and sustainable economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the monitoring her Department will undertake of the experimental traffic scheme on the Ormeau Road and Saintfield Road, Belfast, in particular its impact on motorists at junctions in the vicinity of the Forestside Shopping Centre.
answer_text: My Department will closely monitor the experimental traffic scheme on all of the affected roads, including its impact on motorists. Monitoring will be undertaken through both real-time operational oversight and detailed data analysis.24/7 coverage of Belfast is provided by officials in the Traffic Information and Control Centre via an extensive CCTV network and signalisation can be adjusted to dynamically alleviate congestion. The Greater Belfast traffic signal network operates under Urban Traffic Control, using preset signal plans to manage traffic flows.  Traffic updates are provided on social media platforms and through the TrafficWatchNI website.In addition, the Department has invested in a suite of evidence-based, data-led tools to enable robust pre- and post-intervention comparisons. These include:Floating Vehicle Data (TomTom)which has been monitoring journey times and speeds across the network since September 2024;Bus Journey Time Data to assess public transport reliability and efficiency; andSmart Sensor Technology to capture multi-modal movement and volume data at selected locations.Together, these measures will provide a comprehensive understanding of the scheme’s impact on traffic flow and junction performance, supporting future transport planning and investment decisions.My Department will also review the scheme regularly in collaboration with Translink officials to ensure that public transport performance and wider network impacts are fully understood and addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following his Department’s ADHD needs assessment commissioned in February 2025, to detail any interim measures being taken to support adults with a diagnosis of ADHD who are currently unable to access assessment, treatment, or prescription medication through the health and social care system.
answer_text: There is currently no regionally commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Northern Ireland. ADHD service provision has evolved differently across Trusts in response to local population demand and expertise within HSC teams. Therefore, there is some specific service provision available for people with ADHD across the Region, but this is not delivered consistently and there are lengthy waiting lists across Trusts.Despite the lack of regional commissioning arrangements for Adult ADHD services, Belfast; Northern and Southern Eastern Health and Social Care Trusts provide a level of provision. Belfast Health and Social Care Trust have reconfigured a small resource (0.5 Consultant Psychiatry) from within adult teams to process referrals for ADHD support. Current waiting is in excess of 3500 patients, and waiting times for an ADHD assessment based on current provision, is 8 years from the point of referral.There is no commissioned Adult ADHD service in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT). In response to demand for Adult ADHD Services, the NHSCT decided to progress with a small, unfunded pilot for service users who have been referred to the Trust, to be placed on a waiting list and then offered an appointment with the Trust for pre-screening.  Detailed pre-screening will ensure assessments are offered where there is a high likelihood of Adult ADHD. Communication with individuals on this waiting list (known as the register) commenced in December 2023. Patients are placed on the register in chronological order of date of original referral. It is important to note that all patients placed on waiting lists remain under the care of their GP.South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (SEHSCT) in response to demand, has progressed a small unfunded service for those patients who have transferred with an existing diagnosis and who are prescribed medication and require monitoring as per NICE Guidelines.  For patients awaiting a diagnostic assessment, the demand for ADHD assessment far outweighs capacity, which is resulting in long waiting times of several years.SEHSCT receives referrals for Adult Mental Health Services via Mental Health Assessment Centres located in Newtownards, Lisburn and Downpatrick.  Individuals, including those with a potential/confirmed co-morbid ADHD diagnosis are offered a mental health assessment and are managed via the adult mental health core care pathway, where appropriate.There is no adult ADHD diagnostic service in the Southern Health and Social Care Trust, therefore there is no waiting list for ADHD diagnostic assessment.  If a person is diagnosed with ADHD in children’s services and required ongoing medication management by a Consultant Psychiatrist, they will continue to receive regular reviews by a Consultant Psychiatrist in adult mental health services.The Western Health and Social Care Trust is not commissioned to deliver ADHD assessment. The pathway for ADHD into WHSCT adult serv

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse.
answer_text: We recognise the profound harm caused by clerical child abuse and extend our deepest sympathy and solidarity to all victims and survivors of this appalling abuse. Throughout this process the Junior Ministers have engaged with victims and survivors to hear their stories and their views on what is needed. We are very grateful to all those who have participated in the process and shared their experiences. Work has now completed on three separate pieces of research: one examining records held by faith organisations and statutory agencies; one recording experiences and expectations of victims and survivors; and one examining the safeguarding policies and practices of faith groups. We have received the research reports and are giving them careful consideration. We also met with the independent chair of the Interdepartmental Working Group on 10 November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, further to the ongoing ADHD needs assessment, to detail any plans his Department has to integrate adult ADHD provision within broader neurodevelopmental and neurodiversity support services, to ensure equitable access to assessment and treatment for adults with ADHD and autism.
answer_text: The needs assessment, commissioned by the Department of Health in early February 2025, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, subject to future budget availability. The needs assessment will give recommendations for what such a service might look like, including proposed referral pathways and potential implementation and staffing costs. The needs assessment will take into account linkages between ASD and ADHD where appropriate, in order to ensure equitable access for service users with co-occurring neurodevelopmental conditions. It is planned that the work will be completed by November 2025. Given the importance of this work in informing future ADHD service provision, it is important that sufficient time is given to ensure a robust evidence base and proposals

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will publish a timeline of action for the implementation of each recommendation arising from the research on Historical Clerical Child Abuse, including (i) target dates; and (ii) the departments or agencies responsible for delivery.
answer_text: We are currently considering the findings of the research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse. Officials remain committed to keeping victims and survivors informed throughout this process.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail when they plan to publish the findings and recommendations arising from the research on Historical Clerical Child Abuse.
answer_text: We are currently considering the findings of the research into Historical Clerical Child Abuse. Officials continue to engage with victims and survivors to ensure they are kept informed of the HCCA work.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they intend to meet with the Chair of the Interdepartmental Working Group on Mother and Baby Homes, Magdalene Laundries and Historical Clerical Child Abuse, to discuss the findings and recommendations of the commissioned research on Historical Clerical Child Abuse.
answer_text: We have responded to the Chair of the Interdepartmental Working Group on Mother and Baby Homes, Magdalene Laundries and Historical Clerical Child Abuse to agree to a meeting and diary secretaries are working to find a suitable date

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the long-acting injectable form of HIV prevention treatment, cabotegravir (CAB-LA), which has been approved for use on the NHS in England, Wales and Scotland, is available to patients in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.On 17 October 2025, NICE published its final draft guidance recommending the use of cabotegravir (Apretude®) as an option for pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) alongside safer sex practices to reduce the risk of sexually acquired HIV-1 infection in adults and young people at high risk of getting HIV if they cannot have oral PrEP.  Final guidance is expected on 5 November 2025.When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdf .

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any plans his Department has to develop and fund a transition framework to support young people with autism, aged 18 to 25, in moving from children to adult autism services.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to introduce residents' parking schemes for built-up areas in South Belfast.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to improve public awareness of ovarian cancer symptoms and routes to early diagnosis, given that survival rates here remain among the lowest in Europe.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) works closely with the Department of Health, local Health and Social Care Trusts, and other partners to support a range of activities to raise awareness of ovarian cancer symptoms and routes to earlier diagnosis, including:Ovarian Cancer Awareness Month - each March the PHA issue a press release and social media messaging to increase awareness of ovarian cancer (https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/news/ovarian-cancer-awareness-month-be-aware-signs-and-symptoms-0). Social media - the PHA posts regularly through social media channels to promote cancer awareness messaging, providing information on the signs and symptoms of cancer, including ovarian cancer, and healthy changes people can make to help reduce their risk of developing cancer in the future. ‘Be Cancer Aware’ Website - all PHA cancer awareness messaging signposts to its ‘Be Cancer Aware’ website. The website provides information about the signs and symptoms of a range of cancers, including ovarian cancer, and signposts to recommended sources of support or further information (https://www.becancerawareni.info/signs-and-symptoms/ovarian-cancer).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether women who have undergone unilateral oophorectomy are entitled to routine ultrasound monitoring of the remaining ovary under health and social care services.
answer_text: For women who have undergone unilateral oophorectomy, ultrasound monitoring of the remaining ovary is not routinely conducted in any hospital in Northern Ireland unless there was a known pathology.  Whilst each case is considered individually, routine ultrasound monitoring is not standard for unilateral benign pathologies, unless the patient has pathology at time of original surgery or is high risk of malignancy as determined by genetics or family history. Furthermore, Belfast Trust have also advised there is limited evidence for screening the remaining ovary in cancer cases. The UK Collaborative Trial of Ovarian Cancer Screening showed no reduction in mortality through screening, indicating that there is limited evidence for routine monitoring.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what fertility preservation options, including egg freezing, are available under health and social care for women under 30 who have undergone unilateral oophorectomy due to benign tumours; and (ii) whether there are plans to expand eligibility for fertility support in such cases.
answer_text: Currently there are no publicly funded fertility preservation options for patients who have undergone unilateral oophorectomy due to benign tumours.  There are currently no plans to expand eligibility for fertility support in such cases.Fertility preservation is only available to those undergoing certain medical treatments that may impact on their fertility, such as those with cancer and transgender individuals.On 1 July 2006, my Department established links with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) whereby all guidance published by the Institute from that date is locally reviewed for applicability to Northern Ireland and, where appropriate, is endorsed for implementation in Health and Social Care.  This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent professional, evidence-based best practice guidance on the value of health care interventions. NICE have recently been consulting on updating their current clinical guideline ‘Fertility problems: assessment and treatment’, the consultation ended on 21 October 2025.  It is anticipated that the final guideline will be released on 19 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider instructing the Public Health Agency to update cervical screening information so that it explicitly clarifies the programme does not detect ovarian, womb, vulval or vaginal cancers.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency’s (PHA) public facing information and resources for all screening programmes are kept under regular review and are updated as necessary. The PHA has confirmed they will update the information provided on the NIDirect website to advise that cervical screening does not detect ovarian or other forms of cancer. PHA will also be making similar changes to their patient leaflets for the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme at their next review. In addition, the PHA will continue to increase public awareness of the signs and symptoms of ovarian cancer, through regular social media channels to promote cancer awareness messaging. This includes information on signs and symptoms, and healthy changes people can make to help reduce the risk of developing cancer in the future.  In addition, the PHA website provides information about signs and symptoms of a range of cancers, including ovarian cancer, which explains what people can do if concerned, and signposts to recommended sources of support or further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what action he will take in response to research by Target Ovarian Cancer showing that almost half of women in Northern Ireland mistakenly believe cervical screening detects ovarian cancer.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency’s (PHA) public facing information and resources for all screening programmes are kept under regular review and are updated as necessary. The PHA has confirmed they will update the information provided on the NIDirect website to advise that cervical screening does not detect ovarian cancer. PHA will also be making similar changes to their patient leaflets for the Northern Ireland Cervical Screening Programme at their next review. In addition, the PHA will continue to increase public awareness of the signs and symptoms of Ovarian Cancer, through regular social media channels to promote cancer awareness messaging. This includes information on signs and symptoms, and healthy changes people can make to help reduce the risk of developing cancer in the future.  In addition, the PHA website provides information about signs and symptoms of a range of cancers, including ovarian cancer, which explains what people can do if concerned, and signposts to recommended sources of support or further information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on (i) the allocation; and (ii) the timeline for the release, of grant funding to support the housing redevelopment scheme at Posnett Street, South Belfast, (iii) when work on the project is expected to commence.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Posnett Street, Belfast scheme is currently programmed to start in the 2025/26 year (subject to budget) with a completion date within the 2027/28 year.As with other programmed schemes, the allocation and timing of grant funding will be dependent in several factors associated with the delivery position of the scheme.In order for the scheme to commence on site, several key milestones of delivery will need to be reached. This would include planning approval, acquisition of land, award of a construction contract and confirmation of funding approval by NIHE Development Programme Group (DPG).At present, NIHE DPG has not yet received an application for funding approval for the scheme however we do anticipate that this will be forthcoming from the Housing Association and will be assessed accordingly.Programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department has conducted an assessment of the impact of restricting Postgraduate Certificate in Education bursaries and fee coverage to future Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics cohorts on (i) recruitment; (ii) retention; and (iii) subject diversity, within the teaching workforce.
answer_text: The purpose of the Initial Teacher Education (ITE) bursary scheme and the basis of both business case approval and the proposed evaluation of the pilot is to increase the number of both applications and enrolments in ITE in subjects which are experiencing teacher shortages. The Recruitment and Retention Survey carried out by my Department in 2024, along with both current and historical ITE applications data provided by the Higher Education Institutions, informed this decision.  Extending the scheme to include current students would not increase either applications or enrolments and would not, therefore, be deemed a Value for Money option that would fulfil the objectives of the scheme. Such an extension is also not within the scope of the approved business case, which was specifically designed to target future cohorts in shortage subjects to address recruitment challenges. It would also incur significant additional costs. Based on full enrolment against the approved intake of 580 students, the estimated total cost of extending the scheme to the current 2025/26 cohort across all subjects and the full duration of their studies is £10.75 million. The Education Sector is already facing an overcommitment of c£279m in 2025/26 for inescapable pressures only.I understand this is disappointing for those already enrolled.The pilot scheme will be subject to a full evaluation and future strategic workforce planning will continue to monitor teacher supply across all subject areas. This will help identify emerging needs and inform any necessary adjustments to the scheme’s remit to ensure it remains responsive to the areas of greatest shortage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will extend the newly announced Postgraduate Certificate in Education tuition-fee coverage and bursary to students, currently enrolled in the 2025–26 cohort.
answer_text: The purpose of the Initial Teacher Education (ITE) bursary scheme and the basis of both business case approval and the proposed evaluation of the pilot is to increase the number of both applications and enrolments in ITE in subjects which are experiencing teacher shortages. The Recruitment and Retention Survey carried out by my Department in 2024, along with both current and historical ITE applications data provided by the Higher Education Institutions, informed this decision.  Extending the scheme to include current students would not increase either applications or enrolments and would not, therefore, be deemed a Value for Money option that would fulfil the objectives of the scheme. This will help identify emerging needs and inform any necessary adjustments to the scheme’s remit to ensure it remains responsive to the areas of greatest shortage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the (a) workforce planning evidence; and (b) recruitment data, that informed limiting the Postgraduate Certificate in Education support package to Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics (STEM) subjects; (ii) whether she will include non-STEM shortage subjects; and (iii) the estimated cost of extending the scheme to the current 2025–26 cohort, across all subjects.
answer_text: The purpose of the Initial Teacher Education (ITE) bursary scheme and the basis of both business case approval and the proposed evaluation of the pilot is to increase the number of both applications and enrolments in ITE in subjects which are experiencing teacher shortages. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any engagement they have had with the UK Government regarding the safety, welfare and consular access for UK and Irish citizens, resident in Northern Ireland, who were aboard the Global Sumud Flotilla; and (ii) whether their Department will engage with the UK Government in relation to its obligations under international humanitarian and maritime laws.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2, with particular reference to the South Belfast route.
answer_text: On the 25th February 2025 I was pleased to announce the next steps for the Belfast Rapid Transit 2 project which will see implementation of this innovative service across the North and South of the city, bringing the benefits already established by the Glider service to communities living along this route.My announcement represented a initial £48 million investment in our public transport infrastructure and is one of the projects being taken forward by my department as part of the Belfast Region City Deal. Funding of £35m has been allocated from our Deal partners, with an additional £13 million from my Department to deliver a park and ride on the O’Neill Road.Park and Ride is a key enabler to encouraging behavioural shift and helping to drive down congestion and emissions and this phase will allow us to put in place key infrastructure which will complement the wider scheme.This initial phase of the BRT2 project will specifically see additional bus priority measures implemented from the City Centre to Park Road on the Ormeau Road along the southern route.The implementation of the full BRT 2 project will see the southern route connected to the already established Park and Ride at Cairnshill.Whilst the full cost of the BRT 2 scheme is in the region of £140 - £148 million, I am grateful to the City Deal partners for the allocation of £35m to the scheme and my officials are currently working through the delivery options that could be implemented, utilising this funding envelope.  The Outline Business Case is being finalised in line with the feasibility reports and delivery options.I am committed to progressing the entirety of the scheme and will continue to work with the Executive and the Belfast Region City Deal Partners to ensure that the appropriate level of investment is made available to deliver the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has been made of the risks to public health and water users, arising from the absence of real-time sewage discharge alerts in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) is responsible for monitoring water quality at bathing waters, including carrying out sampling, monitoring and investigations to assess the condition of the bathing water.  The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised that NI Water is currently working with DAERA to develop a pilot project for near real time Event Duration Monitoring (EDM) reporting to support DAERA in making assessments of public health risks for water users, particularly bathers.In addition, NI Water has advised it has approximately 655 EDMs installed and sufficiently verified to support effective use for operational maintenance and monitoring.    For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com .   Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any measures her Department is taking to ensure that Northern Ireland Water releases comprehensive, real-time data, on sewage discharges, in line with practice across other UK jurisdictions.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:The Environment Act 2021 imposes a duty on water companies in Britain to publish wastewater overflow data from storm overflows within an hour of the discharge beginning however, this legislative requirement does not apply here.NI Water continues to work with the Utility Regulator and the Northern Ireland Environment Agency regarding reporting requirements for storm overflows.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline (i) the steps her Department is taking to ensure full monitoring of all Combined Sewage Overflows across Northern Ireland; and (ii) the timeframe within which this will be achieved.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:To monitor and record spills from combined sewer overflows, Event Duration Monitors (EDMs) are being installed across NI Water’s wastewater network over a multi-year investment programme. These EDMs measure the times when a spill is occurring and for how long it lasts. NI Water is taking a phased approach to EDM installation, as agreed with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA). NI Water’s initial focus has been to install EDMs on overflows which spill into the north’s 26 designated bathing waters. More recently, NI Water has extended coverage of EDMs to storm overflows that spill into waterbodies which support shellfish growth / production and other sensitive inland waterbodies and rivers.As of September 2025, NI Water has approximately 655 EDMs installed and sufficiently verified to support effective use for operational maintenance and monitoring. Of these, 222 EDMs have been fully quality assured for both operational maintenance and release of spill count data to the public, via NI Water’s storm overflow webpage (NI Water's Storm Overflows).By the end of Price Control 21 (31 March 2028), NI Water aims to have approximately 40% of its 2,430 storm overflows fitted with EDMs. This ongoing programme of installation of EDMs will continue into Price Control 28 (2028-2034) subject to funding. 	For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on any steps he has taken to progress community asset transfers of unused or underused buildings in public ownership.
answer_text: I am wholly supportive of the important work that third sector groups do to empower communities and advance local regeneration. The Community Asset Transfer process is an area my department is proud to support in helping communities to take charge of local spaces and shape their own futures. My Department has recently set up a Strategic Asset Management Unit (SAMU), a new division within Land & Property Services (LPS). The team will advance work on the Government Land & Property Register (GLPR) that will help to identify public sector assets that are vacant or under used. As the database develops, we will have a much clearer picture of what is available, opening up more opportunities to make the best use of publicly owned property. My officials in SAMU have also recently completed a review of public sector asset disposal guidance making the Community Asset Transfer (CAT) process simpler and more straightforward for community and Third Sector Organisations, as well as their sponsoring bodies. It’s important to highlight that not every surplus public sector asset will be suitable for Community Asset Transfer, but where it is an option, I am confident that the process we have in place is open, fair and gives Third Sector Organisations a real chance to express interest and engage in the disposal process. A number of recent CATs have been successfully delivered through the D1 disposal process including sales at Fairhill Street, Cookstown to Cookstown Enterprise Centre and Falls Road, Belfast to the West Belfast Partnership Board, and a number of others are in the pipeline.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the factors considered by (a) his Department; and (b) relevant research funding bodies, when allocating investment for research in the less survivable cancers; and (iI) whether measures are in place to address historical underfunding.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care (HSC) Research & Development (R&D) Division within the Public Health Agency are responsible for the administration of the Department’s budgetary allocation for Research & Development.At present, HSC R&D do not specifically commission cancer research studies. Rather, they provide fund to cancer research infrastructure such as the Northern Ireland Cancer Trials Network (NICTN), the Belfast Experimental Medicine Centre and the Northern Ireland Biobank. The majority of funding for cancer research in Northern Ireland is provided by national Government, charitable or philanthropic organisations. My Department has no say in how these organisations allocate their funding. I acknowledge the importance of cancer clinical trials and research and recognise that they have historically been underfunded in Northern Ireland.  It is clear that significant and sustained investment in cancer clinical trials and research is urgently required. However, in the context of ongoing budgetary challenges being faced by the Department, this is not possible at this point in time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the level of government-funded cancer research investment allocated to each of the less survivable cancers including; (i) brain; (ii) liver; (iii) lung; (iv) oesophageal; (v) pancreatic; and (vi) stomach, since 2022.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care (HSC) Research & Development (R&D) Division within the Public Health Agency are responsible for the administration of the Department’s budgetary allocation for Research & Development.At present, HSC R&D do not specifically commission cancer research studies and therefore the level of cancer research investment allocated to specific types of cancer including less survivable cancers is not recorded.HSC R&D do however invest significantly in clinical research that supports and enables cancer research to take place, with funding provided to cancer research infrastructure including:Northern Ireland Cancer Trials Network (£5,037,690 investment since 2022) Belfast Experimental Medicine Centre (£1,095,097 investment since 2022)Northern Ireland Biobank (£1,613,371 investment since 2022) A portfolio of ongoing cancer trials, including trials for less survivable cancers, can be viewed at https://cancertrials.hscni.net/portfolio/portfolio-open-trials/.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any consideration he is giving to ensuring that post offices in Northern Ireland receive the same level of business rates support as those in other parts of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: ANSWERPost Offices already receive rate support, including 174 Post Offices that receive Small Business Rate Relief at an enhanced relief level of either 50% or 100% depending on the size of their property. Post Offices with a higher valuation are also eligible for a 20% reduction under the standard Small Business Rate Relief scheme. Further details are available at the link below:-https://www.nibusinessinfo.co.uk/content/small-business-rate-relief-small-post-offices As part of the ongoing Strategic Review of Small Business Rate Relief, my officials have already met with the National Federation of Sub-Postmasters (NFSP) over the summer. Officials also attended their event at the Assembly on 15 September, and individual Post Office business have actively responded to the survey for the Strategic Review. The Terms of Reference for that Review outlined that it would be looking at support provision in other jurisdictions. This will be considered through the lens of our own local needs and taxbase structure.Following the completion of the Review, in the coming weeks I will be considering the various options to brief Executive colleagues on next steps with a view to making any changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline her Department's plans to (i) protect the post office network in Northern Ireland for the future; and (ii) ensure equality of services across all areas.
answer_text: Postal services are a reserved matter, overseen by the Department for Business and Trade. The British government is currently consulting on the future of the Post Office through its Green Paper. The Consumer Council is currently preparing its response to the Green Paper: Future of Post Office consultation and will advocate for maintaining and augmenting services where possible to include ensuring access for customers across all areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any plans to (i) extend banking and postal services available through local post offices; and (ii) expand the range of government services offered through them.
answer_text: Postal services are a reserved matter. The British Government is currently consulting on the future of the Post Office via its Green Paper.  The paper does include proposals on continuance and augmentation of services, including provision of postal and other critical services including banking, government and utility bill-payment services, to those who cannot or will not get them elsewhere. The Consumer Council is currently preparing a response for submission by 6 October deadline.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the role of local post offices in supporting community access to essential services.
answer_text: My department has no policy position on the role of local post offices in supporting community access to essential services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any steps her Department is taking to encourage MLAs to support their local community post offices by submitting a response to the Green Paper, Future of Post Office.
answer_text: The British Government’s Department for Business and Trade has published the Green Paper on the Future of Post Offices, launching a significant consultation that highlights the vital role Post Offices play in delivering essential services to communities, such as postal, banking, government, and payment services.MLAs and other public representatives with an interest in these issues are likely to engage with the consultation proactively, without the need for encouragement from my Department.Meanwhile, the Consumer Council is in the process of preparing its response to the Green Paper and will advocate for continuance and augmentation of services provided to NI consumers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the most recent data on red flag dermatology waiting times will be published.
answer_text: Waiting times for patients starting treatment following GP referral for skin cancer, are published on a quarterly basis in the ‘Northern Ireland Cancer Waiting Times’ statistics bulletin. The most recent figures have a reporting date of 31 March 2025 and are available on the Department’s website at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/cancer-waiting-timesThe next issue of the Cancer Waiting Times statistics bulletin will have a reporting period of 1 April to 30 June 2025 and will be published on 2 October 2025.Official statistics for the Department are pre-announced one year in advance on the Department’s website at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/statistical-releases-calendar

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department ensures that patients and GPs are kept fully informed of the waiting time for urgent referrals.
answer_text: My department continues to work to improve communication with both GPs and their patients. The My Waiting Times NI webpage provides GPs and the public with up-to-date information on average waiting times, broken down by Trust, speciality and referral priority (red flag, urgent and routine). Work is underway to further enhance this web page with the development of waiting time information pertaining to the top decile of waits across specialties.In addition, patients now receive an acknowledgement text message once a referral has been triaged by a consultant, which includes waiting list priority. This helps reassure patients that their referral has been received and is being processed, while also providing a first point of communication on their care journey.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether additional services, including independent sector or cross-border commissioning, are being considered to reduce waiting times for red flag dermatology patients.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any urgent measures being taken to increase dermatology capacity to meet the two-week cancer referral standard.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the steps his Department has taken to ensure that Operation Begrain is proceeding as a robust inquiry; (ii) why no senior officials from the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust or his Department have to date been questioned in connection with the withholding of records, patient harm, or governance failings; and (iii) what actions his Department is taking to promote transparency and justice for affected families.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland’s (PSNI) Operation Begrain is a live police investigation into the clinical practices of the former consultant neurologist Michael Watt.  The PSNI has operational independence in its leadership of this inquiry. The Department of Health does not comment on live police investigations or operational policing matters.In common with all parts of the Health and Social Care system, my Department will cooperate fully with the PSNI as it progresses its investigations. On 11 April 2025 an Information Sharing Agreement was signed between the Department of Health and PSNI to this end. The PSNI’s website does provide updates and points of contact in regard to Operation Begrain – the following link may be helpful to you  -  www.psni.police.uk/latest-news/detectives-police-services-major-investigation-team-issue-update-operation-begrain

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether additional services, including independent sector or cross-border commissioning, are being considered to reduce waiting times for red-flag dermatology patients.
answer_text: A package of initiatives is being implemented to address red flag and long-wait dermatology patients. These include:Additional clinics in hospitals and the independent sectorExpansion of elective services in Primary CareIntroduction of the Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement SchemeThe additional waiting list clinics in hospitals and the independent sector are expected to deliver around 10,500 outpatient and day case procedures between April and December 2025, at an estimated cost of £5 million.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any urgent measures being taken to increase dermatology capacity to meet the two-week cancer referral standard.
answer_text: The Department of Health recognises the pressures upon services across the HSC and the impact this can have on patients.In response to ongoing Dermatology pressures, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) established a Regional Dermatology Forum to consider issues such as waiting times, addressing increases in red flag referrals, enhanced triage and time between referral and first appointment. This group is also considering the current funded capacity within dermatology, challenges in workforce and alternative pathways to address shortfalls in clinical manpower, and potential ways to increase the capacity available in each Trust.Reducing waiting times is a priority across both the Department and HSC Trusts, but solving this challenge will require long term sustained collaboration across our entire healthcare system. With the significant financial constraints our health service is currently facing, it is important to acknowledge that there are challenges in what we can do to increase capacity in the immediate future.Trusts are continuing to seek alternative ways to provide outpatient consultations to help reduce waiting times, through initiatives such as virtual or telephone appointments that provide advice and guidance. The Trusts are also seeking to implement a ‘Patient initiated follow up’ (PIFU) service and enhanced triage clinics, to further reform the outpatient service. Dermatology Services will also benefit from the package of measures currently being taken forward by Trusts to address long waiting patients through the expansion of elective services in primary care, investment in waiting list mega clinics, additional waiting list clinics and the new Waiting List Reduction Reimbursement Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the monitoring his Department carries out to ensure that delays in urgent cancer referrals do not result in avoidable harm or late diagnoses.
answer_text: I acknowledge that current performance against cancer waiting time targets, including red flag outpatient assessments and diagnostics, is unacceptable. These delays are deeply concerning and can have serious implications for patient outcomes. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.Despite these pressures, the Department of Health (DoH) actively monitors cancer performance through regular performance meetings with each Trust. These meetings are used to identify emerging issues and discuss mitigating actions to improve service delivery and patient safety within existing resources or via waiting list initiatives.In addition to cancer-specific performance monitoring, the Department also tracks elective and diagnostic waiting times more broadly, which directly impact the timely diagnosis of suspected cancer referrals. The Elective Care Framework (ECF) sets out a strategic roadmap for tackling waiting lists and building sustainable capacity across the system.To ensure transparency and accountability, the Department regularly publishes waiting time statistics, making this information available to the public, patients, and stakeholders. We also engage with clinicians, patient representatives, and external bodies to inform continuous improvement and ensure that patient safety remains at the forefront of our approach.The Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR) plays a vital role in collecting, analysing, and publishing comprehensive data on cancer incidence, survival, and outcomes across the region. NICR reports provide valuable insights into trends in cancer diagnosis, treatment, and survival, and help inform service planning and quality improvement efforts. The Department remains committed to improving cancer waiting time targets and ensuring that delays do not result in avoidable harm. However, sustained investment and system-wide reform are essential to achieving this goal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current (i) average; and (ii) maximum, waiting times for urgent red flag dermatology referrals, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Information and Analysis Directorate has provided the following statistical data for the average and maximum waiting times for urgent red flag referrals, broken down by Health & Social Care Trust.The most recent official statistics for the median and 95th percentile waiting times for patients starting treatment following an urgent GP Referral for Skin Cancer during Quarter Ending March 2025 by Health and Social Care Trust, are given in Table 1 (overleaf).Table 1: Median and 95th Percentile completed waits for patients starting treatment following an urgent GP Referral for Skin Cancer during Quarter Ending March 2025 by HSC Trust.HSC TrustMedian(days)95th Percentile(days)No. of PatientsBelfast85.5NA8Northern91265.433South Eastern82231.3594Southern62223.453Western67142.532Notes:‘Median’ and ‘95th percentile’ are used to assess the ‘average’ wait time in place of ‘mean’ or ‘maximum’ respectively. The median waiting time is the middle value when all patients are ordered by length of time waiting. This is preferred over the mean as an average of waiting times. The 95th percentile waiting time is the waiting time that 95% of patient waiting times are less than. One in twenty patients i.e. 5%, have been waiting longer than this time. The 95th percentile is used as an indication of the range of current waiting times without being distorted by extreme values or the prioritisation of urgent waits. ‘NA’ indicates that 95th percentile could not be calculated due to small numbers of patients.Data for the Belfast, Northern and South Eastern Trusts are sourced from encompass system. Southern and Western data are sourced from legacy systems.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any engagement his Department has had with (i) Sands; and (ii) other bereavement charities in developing specialist psychological support pathways for bereaved parents.
answer_text: Departmental officials along with the Public Health Agency (PHA) have actively engaged with bereavement charities, particularly SANDS UK, to enhance support services for those affected by pregnancy and baby loss. SANDS UK, with funding from the PHA, are currently developing the National Bereavement Care Pathway (NBCP) in Northern Ireland. This initiative aims to improve the quality and consistency of bereavement care for parents and families. The NBCP includes nine standards designed to ensure sensitive communication, opportunities for memory-making, and dignified support for grieving families. As part of the project healthcare professionals will be equipped with training, resources, and materials to deliver compassionate and informed care. Engagement with families who have a lived experience will be integral to the development of the pathways for NI, ensuring that bereavement support services are both clinically sound and emotionally responsive.My Department do however acknowledge the gaps that exist for Specialist Psychological support for Bereaved Parents and remain committed to improving services.  As you are aware, my Department is faced with a very challenging financial position at this time, but where possible I will prioritise the development of this service to ensure the appropriate advice and support is available to individuals and their families.Please also be assured that I remain committed to listening to those with lived experience and to ensuring that support services are accessible, equitable, and responsive to the needs of all who experience pregnancy loss.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure patients referred under the two-week red flag standard for suspected skin cancer are seen within that timeframe.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's work to phase out the use of glyphosate-based herbicides for weed control on the road network and verges, through addressing ongoing actions in the All-Ireland Pollinator Plan.
answer_text: My Department supports the All-Ireland Pollinator Plan through ongoing actions aimed at phasing out pesticide use on transport networks. Officials are taking this forward with our counterparts in DAERA aligning with the technical guidelines for maintaining roadsides for pollinators and participating in the Pollinator Monitoring Scheme. Alternative treatment options like the use of mechanical weed control, biological controls and organic mulches amongst others are reviewed on an ongoing basis as part of my Department’s drive for continuous improvement.My Department continues to implement and promote its policy on roadside verge management (grass cutting), placing a greater emphasis on environmental protection and enhancement.  The new policy means reducing grass cutting to increase the environmental value of road verges by promoting rewilding and improving the available biodiversity resource. The policy was reviewed and introduced in 2022 with support from Ulster Wildlife https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/verge-management-%28grass-cutting%29-dem-193-22.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will expand perinatal mental health services to include specialist psychological support for bereaved parents in all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My Department recognises the profound emotional impact that pregnancy loss can have on individuals and families.  Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts provides a range of support services, which may include:Bereavement Midwives: Specially trained midwives are available in many Trusts to provide compassionate, one-to-one support following miscarriage or baby loss.Counselling Services: Trusts offer access to psychological therapies, either directly or through referral, to support individuals experiencing grief, trauma, or mental health challenges following pregnancy loss.Referral Pathways: Health professionals can refer patients to external organisations such as the Miscarriage Association, SANDS UK, and Cruse Bereavement Support, which offer specialised resources and peer support.Early Pregnancy Units (EPUs): These units often serve as the first point of contact for women experiencing miscarriage and can provide immediate emotional support and follow-up care.Information and Signposting: Trusts provide written and digital resources to help individuals understand their options and access further support.In addition, a regional Specialist Perinatal Mental Health Service has been established in each Trust to provide assessment, care planning, and treatment for women experiencing severe perinatal mental illness, including postpartum psychosis.  Where appropriate, care is coordinated with maternity units and health visitors to ensure continuity and holistic support.My Department and the Public Health Agency (PHA) have actively engaged with bereavement charities, particularly SANDS UK, to enhance support services for those affected by pregnancy and baby loss.SANDS UK, with funding from the PHA, are currently developing the National Bereavement Care Pathway (NBCP) in Northern Ireland. This initiative aims to improve the quality and consistency of bereavement care for parents and families. The NBCP includes nine standards designed to ensure sensitive communication, opportunities for memory-making, and dignified support for grieving families. As part of the project healthcare professionals will be equipped with training, resources, and materials to deliver compassionate and informed care. Engagement with families who have a lived experience will be integral to the development of the pathways for NI, ensuring that bereavement support services are both clinically sound and emotionally responsive.My Department acknowledges the gaps that exist for Specialist Psychological support for Bereaved Parents and remain committed to improving services.  As you are aware, my Department is faced with a very challenging financial position at this time, but where possible I will prioritise the development of this service to ensure the appropriate advice and support is available to individuals and their families.Please also be assured that I remain committed to listening to those with lived experience and to ensuring that support services are accessible, equitable,

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) what his Department has learned from the findings of the needs assessment on ADHD which was commissioned in February 2025; and (ii) what they plan to do with these findings.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Northern Ireland. ADHD provision has evolved across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams. There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but there are inconsistencies and lengthy waiting lists across Trusts.The Department of Health commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. While the initial estimated completion date was end June 2025, due to the desire and demand for further stakeholder engagement, information gathering and analysis required, it is likely that the work will now be completed by November 2025. Given the importance of this work in informing future ADHD service provision, it is important that sufficient time is given to ensure a robust evidence base and proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the needs assessment on ADHD, commissioned in February 2025, which was estimated to be completed by June 2025.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Northern Ireland. ADHD provision has evolved across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams. There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but there are inconsistencies and lengthy waiting lists across Trusts.The Department of Health commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. While the initial estimated completion date was end June 2025, due to the desire and demand for further stakeholder engagement, information gathering and analysis required, it is likely that the work will now be completed by November 2025. Given the importance of this work in informing future ADHD service provision, it is important that sufficient time is given to ensure a robust evidence base and proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the average waiting time for bereaved parents seeking counselling or psychological support following pregnancy or baby loss.
To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the work of the Hate Crime Advocacy Service.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 9 - AQO 2364/22-27		 ANSWER The Hate Crime Advocacy Service (HCAS) is jointly funded by my Department and the PSNI to assist reporting of hate incidents to the police and provide support to victims throughout the investigative / criminal justice system process. It is delivered through an independent partnership coordinated by Victim Support NI and includes Hate Crime Advocates that are employed by the Migrant Centre NI, Disability Action and The Rainbow Project.   The current contract was extended to a fourth year in April and officials are working on a further extension into a fifth year.  HCAS received 1369 referrals in 2024/25, a 26% increase on the previous year and the highest number recorded by the service since it was established. This upwards trend has continued into 2025/26, in part driven by the abhorrent racist attacks in Ballymena and beyond in June.  HCAS will be hosting an event called “Don’t Hate, Debate” as part of their proactive awareness raising for National Hate Crime Awareness Week which runs from 18th – 25th October.  I would encourage members to attend any of the National Hate Crime Awareness Week events taking place.   While it is disappointing that our society requires this service, it is encouraging to see victims of hate crime seeking and receiving this vital support at a critical time.  My Department is committed to playing its part in delivering a safer community for all, including victims of hate crimes, and in partnership with others in Government and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the specialist psychological support pathways currently available in each Health and Social Care Trust for parents who experience pregnancy or baby loss.
answer_text: The Department of Health recognises the profound emotional impact that pregnancy or the loss baby can have on individuals and families. Each of the five Health and Social Care Trusts provides a range of support services, which may include:- Bereavement Midwives: Specially trained midwives are available in many Trusts to provide compassionate, one-to-one support following miscarriage or baby loss.- Counselling Services: Trusts offer access to psychological therapies, either directly or through referral, to support individuals experiencing grief, trauma, or mental health challenges following pregnancy loss.- Referral Pathways: Health professionals can refer patients to external organisations such as the Miscarriage Association, SANDS, and Cruse Bereavement Support, which offer specialised resources and peer support.- Early Pregnancy Units (EPUs): These units often serve as the first point of contact for women experiencing miscarriage and can provide immediate emotional support and follow-up care.- Information and Signposting: Trusts provide written and digital resources to help individuals understand their options and access further support.In addition, my Department is actively supporting the development of a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme for Northern Ireland. This initiative, currently under public consultation, aims to offer formal recognition of pregnancy loss before 24 weeks’ gestation, acknowledging the emotional significance of such losses.Furthermore, my Department is progressing with the establishment of a Maternity and Neonatal Partnership, which will oversee the implementation of a regional action plan to improve maternity care, including bereavement support. This work is informed by the recent independent review of midwifery services and reflects our commitment to ensuring that all women and families receive safe, respectful, and compassionate care.We remain committed to listening to those with lived experience and to ensuring that support services are accessible, equitable, and responsive to the needs of all who experience pregnancy loss.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for the publication of the findings from the needs assessment on ADHD which was commissioned in February 2025.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Northern Ireland. ADHD provision has evolved across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams. There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but there are inconsistencies and lengthy waiting lists across Trusts.The Department of Health commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. While the initial estimated completion date was end June 2025, due to the desire and demand for further stakeholder engagement, information gathering and analysis required, it is likely that the work will now be completed by November 2025. Given the importance of this work in informing future ADHD service provision, it is important that sufficient time is given to ensure a robust evidence base and proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the proposed new build for Malone Integrated College.
To ask the Minister of Health what research projects are currently being supported by his Department to develop or test early diagnostic tools for pancreatic cancer.
answer_text: The Department funds, through the HSC Research and Development Division of the Public Health agency and other routes, a range of infrastructure which facilitates and supports cancer research.  This includes the NI Cancer Trials Network (NICTN), the NI Biobank and the NI Cancer Registry.  The Department does not hold details of all of the research facilitated by funded infrastructure.  A number of studies related to the treatment of pancreatic cancer are currently being supported, but we are not aware of any which are specifically focussed on the development or testing of early diagnostic tools at present.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what investment his Department has made into research of early diagnostic tools for pancreatic cancer in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department funds research infrastructure which supports research into cancer and diagnostics.  This infrastructure facilitates cancer research, which could include research into early diagnostic tools for pancreatic cancer.   Relevant infrastructure funding awarded over the last five years is summarized below:2020-20212021-20222022-20232023-20242024-2025NI Cancer Trials Network3,411,0003,411,0003,411,0003,240,0003,200,000National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR)1,195,2421,246,4831,170,7561,248,9491,264,524NI Biobank473,293357,255336,815294,064532,492Total 5,079,5355,014,7384,918,5714,783,0134,997,016Researchers utilise this infrastructure to support funding applications for specific research projects or programmes, including those related to cancer.  However, the Department does not hold information on funding obtained for specific projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a public awareness campaign is being considered to improve understanding of the early signs of pancreatic cancer.
answer_text: While I would like to be in a position to commit to fund an advertising campaign on the symptoms of pancreatic cancer, the budgetary pressure imposed on my Department in recent years has required a pause on most health-related advertising spend.  Consequently, there are no current plans to fund an advertising campaign on this topic. The Department, the Public Health Agency and NI Direct will, however, continue to promote cancer awareness messaging through non-paid for channels, including social media platforms and media activity, directing the public to the website www.becancerawareni.info and appropriate pages on NI Direct for more information. My Department’s Press Office will work with the communications teams in the PHA, NI Direct, Health and Social Care Trusts and other partners to develop a series of social media posts across all platforms to raise awareness of the symptoms of pancreatic cancer.  This will include messaging in November, for example, to coincide with Pancreatic Cancer Awareness Month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered introducing awareness campaigns for GPs to assist identifying the early warning signs of pancreatic cancer.
answer_text: As you may know, Northern Ireland was the first part of the UK to agree to implement the Optimal Care Pathway for pancreatic cancer developed by Pancreatic Cancer UK (PCUK).  My officials and colleagues in Health and Social Care (HSC) are currently working alongside PCUK to take this forward.  My officials are also developing policy principles to support the expansion of the Rapid Diagnosis Centre model to a pancreatic cancer diagnostic pathway.  This work will involve a review of primary care referral guidelines for pancreatic cancer.  In taking this forward, where appropriate the Northern Ireland Cancer Network (NICAN) will engage with key stakeholders, including GPs; provide written updates and guidance to GP practices; and organise education sessions to inform GPs about new referral pathways and highlight symptoms that warrant a red flag referral for suspected cancer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what funding and support is available for families and carers of those diagnosed with pancreatic cancer, particularly in relation to palliative care and psychological support.
answer_text: Specialist support services are available across Health and Social Care Trusts for people diagnosed with pancreatic cancer and their families.  Clinical Nurse Specialists are the first point of contact and key worker for any person diagnosed with pancreatic cancer, providing ongoing support for individuals and their families, including during hospital admissions.    They work across upper gastrointestinal and hepatobiliary cancers and are employed in each Trust area.  Patients diagnosed with pancreatic cancer are offered a Holistic Needs Assessment to understand the clinical, physical, psychological, emotional, social, financial and spiritual concerns which arise for the patient and their family.  Individual needs are assessed and prioritised, which may lead to referrals for psychological support or to a specialist benefits advice service to expedite financial assistance.  Patients and their families may be referred to Cancer Focus if they would benefit from psychological support. The Northern Ireland Pancreatic Cancer charity (NIPANC) sponsor counselling services specifically for pancreatic cancer patients.  Children of patients can also avail of counselling services through Cancer Fund for Children and Cancer Focus.  Clinical Nurse Specialists make onward referrals for appropriate support and signpost patients to the Macmillan Information and Support service.  The Macmillan Information and Support service provides further information and connection with local voluntary and community services where patients and family members can benefit from complementary therapies and activities such as yoga, pilates and walking groups.  When a patient with terminal pancreatic cancer is identified as being in the final year of life, a Palliative Care Keyworker, typically a District Nurse, will support the patient and those important to them.  A Palliative Care Needs Assessment is undertaken to address the patient's physical, psychological, spiritual, and social needs, ensuring a holistic approach to person centred care.  The keyworker coordinates care and provides consistent support to the patient and their family, sensitively referring on to other services as appropriate.  Where the patient presents with complex symptoms or circumstances that cannot be met by the core healthcare team, a referral is made to the appropriate local specialist palliative care service for further support and intervention.  My Department is currently addressing funding needs of Clinical Nurse Specialists in order to provide stability to the service and ensure experienced staff are retained in their positions.  This is made possible through the Elective Care Implementation and Funding Plan 2025-2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions his Department is taking to improve the early detection of pancreatic cancer, given that most cases are diagnosed at a late stage.
answer_text: Pancreatic cancer is a devastating illness that progresses rapidly and has very poor outcomes when diagnosed at a late stage.  Achieving earlier diagnosis is key to improving outcomes for patients and accessing potentially lifesaving treatment. Northern Ireland was the first part of the UK to agree to implement the Optimal Care Pathway for pancreatic cancer developed by Pancreatic Cancer UK (PCUK), which sets a target of diagnosis within 21 days.  My officials and colleagues in Health and Social Care (HSC) are currently working alongside PCUK to take this forward. My officials are also developing policy principles to support quicker diagnosis of pancreatic cancer through the expansion of the Rapid Diagnosis Centre model.  The establishment of the RDC model has already made a hugely positive impact by delivering earlier cancer diagnoses and better patient outcomes through the Vague Symptom Pathway.  The expansion of the model into red flag diagnostic pathways provides a huge opportunity to diagnose patients quicker and shorten treatment pathway times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the overdue business case for the mother and baby unit at Belfast City Hospital.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a Director and five support staff for the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the date on which they will be responding to the UK Home Office's request for responses to the proposals in the recent White Paper on Immigration.
answer_text: The Executive Office has issued a response. The deputy First Minister and Junior Minister Reilly also attended an Inter-Ministerial Group meeting where the immigration white paper was discussed. They raised key local issues and highlighted local needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the planned investment in the Anti-Poverty Strategy, during (i) the current financial year; and (ii) in future years.
answer_text: Most of the services delivered by my Department help to tackle poverty, both directly and indirectly.  Each Department has committed to ensure that current and future interventions based on key strategic actions will be adequately and sustainably resourced. It is intended that the Action plan accompanying the Strategy will be updated on an ongoing basis. Where additional actions for contribution to the Anti-Poverty Strategy are identified, contributing departments will identify and bid for appropriate resources to take these forward.The final content of the Strategy will be subject to public consultation before final agreement of the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how he will engage with the Anti-Poverty Co-Design Group, during the consultation process on the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
answer_text: I met with representatives of the Anti-Poverty Co-Design Group prior to the launch of the public consultation. I would also encourage all members of the Co-Design Group and all interested stakeholders to engage directly with the consultation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how he will engage with the Anti-Poverty Strategy Expert Advisory Panel in advance of the public consultation being launched.
answer_text: There are no plans to re-engage with the membership of the Expert Advisory Panel, but I would encourage all those individuals and organisations who contributed to the development of the draft Anti-Poverty Strategy to engage directly with the public consultation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities if the public consultation process on the Anti-Poverty Strategy will be extended, if it includes the summer period.
answer_text: No.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when will Health and Social Care Trusts be able to provide updated details on waiting times for procedures such as cataract surgery and hip replacements.
answer_text: Following the roll out of encompass, a significant amount of data validation has been required in each Trust and, an extensive programme of work has been put in place to assist Trusts in validating their information and improving the reporting capability.  A report has been developed on the encompass system to provide waiting times for Inpatients and Daycases primarily for the quarterly publication of statistics at specialty level.  Patients waiting for individual procedures cannot be easily identified as the recording of Intended Primary Procedure Codes on encompass differs to how it was recorded on the historic Patient Administration System (PAS). Further work with Trusts, along with advice from the Regional Clinical Coding team, is required in order to utilise this regional report to accurately identify and group procedure codes appropriately. It is expected that this work will be complete by October this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why blood tests to screen for Down's syndrome, Edward's syndrome and Patau's syndrome are not being offered, despite National Institute for Health and Care Excellence Guidelines, by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), which advises the 4 UK Health Departments, has made a number of recommendations in relation to antenatal screening for fetal anomalies and inherited conditions.  These have been considered by the Northern Ireland Screening Committee (NISC), following which, the Department asked the Public Health Agency and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to undertake an assessment of the actions required and potential costs of offering antenatal screening for fetal anomalies and inherited conditions in Northern Ireland, as recommended by the UK NSC. Further assessment work is currently being undertaken, with this programme of work expected to inform a future Northern Ireland policy decision on antenatal screening for fetal anomalies and inherited conditions.In January 2022, the Department formally endorsed NICE Clinical Guideline NG201 on Antenatal care as applicable in Northern Ireland. The circular issued to Health and Social Care Trusts notes that where the NICE guidance makes reference to Public Health England/NHS screening programmes, Northern Ireland healthcare professionals should refer to Northern Ireland specific information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme, to outline the rationale of having Health and Social Care NI pay for people to avail of private treatment or procedures in the European Union but not in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme is based on the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive which allowed patients to get reimbursed for healthcare treatment outside Northern Ireland and GB.  As I outlined in my statement there is other work ongoing with Independent Sector Providers in Northern Ireland as part of a package of initiatives to tackle hospital care backlogs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether a blood test to screen for Down's syndrome, Edward's syndrome and Patau's syndrome should be offered as standard between weeks 10 and 14 of pregnancy in line with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence Guidelines across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, antenatal screening for Down’s Syndrome (Trisomy 21), Edwards' Syndrome (Trisomy 18) and Patau’s syndrome (Trisomy 13) is not currently offered as part of HSC care.The UK National Screening Committee recommends screening for Down’s Syndrome (Trisomy 21), Edwards' Syndrome (Trisomy 18) and Patau’s Syndrome (Trisomy 13), ideally in the first trimester between 10 and 14 weeks of pregnancy. These recommendations have been considered by the Northern Ireland Screening Committee (NISC), following which, the Department asked the Public Health Agency and the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to undertake an assessment of the actions required and potential costs of offering antenatal screening for fetal anomalies and inherited conditions in Northern Ireland, as recommended by the UK NSC.
