This work is ongoing and once completed, the assessment will be considered by the Northern Ireland Screening Committee before seeking my approval for any proposed changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 26305/22-27, to detail the amount of the Driver and Vehicle Agency's budget is derived from (i) fees; and (ii) Departmental subsidy, in each of the last three financial years.
answer_text: A breakdown of the Driver and Vehicle Agency’s annual budget in each of the last three years is shown in the table below.2022-23£’0002023-24£’0002024-25£’000Fee income 48,05853,63663,234Resource subsidy provided by the Department5,4437,721565Capital grant provided by the Department 21,004-1,155Other income (including bank interest)1,1133,0643,654Total Budget75,61864,42168,608

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what steps her Department is taking to further develop residents' parking schemes within the Belfast City Council area.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to develop the residents' parking scheme already commenced in the Iveagh area of Belfast and will give consideration to a scheme to address the emerging pressures with on-street parking in the vicinity of the Ulster University campus in Belfast. In addition, following the publication of my Department’s Residents’ Parking Review Report on 29 November 2024, there has been a significant level of interest for the establishment of residents’ parking zones across the north. Given the level of demand, and ongoing budgetary and resource constraints, I have asked my officials to expedite the development of an application process for demand-led schemes and an assessment process for all potential residents’ parking schemes to ensure that any requests can be appropriately considered. This process will take into account the findings and recommendations of the report and provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for residents parking schemes going forward. It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration her Department is giving to improving the linkage between Belfast City Centre and York Street train station, including the potential for free bus connections, similar to those available for Lanyon Place train station.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink currently offer free rail connection from York Street station to the City Centre. This is in the form of free Metro bus transfer travel on production of a valid NI Railways ticket. Between Yorkgate Station and Ulster University Belfast Campus and the City Centre, both on the U1 Service and passing Metro scheduled services (2a, 2b, 2c, 2d, 2e, 2f, 2g and 2p). This will also apply for return journeys to Yorkgate station from Belfast City Centre and Ulster University Campus for those in possession of a valid rail ticket.  Alternatively customers can travel to other parts of the City Centre via rail services from Grand Central Station or Lanyon Station. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any scoping exercises are currently being carried out to determine where the next residents' parking scheme may be applied within the Belfast City Council area.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to develop the residents' parking scheme already commenced in the Iveagh area of Belfast and will give consideration to a scheme to address the emerging pressures with on-street parking in the vicinity of the Ulster University campus in Belfast. In addition, following the publication of my Department’s Residents’ Parking Review Report on 29 November 2024, there has been a significant level of interest for the establishment of residents’ parking zones across the north. Given the level of demand, and ongoing budgetary and resource constraints, I have asked my officials to expedite the development of an application process for demand-led schemes and an assessment process for all potential residents’ parking schemes to ensure that any requests can be appropriately considered. This process will take into account the findings and recommendations of the report and provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests for residents parking schemes going forward. It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of a proposed increase in the registration fee for pharmaceutical chemists, to outline any recent engagement with the Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland regarding their registration fees being higher than other equivalent bodies in the UK.
answer_text: The President, and the Chief Executive of Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland, wrote to the Department in April regarding the outcome of the consultation on their proposed increase in the registration and retention fees for Pharmacists registered in Northern Ireland.  The Society have acknowledged that this increase has not been welcomed despite their proposed changes to fee collection. They have therefore indicated that they will commit to enhancing transparency, through their Annual Reporting process, on how monies are spent and to explore options to support the introduction of a flexible fee structure. I have made clear to the Society my expectation that, when bringing forward draft regulations on changes to their fees, they are able to demonstrate how the considerable financial pressures facing the pharmacy sector have been taken into account. The Department expects that PSNI will set out this rationale in a business case.The Society is responsible for the underlying policy in relation to fees for registrants, making the legislative amendments and implementing the final regulations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the reasons that applicants for Administrative Officer roles in the Civil Service were told they would receive letters of emails indicating whether they had been successful by the end of April 2025.
answer_text: I refer you to the response that issued for AQW 26577/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the final report of the working group formed to review scalp cooling will be published, given the urgent need for this service among cancer survivors.
answer_text: No working group has been formed to review scalp cooling. Cold caps/scalp cooling treatment are not routinely commissioned in Health and Social Care in Northern Ireland.  I am advised that they are not part of the National Institute of Health and Care Excellence (NICE) recommended care pathway for treatment of chemotherapy/Systemic anti-cancer therapy (SACT) induced hair loss.  The routine commissioning of this treatment can only be considered based on the long-term evidence of efficacy, available funding and the relative priority of this service in the context of other service pressures and service development proposals, and the overall HSC financial position.The commissioning position on Scalp Cooling can be reviewed on receipt of any emerging NICE recommendations for cancer patients. The NI Cancer Network (NICaN) SACT Clinical Reference Group, comprising regional and multi-professional members, reviewed the literature and evidence base in January 2018 and again in April 2022. Besides efficacy concerns, scalp cooling treatment alongside SACT would significantly impact SACT chair time. Given the current system pressures to meet demand and deliver essential SACT services across all units in Northern Ireland, the group consensus was not to prioritise the introduction of Scalp Cooling over other SACT treatment needs at this time. The group continues to review any new scalp cooling evidence as it emerges.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when letters will be issued to successful applicants in the recruitment process for Administrative Officers in the Civil Service, given interviews were held in November 2024.
answer_text: The merit list is anticipated by 16th May 2025, and candidates will be notified the following week.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail his assessment of the length of time that the recruitment process for Administrative Officers in the Civil Service is taking, considering it commenced in August 2024.
answer_text: Just under 10,000 people applied for the Administrative Officer competition.  Processing this volume of applications through the selection process including tests and interviews is a significant administrative undertaking for NICSHR and the delivery partner HR Connect.I also refer you to the response that issued for AQW 26577/22-27 which provides an update on the current position of this competition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the impact that the delay in recruiting Administrative Officers for the Northern Ireland Civil Service will have on overall morale among existing staff.
answer_text: Departments can put temporary arrangements in place to fill vacancies such as utilising agency staff, thereby minimising impact. There are several stages in the recruitment process that need to be completed before successful applicants are notified.  The large number of candidates for this AO competition resulted in some delay in issuing results to successful candidates.  All stages have now been completed, and the paperwork is being finalised.The merit list is anticipated by 16th May 2025, candidate results would then issue in the following week.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any discussions they have had with Executive colleagues in relation to the lack of solicitors qualifying in Northern Ireland to practice immigration law.
answer_text: Specialisms for solicitors are a matter for solicitors themselves.We have not held any discussions in relation to this with Executive colleagues.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when a Refugee Integration Strategy will be published, even if this is in draft form.
answer_text: We are pleased that a Refugee Integration Strategy and associated Delivery Framework was recently agreed by the Executive. We are preparing to formally publish and launch the Strategy in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail their role in working with the UK Home Office to ensure that human trafficking victims who end up in Northern Ireland are supported adequately and not subject to exploitation.
answer_text: Individuals arriving in Northern Ireland who are identified as potential victims of modern slavery or human trafficking (MSHT) are referred for support via the National Referral Mechanism (NRM) in the first instance by an authorised First Responder Organisation including: UK Visas and Immigration; Border Force; Immigration Enforcement; Police Service of Northern Ireland; Health and Social Care Trusts; and Belfast and Lisburn Women’s Aid.  The NRM is administrated by the Home Office and is the UK wide mechanism for identifying and protecting victims of MSHT and ensuring they receive the appropriate support.  Once referred, adult victims/potential victims will receive support from one of the Department of Justice’s (DoJ) contracted support providers.  Support for child victims is the responsibility of the Department of Health.Support for victims/potential victims is supplied on the basis of individual need and can include, among other things: accommodation; living expenses; access to health and social care; and counselling. The support contracts with Belfast Lisburn Women’s Aid (BLWA) and Migrant Help (MH) were renewed in 2023 following a tender process. MH provide support to male victims and their families and BLWA to female victims and their families.  The victims supported through the DoJ support contracts have suffered major traumas in their lives, the support provided by BLWA and MH helps them deal with the trauma, empowers them and their families to recover and thrive in NI and helps protect them from further exploitation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her role in implementing the Modern Slavery Strategy, including with specific reference to the provision of culturally competent trauma services for migrant trafficking victims in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: ANSWER The Department of Justice has lead responsibility for the implementation of the Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Strategy 2024-2027, which was published on 10 July 2024.  Alongside key delivery partners across statutory bodies and civil society, the Department is responsible for a number of commitments within the Strategy.  This includes a commitment to ensure appropriate support mechanisms are in place for all people referred as potential victims of modern slavery and human trafficking.  The Department coordinates the implementation of the Strategy and delivery of strategic objectives via the Organised Crime Task Force Modern Slavery and Human Trafficking Sub-group.Support for victims/ potential victims of slavery and trafficking in Northern Ireland is supplied on the basis of individual need via the Department’s contracted victim support providers, Migrant Help and Belfast and Lisburn Women’s Aid.  Both Migrant Help and Belfast and Lisburn Women’s Aid have developed significant expertise in working with vulnerable migrant victims of exploitation in a culturally competent manner.  Both organisations have training opportunities available to all staff in areas including: Equality, Diversity and Inclusion; Unconscious Bias; and, Religious and Ethical Inclusion.  Support providers link victims/ potential victims with various groups and support agencies including faith groups and LGBTQIA+ support organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the grounds on which the Driver and Vehicle Agency ceased to provide public-facing services from its head office.
answer_text: Counter services at the Driver and Vehicle Agency’s (DVA) Driver Licensing head office in County Hall Coleraine have been closed since March 2020 as a response to the Covid-19 pandemic. At this time, the closure of all DVA counter services was already under consideration following the introduction and promotion of digital and online services.  These new systems supported more transactions being carried out online, cashless electronic payments and a reduced reliance on paper-based processes. A decision to permanently close all DVA counter services, including those at County Hall Coleraine, was approved by Minister O’Dowd in July 2022.For customers that are not able to avail of the online services, the DVA continues to accept postal applications. Information on the services is available through the NI Direct Website at Driving licences | nidirect and through the  DVA Call Centre on Telephone number 0300 200 7861.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of people that physically work at the head office of the Driver and Vehicle Agency.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency’s Driver Licensing and Passenger Transport Licensing Divisions are based in County Hall, Coleraine.  There are currently 143 staff that work in these divisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale for determining that DL1 forms should only be available from Post Offices rather than downloadable.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency’s (DVA) Driving Licensing IT system uses Intelligent Character Recognition (ICR) and Optical Character Recognition (OCR) scanning software tools.  To optimise the scanning results, the scanners are configured to recognise the layout of the DL1 application form, and read the answers provided by the applicant within the form.  If the form were available as a downloadable form, individuals printing the document would be able to print on various paper sizes and orientation, meaning the ICR and OCR tools would not be able to read all the forms, increasing manual intervention and keying to the system and increasing processing times to determine an application.The DVA has introduced a number of online application services which are available in place of paper-based applications.  These include applications for a first provisional driving licence; to renew an existing driving licence; for a replacement driving licence; and to notify a change of address.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the annual budget of the Driver and Vehicle Agency in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) is funded primarily from fee income generated from delivering its range of services. Central funding is also provided to the DVA from my Department to cover expenditure which is not recovered through fees. The annual budget of the DVA in each of the last three years is shown in the table below.2022-23£’0002023-24£’0002024-25£’000Total Budget75,61864,42168,608

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of his Department's Screening Group to meet National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines.
answer_text: The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific committee, makes recommendations to the four nations in relation to screening matters. The UK NSC routinely reviews the evidence available for the population screening programmes. The Northern Ireland Screening Committee (NISC) considers the UK NSC recommendations before providing me with advice.  NISC does not consider screening guidelines from the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how his Department is currently monitoring and evaluating its part-funding of the Physician Associate course.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking an evaluation of the Physician Associate role across the HSC. It is hoped that it will provide firm evidence base and recommendations on the future of the Physician Associate role in Northern Ireland. This evaluation will also consider learning points emerging from the review of Physician Associate and Anaesthesia Associates roles currently underway in England.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of graduates of the Physician Associate course, part-funded by his Department, that have subsequently found employment as Physician Associates since January 2020.
answer_text: There are nearly 80 Physician Associates (PA) currently working across health and social care in Northern Ireland in a range of settings. While my Department is unable to give a definitive answer to this question, the vast majority of these will have graduated from the PA MSc programme at Ulster University.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the areas in which the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines on screening are currently not being met.
answer_text: The UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific committee, makes recommendations to the four nations in relation to screening matters. The UK NSC routinely reviews the evidence available for population and targeted screening programmes. The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) publishes evidence-based recommendations for the health and social care sector, developed by independent committees, including professionals and lay members, and consulted on by stakeholders. The Northern Ireland Screening Committee (NISC) considers the UK NSC recommendations but does not consider National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines. I am not aware of any NICE guidelines that are currently not being met in relation to the screening programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of his Department’s final budget allocation for 2025-2026.
answer_text: On Resource DEL, my assessment is whilst I welcome the Executive earmarking of the £332.5m for agriculture, agri-environment, fisheries and rural development previously received as part of Westminster earmarked funds, I am disappointed that an inflationary uplift in this funding has not been possible given the wider pressures facing the Executive.		In relation to the agreed £19.0m general allocation to my Department, this will cover some but not all of DAERA’s significant statutory obligation on bTB Compensation or contractual pressures on pay, employers national insurance and running costs.  		This is an extremely difficult outcome for DAERA and further work will be required as part of the imminent June Monitoring Round to determine the extent of these pressures across the Department.  		However, I welcome the £5.0m indicative Executive Earmarked Resource DEL allocation to DAERA for Lough Neagh at June Monitoring. 		This would align with the ‘Protecting Lough Neagh and the Environment’ priority in the PfG and would help my Department take forward further work in the Lough Neagh Action Plan in 2025-26.  		On Capital DEL, I welcome the Executive Earmarked allocation of £12.3m to DAERA for a Just Transition Fund for Agriculture.  		However, as the £119.5m total Capital DEL allocation is £7.1m less than my Department’s £126.6m inescapable bids, I have agreed to make an opening overcommitment of £15.0m to take forward all of DAERA’s inescapable and some high priority bids.  		This is on the basis that there will be some in-year slippage as well as opportunities to secure additional funding at Monitoring Rounds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timescale for work on the North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route at Bankmore Square and close to Ormeau Avenue.
answer_text: Subject to Outline Business Case approval, my officials will work to progress a detailed design of the project, however initial estimates indicate that the work on the route at Bankmore Square could be delivered in the 2027/28 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how much the Half Fare SmartPass concession scheme cost her Department, including in terms of its allocation to Translink, during the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: It cost £889k to reimburse Translink for revenue foregone for Half Fare journeys in 2024/25 on Metro, NI Rail and Ulsterbus services.  There were costs of £177k for Half Fare journeys on Glider.  Those in receipt of a Half Fare SmartPass can travel for free on Glider services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the estimated cost to Translink for introducing free travel for disabled people, for (i) off-peak; and (ii) at all times.
answer_text: To extend free travel to those currently in receipt of a Half Fare SmartPass may cost in excess of £889k which is the cost to my Department of reimbursing Half Fare journeys to Translink in 2024/25 (excluding £177k costs for journeys on Glider services as these journeys are free). Therefore, the costs would potentially double.However, it is difficult to estimate a cost as demand may be impacted in terms of the numbers applying and using their SmartPass, people potentially using it more because it is free and reimbursement rates to Translink.There would be no difference between off peak and at all times.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the projected annual cost to Translink for providing a night-time bus service.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of supporting the growth of the nighttime economy and the need to ensure that those working, visiting or socialising in the city centre at night have a safe, affordable and convenient way of getting home.My officials have been working with Translink to explore the feasibility of extending nighttime public transport services beyond the Christmas period.  I am also planning to meet with key stakeholders with an interest in the nighttime economy, to explore what options might exist in terms of a partnership approach to funding a service extension.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her Department's implementation of a Barnahus model.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION 6 - AQO 1920/22-27ANSWERA jointly chaired working group between the Departments of Justice and Health is considering a Barnahus model for Northern Ireland, as this is a shared objective of both mine and the Health Minister.A wider workshop including additional operational partners was held in October 2024 to inform possible options, using a benchmarking assessment of current experience and services against the Barnahus standards. Following the outcome of that workshop, officials are drafting options and associated high level costs to be considered by the working group and partners at a meeting planned for June 2025. In line with Barnahus recommendations and best practice in policy development, it is envisaged that children and young people with lived experience will be engaged to provide input on draft proposals. I understand that proposals for a Barnahus model may be available for consideration by myself and the Health Minister in Summer 2025. Implementation of an acceptable model will be dependent on funding.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 23342/22-27, to detail (i) how the work of the NI Bureau in Washington, DC is currently being evaluated; and (ii) against which existing targets and outcomes it is evaluated.
answer_text: The International Relations Strategy will set out agreed priorities and clear aims, alongside a fully integrated program of actions and performance indicators. This will support a consistent approach across government, amplify our messages globally, maximise what we can deliver in geographical regions and deliver value for money.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 23342/22-27, to detail (i) how the work of the NI Bureau in Beijing is currently being evaluated; and (ii) against which existing targets and outcomes it is evaluated.
answer_text: The International Relations Strategy will set out agreed priorities and clear aims, alongside a fully integrated program of actions and performance indicators. This will support a consistent approach across government, amplify our messages globally, maximise what we can deliver in geographical regions and deliver value for money.Our international teams work closely with UK Embassy partners to ensure policy and operational development gives consideration to the Northern Ireland trade circumstances, capabilities, and market opportunities globally.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given their concern on the spending of public money, even in the tens of thousands, to publish the Key Performance Indicators against which international relations work funded by the Executive Office is currently evaluated.
answer_text: The International Relations Strategy will set out agreed priorities and clear aims, alongside a fully integrated programme of actions and performance indicators. This will support a consistent approach across government, amplify our messages globally, maximise what we can deliver in geographical regions, and deliver value for money. Over the past 12 month, our officials have supported more than 75 official visits from over 20 different countries including the US, Germany, China, Japan, France and India – which represent the world’s largest or fastest growing economies. We will continue to use our international engagements at home and abroad to maximise our impact and reach with a clear focus on shared priorities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 23342/22-27, to detail (i) how the work of the NI Executive Office in Brussels is currently being evaluated; and (ii) against which existing targets and outcomes it is evaluated.
answer_text: The International Relations Strategy will set out agreed priorities and clear aims, alongside a fully integrated program of actions and performance indicators. This will support a consistent approach across government, amplify our messages globally, maximise what we can deliver in geographical regions and deliver value for money.The Brussels Office provides a quarterly report on its work to the Northern Ireland Assembly, which is an output associated with the objective of keeping key stakeholders informed about the work of the Office and EU developments.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the investigation into the use of grants provided to the Ballymac Friendship Centre is the only such investigation currently ongoing within the Executive Office.
answer_text: We would confirm that Ballymac Friendship Centre is the only investigation into the use of grants currently ongoing within The Executive Office.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update into the investigation into the use of grants provided to the Ballymac Friendship Centre.
answer_text: The Department of Finance Fraud Investigation Service (FIS) has investigated allegations concerning the Ballymac Friendship Centre. A full brief of the case including all the allegations and FIS enquiries and findings, is currently with the PSNI Economic Crime Unit for assessment.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how they intend to provide the public with assurances that good relations funding will assist with tackling paramilitarism, in light of the recent investigation.
answer_text: The Executive Office’s Good Relations programmes fund constituted organisations to deliver a range of Good Relations projects which contribute to achieving the aims set out in the T:BUC Strategy.Any group found to breach the conditions of their Letter of Offer can have funding withdrawn.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will conduct a review of the system to ensure that funding provided under good relations programmes is not subject to undue influence by paramilitary groups.
answer_text: We are currently considering the recommendations of the T:BUC Review, which includes an assessment of structures and governance around existing funding streams. Currently, Good Relations programmes fund constituted organisations, not individuals. Any group found to breach the conditions of their Letter of Offer can have funding withheld/withdrawn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the findings in research from his Department that minimum unit alcohol pricing would save lives, reduce the burden on the health and justice systems and improve the future sustainability of our overall budget and public services.
answer_text: I have set out that I believe that Minimum Unit Pricing (MUP) for Alcohol could be a key mechanism to reduce alcohol-related harm in Northern Ireland and to address the health inequalities that exist in this area. Alcohol related harm has been estimated to cost Northern Ireland up to £900m every year.However, this is an issue that requires an Executive-wide approach, and I have therefore requested the definitive positions of all Executive colleagues.Modelling by the University of Sheffield suggests that the benefits from an MUP policy in Northern Ireland would be seen in a reduction of alcohol-related health harms (deaths and hospital admissions), alcohol-related crimes and lower rates of absenteeism/presenteeism in workplaces. The level of impact would depend on the level that MUP is set at but the modelling set out the estimated impacts at a range of different levels: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/consultations/health/doh-mup-revised-modelling.PDF Comprehensive studies on the implementation of MUP in Scotland and Wales show that MUP has had an impact on the sale of cheap, high-strength alcohol products, and, in their particularly comprehensive analysis, Scotland has reported a reduction in alcohol-attributable deaths in comparison to what would have occurred in the absence of MUP. Importantly, there is no evidence of significant negative social impacts as a result of MUP.However, it is important to acknowledge that MUP is not the only answer to addressing alcohol related harms. While it is expected to bring significant benefits to our public services, the need for appropriate and sustained investment across our public services will continue to be of paramount importance. My Department remains focused on improving the access to quality substance misuse services across Northern Ireland the Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan 2024-28, co-developed following considerable stakeholder engagement, will inform future funding decisions around substance use related services, subject to required additional funding being made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timescale for the Knockbreda site to be used by a multi-disciplinary team in primary care.
answer_text: The Primary Care Multidisciplinary Team (MDT) Programme is a partnership between GP Federations and HSC Trusts which introduces new physiotherapy, social work and mental health roles into general practice, to work alongside the existing practice teams.  The MDT Programme remains key to stabilising and strengthening vital Primary Care services. Phase I sees the confirmation of £61m of Transformation funding which over the next four years 2025-29 will see the MDT Programme brought to completion in the 7 GP Federations which have already commenced and expansion into an additional 5 GP Federation areas with a combined population of 670,000.Phase II is anticipated to run for a further 3-4 years from 2029-30 and would see MDT complete in the remaining 5 GP Federation areas. Practice 136 (Mount Oriel Medical Practice) is located in Knockbreda Wellbeing and Treatment Centre, South Belfast Federation, which would be included in Phase II. Plans for completion for both Phase I and II are currently being worked up and include remaining federation areas from April 2029 onwards, and are in line with previously agreed roll-out order.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will next meet representatives of the British Medical Association.
answer_text: I have a meeting scheduled with the BMA NI Branch of Practice Chairs, to take place on Wednesday 30 April 2025. In addition to this my officials continue to meet with them on a regular basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his plans to introduce minimum unit alcohol pricing.
answer_text: I have set out that I believe that Minimum Unit Pricing (MUP) for Alcohol could be a key mechanism to reduce alcohol-related harm in Northern Ireland and to address the health inequalities that exist in this area. Alcohol related harm has been estimated to cost Northern Ireland up to £900m every year.However, this is an issue that requires an Executive-wide approach, and I have therefore requested the definitive positions of all Executive colleagues.Modelling by the University of Sheffield suggests that the benefits from an MUP policy in Northern Ireland would be seen in a reduction of alcohol-related health harms (deaths and hospital admissions), alcohol-related crimes and lower rates of absenteeism/presenteeism in workplaces. The level of impact would depend on the level that MUP is set at but the modelling set out the estimated impacts at a range of different levels: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/consultations/health/doh-mup-revised-modelling.PDF Comprehensive studies on the implementation of MUP in Scotland and Wales show that MUP has had an impact on the sale of cheap, high-strength alcohol products, and, in their particularly comprehensive analysis, Scotland has reported a reduction in alcohol-attributable deaths in comparison to what would have occurred in the absence of MUP. Importantly, there is no evidence of significant negative social impacts as a result of MUP.However, it is important to acknowledge that MUP is not the only answer to addressing alcohol related harms. While it is expected to bring significant benefits to our public services, the need for appropriate and sustained investment across our public services will continue to be of paramount importance. My Department remains focused on improving the access to quality substance misuse services across Northern Ireland the Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan 2024-28, co-developed following considerable stakeholder engagement, will inform future funding decisions around substance use related services, subject to required additional funding being made available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the appointment of a Clinical Lead for myalgic encephalomyelitis.
answer_text: At least two attempts have been made to secure a clinical lead, and Belfast Trust remains unable to fill the post. I am disappointed at the time it is taking to find a willing and qualified lead.The funding, which equates to a 0.5 Whole Time Equivalent consultant position, will continue to be protected for the position while my officials work with Trusts and local charities to find a solution.There are a number of challenges to overcome in this area, for example the lack of specialist training and specialist understanding about ME/CFS; variation in service models; and the range of related conditions adding to the complexity surrounding diagnosis and management.My officials continue to engage with various charitable organisations associated with ME/CFS, most recently in March, and will continue to examine potential ways forward.We have already begun to increase awareness among General Practitioners through webinar education sessions and I am also grateful to the charities concerned in their work to increase awareness and understanding of ME/CFS among medical students.I can also confirm the Strategic Planning and Performance Group, with the Public Health Agency, is actively exploring the potential of including new ME/CFS referrals within the Long COVID service model being developed for later in the year. Combining new referrals for Long COVID and suspected ME/CFS is possible due to the common symptoms experienced. Whilst not the long-term solution, we know we can offer something better than is currently available.It is also our ambition that a larger team of specialists, delivering across Northern Ireland, will improve the opportunities of attracting a clinical lead.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline his Department's policy on supporting schools located across split sites.
answer_text: The Department’s dual site policy is set out in the Common Funding Scheme (CFS). Eligibility for dual site funding is determined by the Education Authority (EA) based on the criteria set out in the CFS.  Schools must meet all three criteria as follows:two or more distinct campuses between which normal travel by means of the public highway is required;class teaching and learning must take place on both sites; anda minimum of 15% of the total pupil enrolment must be based on each of the sites. In operating these arrangements, the Funding Authority will advise the school on what it sees as the most efficient manner of operation across the split site, taking account of local circumstances such as the nature and phase of schooling, timetabling and transfer difficulties for pupils/teachers, together with any associated costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to improve bus connectivity to Belvoir estate.
To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the expected impact arising from the allocation of funding to her Department for transformation projects.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on any actions to address waste crime.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given his attendance at the launch in February 2025, to detail the role his Department currently has in the development of the Irish Football Association's proposed National Training Centre.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Football Fund includes a commitment to work with the Irish FA on the development of a National Training Centre.My Department received an Outline Business Case (OBC) from the Irish FA on 7 February detailing the key benefits that will be realised through thedevelopment of this project. My officials will continue to engage with the Irish FA whilst considering the proposals in the Outline Business Case (OBC).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any communication Invest NI has with companies that manufacture components for fighter jets in Northern Ireland, in order to ensure they are deployed in a way which respects highest humanitarian standards and the Geneva Convention.
answer_text: Enforcement of the Genova Convention is the responsibility of national states through their legal systems and the International Criminal Court.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assurances she has received from Invest NI about where the F-35 fighter jets for which components are manufactured in Northern Ireland are deployed.
answer_text: I have been assured by Invest NI that it does not fund projects involving the supply of arms to Israel.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a Commissioner for Victims and Survivors.
To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a list of the number of full-sized hockey pitches, broken down by (i) local council area; and (ii) county.

Please see breakdown by (i) local council area; and (ii) county below. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's assessment of the annual cost for Translink to provide concessionary fares during peak hours.
answer_text: This information is not available as my Department does not differentiate the costs associated with peak and off-peak travel, in relation to the concessionary fares scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action she is taking to speed up the turnaround in applications for concessionary fares for use of public transport, in particular to mitigate the difficulty caused to members of the public impacted by delays.
answer_text: I can confirm that officials from my department are continuing to work with Translink to streamline the application process for concessionary fares and to reduce the administrative burden and costs associated with the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the delays in processing concessionary fare mLink cards, whether she will consider introducing a policy to enable people who have already benefited from concessionary fares on public transport to continue benefitting from them, pending a new mLink card and provided they can show evidence that they have applied on time.
answer_text: The mLink fare is a commercial product offered by Translink and does not fall within the scope of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when she will review her Department's policy on concessionary fares, taking into account the desirability of (i) free travel for disabled people; and (ii) reduced or zero fares for young people, at least outside peak hours.
answer_text: The NI Concessionary Fares Consultation ran from 1st June 2023 to 24th August 2023 and included an option which would extend free travel for those currently receiving a half fare concession due to a qualifying disabilityAlthough public support for this option was strong (with over 80% of respondents in favour of the change), unfortunately, due to the severe financial pressures facing the Department due to over a decade of cuts and austerity by the British Government, changes which would increase the cost of the Scheme could not be implemented at that time.While the budgetary outlook has not changed, I will reconsider this option again.Although I currently have no plans to introduce free travel for young people, I will be considering fares as a whole and the incentivisation of public transport. Additionally, a number of discounted fares are already available.  Translink currently offer half fare to those age 5-16.  From age 16-23 Translink offer a yLink card which is a commercial product to encourage young people to use public transport. The scheme entitles them to 50% discount on bus and rail fares in Northern Ireland and on Enterprise cross border services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any options being considered to relieve vehicular traffic near Finaghy Health Centre, Belfast, not least given the negative impact on air quality from fumes at that location.
answer_text: The current traffic signal sequencing at Finaghy crossroads is complex, with up to 6 traffic movements and 4 pedestrian movements that need to be controlled efficiently but more importantly, safely, within the limited road space available.The Department has introduced advanced dynamic traffic signal control called MOVA (Microprocessor Optimised Vehicle Actuation).  MOVA gathers flow information, on each movement, from detector loops buried in the carriageway surface and amends signal timing according to the prevailing flow.The benefits of MOVA control vary from site-to-site but delay savings are typically between 10-20%. My Department is actively seeking to empower people to make more sustainable choices, particularly for shorter everyday journeys and in combination with public transport for longer journeys.  With safe, reliable, and accessible active travel infrastructure, individuals are more likely to embrace healthier alternatives to driving, in turn reducing congestion in our towns and cities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the last survey of traffic passing through Orpen Park was carried out.
answer_text: Officials have confirmed that the last survey of traffic passing through Orpen Park, Belfast was carried out in 2018.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure concerning actions to improve air quality near Finaghy Health Centre, given the significant rise in vehicular traffic at that location over recent years.
answer_text: I have not had discussions with the Minister for Infrastructure regarding actions to improve air quality near Finaghy Health Centre. The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) is the lead Department responsible for air quality policy and my officials continue to work closely with colleagues in relation to health outcomes.Under The Environment (Northern Ireland) Order 2002, Local District Councils assess local air quality against air quality objectives. Where this assessment indicates that an air quality objective is unlikely to be met, the council must declare an air quality management area and prepare an Action Plan. DAERA, through the Local Air Quality Management grant, financially supports all district councils to carry out air quality reviews, assessments and to prepare and implement related action plans.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has set a date for a smoke-free Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Ten-Year Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland has an overall aim to create a tobacco-free society. In September 2023 a final review of the current Strategy was published and acknowledged stakeholder support for a smoke-free target date for Northern Ireland. Preliminary work on a new Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland had commenced late 2023 but paused to prioritise work on the Tobacco and Vapes Bill (currently progressing through Parliament). The Tobacco and Vapes Bill provides a unique opportunity to protect our children, young people and future generations from the harms of tobacco, as well as new forms of nicotine addiction. The Department’s immediate focus is supporting the inclusion of provisions in respect of Northern Ireland in the Bill and their implementation. My Department will then identify the remaining challenges, including further consideration of a smoke-free target date, which will be the focus of a successor tobacco control strategy for NI.  Such ambitious targets will require careful consideration, adequate resourcing and realistic plans as to how to best achieve the target. The current strategy will continue to provide strategic direction in the meantime.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any studies carried out to ensure that the provision of a rapid transit service along the A24 Ormeau Road does not impede loading and delivery requirements of local businesses.
answer_text: My Department is committed to proactive and meaningful stakeholder engagement in the delivery of any major infrastructure project.In 2021, and prior to any route selection for the BRT2 project, my department undertook a significant public consultation exercise between 26th July and 4th October.  This engagement gathered a wide range of stakeholder feedback, from local councils, community groups and businesses in the area.The findings were reviewed, detailed in a report and published on my Department’s website in October 2022 (Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2 – Consultation Report | Department for Infrastructure).  This report helped to shape the selection of the preferred route for BRT2.Further work will be undertaken to ensure appropriate and meaningful engagement with key stakeholders throughout the BRT2 scheme development and delivery, which will include formal briefings and ad hoc meetings with individuals and interest groups.Public Information Events will be held as the project progresses through detailed design and into delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to regulate the type of electric scooter which may be purchased in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: It is currently illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here and I have no plans to change that position. Anyone using an e-scooter illegally may be subject to enforcement action by the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale for the proposed North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route within the Belfast Rapid Transit Phase 2 being through Royal Avenue.
answer_text: My Department is proud to work in partnership with colleagues in the Department for Communities and Belfast City Council in the delivery of the Streets Ahead and Bolder Vision Initiatives.My department will contribute to these initiatives by designing and constructing the northern civic spine of the city, along Donegal Place and Royal Avenue which will include the city centre bus priority measures required for BRT2.By directing the North/South Glider route through Royal Avenue, my department will increase accessibility for users to the city centre and support city centre living through the creation of safe and green streets, providing a viable and sustainable alternative to the private car.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to legislate for safer use of electric scooters on public highways.
answer_text: It is currently illegal to use e-scooters on public roads and public spaces here and I have no plans to change that position. Anyone using an e-scooter illegally may be subject to enforcement action by the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 9494/22-27, to detail when they sought, or will seek, Executive agreement to a final Refugee Integration Strategy.
answer_text: We are pleased to have approved the Refugee Integration Strategy and associated Delivery Framework, which has also been agreed by our Executive colleagues. The Strategy and Framework are now published on the TEO website at the link below. https://www.executiveoffice-ni.gov.uk/publications/refugee-integration-strategy-documents

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in the absence of an updated International Relations Strategy, how the work of the Northern Ireland Bureaux in Beijing and Washington D.C. is to be evaluated, including against which identified outcomes such value is to be measured.
answer_text: It is intended that the International Relations Strategy will intersect with broader Executive and departmental strategies and actions, supporting future long-term strategic policy planning by the Executive and maximising delivery of cross-departmental objectives. The role of all the overseas offices will be reviewed as we develop our new International Relations strategy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their Department's precise role in the delivery of the Northern Ireland Children's Hospital, ensuring value for money and completion by the end of this decade.
answer_text: The delivery of the hospital falls under the remit of the Department of Health.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how the work of the Maze Long Kesh Development Corporation is to be evaluated, including against which identified outcomes the value of its work is to be measured.
answer_text: The work of the Development Corporation will be evaluated against delivery of their current remit. This is reflected in the objectives and targets set out in their draft business plan, which is currently under consideration.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will publish the review work carried out on the current International Relations Strategy, drafted eleven years ago.
answer_text: It is intended that a future International Relations Strategy will maximise delivery of our shared priorities.Officials are in the process of finalising a draft International Relations Strategy which will form the basis for further engagement and a public consultation. Preparatory work that is used to inform strategy and policy development is not routinely published.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail over what period the consultation on a new International Relations Strategy will take place.
answer_text: The International Relations Strategy will intersect with broader Executive and departmental strategies and actions and will be important in setting out our long-term approach to international engagement. The Strategy requires Executive agreement; therefore, it will be subject to consultation and Executive colleagues’ consideration.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 13230/22-27, to detail the outcome of their considerations of a submission from officials in relation to the High Street Task Force’s Delivering a 21st Century High Street report and appropriate next steps.
answer_text: Following consideration of the issue we wrote to Ministerial colleagues at the Departments for Communities, Economy, Finance, and Infrastructure asking them to consider the recommendations of the High Street Task Force report and determine the way forward, bringing matters to the Executive for consideration, as necessary.Responses have been received from all four departments, outlining how they would take forward recommendations that fell within their responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, based on their consideration of how to engage with the rest of Europe, to detail their approach to a new Europe Strategy.
answer_text: Our approach to engagement with Europe will be reviewed as part of the work to develop our new International Relations strategy. Work on the strategy is ongoing, and we expect it to conclude soon.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on their response to the Draft Investment Strategy for 2050.
answer_text: Work on the draft Investment Strategy is almost complete, and we expect to receive a final version in the coming weeks. It will then be brought to the Executive for approval.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the plans they made during 2024 for the way forward in relation to filling the post of the Commissioner for Public Appointments.
answer_text: During 2024, a Skills Audit for the position of Commissioner for Public Appointments was conducted, and following consideration of the way forward, a panel was convened, with the Competition Initiation Meeting taking place in October, alongside the preparation of competition documentation. We have approved the submission from officials regarding the process to appoint a Commissioner for Public Appointments, and the competition will be launched shortly.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the outcome of the Executive Office's bid to the UK Treasury for a budget for the Northern Ireland Executive Office in Brussels to assist the Windsor Framework Democratic Scrutiny Committee with its work.
answer_text: The Office of the Northern Ireland Executive in Brussels was unsuccessful in its bids to the UK Treasury for funding to assist with its work in relation to the Windsor Framework for 2024-25. The Office has submitted a bid for 2025-26.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an assurance that the final Programme for Government will be published during the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: The Programme for Government was agreed by the Executive on 27th February and endorsed by the assembly on 3rd March 2025.The Programme for Government 2024-2027 and accompanying impact assessments together with a consultation report have been published and are available at www.northernireland.gov.uk/pfg.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the timeline for the development of the Gender Strategy, as set out in New Decade, New Approach.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 21239/22-27, when the procurement process for the administrator of the Crisis Fund will be completed.
answer_text: Officials are currently working through all necessary approvals processes to enable the procurement for the administrator of the Crisis Fund to be completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on when the Northern Ireland Fiscal Council will be placed on a statutory footing.
answer_text: The Bill to place the Fiscal Council on a statutory footing has been included in the Executive’s legislative programme for this year (2024/25). The drafting of the Bill is at an advanced stage, and I anticipate introducing it to the Assembly before the summer recess.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he is considering a Vacant Site Levy, similar to that in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: Vacant or derelict sites are not encompassed within either the domestic or non-domestic rating administered by my Department. A policy to place a levy on vacant sites could not rest within the Department of Finance as a sole entity. Rather it is cross-cutting and spans a number of ministerial portfolios including Communities, Economy and Agriculture.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on traffic calming measures at Orpen Park, Finaghy, given that consultation was due to be brought to a conclusion in November 2024.
answer_text: I acknowledge the concerns raised by residents of Orpen Park, Finaghy, in relation to vehicle speeds and road safety. I have asked my officials to consider the most appropriate action to bring this matter to a conclusion and to provide an update to residents before the summer of 2025.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions she or her predecessor has had with the Minister of Finance in relation to a Vacant Site Levy, similar to that in use in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: Neither I nor my predecessor, Minister O’Dowd have had any discussions with the Minister of Finance in relation to a Vacant Site Levy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the implementation of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of their recent meeting with the new Consul General of China and the forthcoming St Patrick's Day events in Washington DC, to detail when they will (i) set out their objectives in terms of International Relations; and (ii) publish a new International Relations Strategy containing these objectives.
answer_text: It is intended that a future International Relations Strategy will maximise delivery of our shared priorities.Officials are in the process of finalising a draft International Relations Strategy which will form the basis for further engagement and a public consultation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when the Programme for Government will be signed off.
answer_text: The Programme for Government has been agreed, endorsed by the Assembly and published.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether Health and Social Care Trusts are securing high quality services and value for money under current care home commissioning processes.
answer_text: When an individual is identified as requiring a care home placement, Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts will advise the individual and their family of available placements from the list of care homes registered by the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA). The registration and ongoing inspection of care homes is to ensure the provision of a secure environment where the quality of care provided to all residents is safe and effective. Whilst the aim should always be to facilitate choice for service users’, HSC Trusts have a parallel responsibility to secure the best value for money and protect the public funds for which they are accountable.In accordance with the 2010 Circular HSC Trusts are required to contract for care home placements at the most competitive rate available for accommodation which it considers suitable for meeting the service user’s need. The regional rate for residential care and nursing home placements, set annually by SPPG DoH provides the benchmark for residential care and nursing home placements. A review of the Regional Care Home Contract (2015) and the commissioning model for care homes is currently being taken forward through the Commissioning and Contracting Workstream of Social Care Collaborative Forum. This work is at an advanced stage and I expect the new contract to be available by the end of June 2025. The Workstream expects to progress to the review of the related commissioning and procurement model as the next step in this process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what his Department is doing to support children from displaced families, who have to change schools at short notice as a result of being allocated alternative housing arrangements in a different location.
answer_text: The Department recognises the additional challenges faced by children who have been moved a significant distance from their school as a result of leaving Home Office provided accommodation and entering Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) accommodation.DE works closely with the Education Authority’s (EA) Intercultural Education Service (IES), in providing funding and support to help assist children who are in this position, to access education.  Families who are displaced due to move-on are supported in accessing new school places for their children by the IES who assist families in making school admission applications, school uniforms grants, home-to-school transport solutions and free school meals application. In addition, the NIHE works with the EA, through a data sharing agreement, to minimise disruption for families where possible.The Department continues to engage regularly with The Executive Office (TEO) and other NICS departments to ensure the needs of children seeking sanctuary are met. DE officials attend a fortnightly meeting with NIHE, TEO, the EA and other Departments to focus on issues caused by the displacement of families at the point they achieve the right to remain in Northern Ireland.  The group focuses on operational issues in ensuring such families can access public services, including education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when she will approve the implementation of the national pay award for employees of the Housing Executive.
answer_text: This question was asked to the Minister of Finance but has been referred to my Department which has responsibility for this issue.I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 21575/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the date on which the approval of the national pay award for employees of the Housing Executive will take place; and (ii) when notification of this will be forwarded to the Department of Finance.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 21575/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any actions she has taken to establish and support the ministerial subgroup on implementation of the Renewable Heating Incentive Inquiry.
answer_text: The Executive Subcommittee on Reform Following the RHI Inquiry was set up after the publication of the Inquiry Report in 2020 in order to produce an action plan for implementation of recommendations of the Inquiry; and to agree a final report to be submitted to the Executive for publication.At its third and last meeting on 16 December 2020, it completed its preparation of the action plan.  Its final report was agreed by the Executive in October 2021.I have circulated a paper to the Executive for the oversight of the remaining actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will meet ministers in the new Irish Government in relation to Shared Island funding for communities and sport in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As I stated in previous responses, I continue to look forward to working with the widest range of funding partners where there is an opportunity to improve investment in communities, including in sporting facilities in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department is currently undertaking to raise awareness of the Acute Care at Home service.
answer_text: In March 2022, my Department published the Review of Urgent and Emergency Care. Following the recommendations of the review, a regional group was established under the auspices of the Chief Nursing Officer to examine the current challenges regarding Hospital at Home services, particularly how to ensure consistency of approach and outcomes of a high performing model. This group concluded its work in mid-2024 and identified a number of recommendations and priorities for further work.A Working Group has been established, led by HSC Trusts with support from the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and the Public Health Agency (PHA).The Working Group will take forward the review’s recommendations to reduce variation in referral criteria and maximise capacity of Hospital at Home Services across Northern Ireland.To alleviate pressure, Hospital at Home take referrals from Emergency Departments (ED), however, the priority is to prevent patients needing to attend EDs in the first place and so Hospital at Home referrals are prioritised for GPs and NIAS, then ED and then inpatient units. Focusing Hospital at Home efforts at the pre-hospital pathway is expected to make the most impact on patient experience and the best use of limited resources. Where patients self-present to EDs and they are suitable for Hospital at Home, then referrals can be made to the service. In regard to age, the priority patient group for Hospital at Home services in Northern Ireland is for older adults with frailty syndromes, significant learning disabilities or chronic neurological conditions, whose acute medical needs can be met in their home environment.  Referral criteria are based on need and benefit from the service, rather than age restrictions or health conditions.  However, currently the majority of patients who use the service are over the age of 75 years.  Concerning awareness, GPs, NIAS, community services and hospitals are aware of the Hospital at Home service and are able to refer patients. Currently the vast majority of referrals are made by primary care. It is important that service users receive the form of care that meets their needs and HSC Trusts will discuss a range of care options with individual patients. Discussions are underway with the Northern HSC Trust to establish a Hospital at Home service. Relevant stakeholders, including primary care, NIAS and Care Homes will be involved in this work to ensure that there is full awareness of the service and how it can benefit patients. Concerning budgets, while an analysis of costs and benefits formed part of the review work, no assessment has been made to date of the impact on budgets of aligning age and other criteria across all Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has made any assessment of the potential benefits to budgets and to the public of expanding the Acute Care at Home service so that it applies from the same age and in the same circumstances across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the needs of pedestrians, particularly the young, elderly and those with disabilities and tries to provide facilities where a need has been identified. As part of the resurfacing scheme on Purdysburn Road three existing pedestrian islands will be upgraded and a new pedestrian island will be provided between Bests Hill and Greenwood Glen. These measures will enhance the pedestrian infrastructure and promote active travel in the area. Officials have carried out a speed limit assessment on the road in line with my Department’s policy. This assessment has concluded that the existing speed limit on Purdysburn Road is appropriate. The existing road markings will however be reconfigured during the resurfacing scheme to encourage drivers to lower their speed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment that has been made of the potential to use capacity in the Acute Care at Home service to improve hospital throughput and alleviate pressure on Emergency Departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any guidelines his Department provides to Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to the age from which the Acute Care at Home service should be available.
