answer_text: AQW 8320/22-27 detailed my Department’s response to the Independent Neurology Inquiry (INI) Report and the significant programme of work which has progressed in respect of the INI Implementation Programme. The INI Action Plan published on 7 March 2024 sets out the detail of the Implementation and Assurance Framework and the engagement process which will sit alongside the INI Action Plan. Stakeholder engagement continues to be a key element of the INI Implementation Programme. The Independent Neurology Liaison Group was established to increase engagement and to focus on the INI Implementation Programme. This group membership is exclusively drawn from the neurology service users and families, who have been directly affected by the events which led to the recall of patients of Dr Michael Watt and provides the opportunity for those who wish to advise on the INI Implementation Programme assurance process.The Independent Neurology Liaison Group aligns with the work of the IIPMB Assurance Sub-group to further enhance feedback and recommendations from the lived experience of service user, carer, and families on the assurance process by reviewing and providing feedback on the individual INI Implementation and Assurance Documents.To date a total of eight Independent Neurology Liaison Group meetings have taken place and a further meeting has been scheduled for 8 May 2024.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister on what date, at the latest, the new Permanent Secretary at the Executive Office will take up post.
answer_text: The role of the Permanent Secretary at the Executive Office will be filled  on an interim basis by David Malcolm from 17 June 2024. David is a Senior Civil Service Grade 3 and will be temporary promoted to the   Permanent Secretary post. The temporary promotion arrangement is expected to last for six to twelve months.  During this time a review of the most recent Permanent Secretary recruitment competition will be undertaken and a new recruitment competition to fill the TEO Permanent Secretary vacancy on a substantive basis will be progressed.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister on what date, at the latest, an Interim Advocate for Victims and Survivors of the Mother and Baby Homes, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses will be in post.
answer_text: There is currently no date for an Interim Advocate to be appointed.As per the recent response to AQW 9733/22-27, the Truth Recovery Design Panel Report did not recommend a dedicated Advocate role. Instead, a series of advocacy interventions have included dedicated and specialist support services, the monthly Victim and Survivor Consultation Forum (with an Independent Chair) and the Independent Panel Victim and Survivor Representatives.While there is no immediate plan to appoint a single Advocate role, there is currently a series of workshops underway with Consultation Forum members which include views on advocacy. On completion of this there will be opportunity to consider how the current advocacy model may be enhanced.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister on what date, at the latest, the new Commissioner for Public Appointments will be appointed.
answer_text: In May 2021 a public appointment competition was launched for the role of Commissioner for Public Appointments for Northern Ireland (CPANI), although an appointment decision was not reached prior to the end of the last mandate.In the absence of an Executive, CPANI was added to the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2022 list of specified offices to allow the appointment function to be exercised by the Secretary of State. However, this did not happen prior to the recent return of the Executive.The appointing authority for CPANI has therefore now reverted to the First Minister and deputy First Minister acting jointly. We will shortly be considering plans for the way forward regarding filling the post.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister on what date, at the latest, the new Commissioner for Victims and Survivors will be appointed.
answer_text: We are currently reviewing the appointment plan for the recruitment competition to appoint a new Commissioner which will be launched as soon as possible. The appointment process is regulated by the Commissioner for Public Appointments, and we estimate that the process will take approximately six months to complete.We are hopeful that a new Commissioner may therefore be appointed in the autumn.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will consider a public inquiry following the Truth Recovery Design Panel's report into the Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.
To ask the Minister of Health what action he has taken on the pay and conditions of practice-based pharmacists since returning to office in February 2024.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the various issues regarding remuneration and conditions for practice-based pharmacists employed by GP Federations which you have raised. I can assure you that such issues have been subject to ongoing discussions between the Department of Health (DoH) and key stakeholders.The GP Pharmacist Oversight Board was established by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the DoH in December 2023 before I came to office. The group brings together representatives from all key stakeholder groups, including SPPG, DoH, GP Federations and GPC (General Practitioners Committee) to facilitate collaborative working to optimise the working arrangements of the GPP workforce, in line with strategic priorities of the DoH Medicines Optimisation Quality Framework. The group will oversee the delivery of the service and facilitate discussion and resolution to issues relating to the GPP workforce employed in General Practices. The group also aims to develop the General Practice Pharmacist workforce, in line with DoH strategic direction and the Pharmacy Workforce Review, which is being progressed through Service Delivery and Service Development subgroups.My Department will continue to work closely with stakeholders over the following months to address the issues to which this question refers

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Band 6 rate of pay for practice-based pharmacists in comparison to the higher pay rates of other multi-disciplinary team members.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the various issues regarding rates of pay for practice-based pharmacists employed by GP Federations which you have raised. I can assure you that such issues are already subject to ongoing discussions between the Department of Health (DoH) and key stakeholders.The GP Pharmacist Oversight Board was established by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the DoH in December 2023. The group brings together representatives from all key stakeholder groups, including SPPG, DoH, GP Federations and GPC (General Practitioners Committee) to facilitate collaborative working to optimise the working arrangements of the GPP workforce, in line with strategic priorities of the DoH Medicines Optimisation Quality Framework. The group will oversee the delivery of the service and facilitate discussion and resolution to issues, including pay bands and workforce development, for the GPP workforce employed in General Practices. The group aims to progress these issues in line with DoH strategic direction and the Pharmacy Workforce Review, which is being progressed through Service Delivery and Service Development subgroups.I will continue to liaise with my adviser and work closely with stakeholders over the following months to address the issues to which this question refers

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a deadline has been set for ensuring that pay of practice-based pharmacists is more aligned to that of other multi-disciplinary team members.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the various issues regarding remuneration for GP practice pharmacists employed by GP Federations which you have raised. I can assure you that such issues are already subject to ongoing discussions between the Department of Health (DoH) and key stakeholders.The GP Pharmacist Oversight Board was established by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the DoH in December 2023. The group brings together representatives from all key stakeholder groups, including SPPG, DoH, GP Federations and GPC (General Practitioners Committee) to facilitate collaborative working to optimise the working arrangements of the GPP workforce, in line with strategic priorities of the DoH Medicines Optimisation Quality Framework. The group will oversee the delivery of the service and facilitate discussion and resolution to issues relating to the GPP workforce employed in General Practices. The group also aims to develop the General Practice Pharmacist workforce, in line with DoH strategic direction and the Pharmacy Workforce Review, which is being progressed through Service Delivery and Service Development subgroups.DoH will continue to work closely with stakeholders over the following months to address the issues to which this question refers

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what guidance is provided to doctors and clinicians in Northern Ireland in relation to accepting ADHD diagnoses provided by public health authorities elsewhere in the UK, Channel Islands and Isle of Man.
answer_text: Service users accepted onto an ADHD waiting list with an ADHD diagnosis will be placed on a waiting list in chronological order of their referral in line with Integrated Elective Access Process (IEAP) guidelines. Trusts will assess the assessment process against compliance with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines. If the diagnostic assessment complies with NICE the diagnosis will be accepted and ongoing support will be provided. If the assessment does not meet with NICE guidelines an HSC clinician will complete the diagnosis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a deadline has been set for the roll-out of multi-disciplinary teams across the all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The MDT model remains the key to stabilising and strengthening Primary Care services to ensure they can continue to provide high quality care to our people, now and into the future.  The Department remains committed to the further implementation of the MDT model across Northern Ireland and is developing a plan for rollout over the next number of years subject to the availability of the necessary recurrent funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any understaffing issues currently in the (i) South Belfast DFI Roads Office; and (ii) Lisburn DFI Roads Office.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a very difficult financial position due to years of underfunding as a result of years of Tory austerity and cuts, this has been compounded by ongoing challenges in recruitment. Financial pressures have also restricted my Department’s ability to recruit and fill vacant posts. The current vacancy rates in the South Belfast and Lisburn section offices are 35% and 28% respectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what proposals he will bring forward to address the impact of potholes, specifically on cyclists.
answer_text: My Department’s Resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, has been under pressure for a number of years. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired.The level of repairs that can be carried out in future financial years will depend on the 2024-25 final budget determination for my Department. Until such times as sufficient funding is made available, it will not be possible to change the current level of service. The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what investment his Department (i) made in cycling infrastructure in South Belfast during the 2023-24 financial year; and (ii) intends to make in cycling infrastructure in South Belfast during the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Monitoring of the Active Travel spend within the Department is not broken down by parliamentary constituency. The South Belfast constituency falls within the Eastern Division. The cycling infrastructure priority in Belfast is the delivery of the Belfast Cycling Network which amounted to a spend of approximately £0.5m in 2023/24, and grants were awarded for approximately £0.5m to the Belfast City Council for active travel and greenway schemes. Budgets for the 2024-25 financial year have not yet been set. Once the outcome of the budget settlement is known I will then consider funding for active travel in the context of my Department’s overall budget, taking into consideration factors such as other competing priorities, legislative requirements and delivery capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there will be any delay in the delivery of the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan 2022-31.
answer_text: My officials are currently progressing the majority of short-term schemes within the BCN and are confident that these can be delivered in the time period up to the end of 2025, subject on the successful completion of statutory processes. My officials aim to commence preliminary design on a number of medium-term schemes in due course.Delivery of the plan, along with many other Departmental priorities, remains subject to future budget allocations and available resources. I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions he is taking to ensure that issues with understaffing in the (i) South Belfast DFI Roads Office; and (ii) Lisburn DFI Roads Office do not impact on road repairs.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a very difficult financial position due to years of underfunding as a result of years of Tory austerity and cuts  this has been compounded by ongoing challenges in recruitment.While my officials and maintenance teams have worked very hard to maintain the road network they have, nonetheless, been operating with a higher than acceptable vacancy rate and are under significant pressure. This is the situation across all of the roads section offices including the South Belfast and Lisburn areas. Whilst staff have been doing everything they can to manage this under-resourcing, it is inevitable that it will impact on my Department’s ability to carry out essential work such as road maintenance.My Department is taking steps to deliver a programme of recruitment and training opportunities, particularly focused on those roles within the technical and operational areas of the organisation to deliver capacity and capability.  This will include direct entrant competitions for recently qualified graduates, and apprenticeship opportunities to encourage pathways to a career in civil and infrastructure engineering. However, delivering the sustainable capacity and capability needed will take time, and it should be recognised that until additional capacity is built up, my officials will have to continue to prioritise that work which is most critical to maintaining road safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what changes he is proposing to teacher training on the prevention and addressing of bullying in schools.
answer_text: Following the commencement (in 2021) of the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, the Education Authority (EA) established an Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT). This team, which has been in existence since September 2022, provides a support service for schools to enable them to meet their statutory requirements, as outlined in the Act. This service offers targeted advice, guidance, and professional learning courses to schools on effective prevention of, and responses to, bullying type concerns. The ABSIT has met with a number of initial teacher education (ITE) providers in Northern Ireland, provided resources to support ITE and signposted staff and student teachers to appropriate resources on the EA website. ABSIT has also delivered a professional learning course to student teachers in Stranmillis University College and offered this to St Mary’s University College and Queen’s University Belfast. While ABSIT has not yet met with Ulster University regarding its ITE provision, the team are open to engaging with the University. Additionally, ABSIT is launching a specific online professional learning course for student teachers and will track its completion, with a view to identifying further TPL needs on this issue. The online programme will be available to all student teachers through the EA’s school development portal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what research his Department has commissioned on the impact of current intervention and prevention strategies for bullying in schools since 2016.
answer_text: The Department’s last research study on bullying type behaviour in our schools was completed in 2011. I recognise that it would be beneficial to commission a new study in order to:gather up-to-date data on the nature and prevalence of bullying type behaviour; baseline the impact of the Addressing Bullying in Schools Act (NI) 2016; gather evidence on the profile of pupils involved in bullying type behaviour; quantify the impact, identify challenges and emerging issues in relation to bullying type behaviour; andhighlight best practice in addressing bullying type behaviour in schools.I will consider the scope to commission this research within the context of the Department’s final budget allocation for this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps he is taking to identify an anti-bullying lead in all schools to coordinate anti-bullying policy.
answer_text: The Department funds the Addressing Bullying in Schools Implementation Team (ABSIT) within the Education Authority (EA). ABSIT support schools to implement the Addressing Bullying in Schools (Northern Ireland) Act 2016 (the Act).ABSIT training provides for leadership roles within a school to coordinate the school’s addressing bullying policy and the team deliver a Level 3 training programme.  Pastoral leaders from 147 schools including 120 post primary schools, 20 primary and seven special schools have completed this training to date. 110 school governors attended face to face training delivered by ABSIT from September 2023 - March 2024.  All Board of Governors have access to online training which outlines their statutory role in overseeing the implementation of the Act.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide a timeline for the introduction to the Assembly of the requisite legislation to establish (i) an inquiry; and (ii) redress schemes arising from the Truth Recovery Design Panel's report into the Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.
answer_text: We are progressing legislation to establish both a Statutory Public Inquiry and financial redress schemes for those impacted by their experience in Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries, and Workhouses. There are a number of complex and sensitive issues which need to be considered ahead of the next key stage, which is to bring a proposal to Executive colleagues with the intention to launch a public consultation ahead of the summer recess.Following an analysis of these responses, the draft Bill can be finalised and brought to the Executive to agree and be introduced in the Assembly, subject to the Speaker’s agreement.We appreciate this is an important and time sensitive piece of legislation which will require careful scrutiny by the Assembly, and we are keen to work closely with the Committee for the Executive Office and the Assembly to prioritise this Bill.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department has any role in the Independent Review of Education, including with reference to the allocation of funding to the Strule Campus.
answer_text: Executive Office officials provided input into the Independent Review of Education in March 2022 as part of the stakeholder engagement process.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail their Department's role in ensuring infrastructure projects taken forward by Executive Departments provide clear value for money.
answer_text: The Strategic Investment Board (SIB) has a statutory role to advise bodies carrying out major investment projects on the delivery of such projects.  SIB does this by providing research, consultancy, advisory and other services and general assistance.  SIB also has a statutory role to advise the Executive in relation to the formulation and implementation of its programme of major investment projects.  This is discharged primarily through the Investment Strategy, and examination of systemic issues, such as the root causes of delays.Ultimately responsibility for ensuring value for money on specific projects rests with individual departments.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the deadline for an operational Programme for Government.
answer_text: The Executive is preparing a draft Programme for Government document. We hope to move at pace to agree a Programme and bring it before the Assembly for scrutiny and approval. We will keep members updated as progress continues.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they plan to respond to the Strategic Investment Board's draft Investment Strategy for 2050.
answer_text: Infrastructure 2050 – the final draft Investment Strategy – was presented at the NICS Board at the end of January and shared with each of the departments for comment. Departments have provided initial feedback on the draft to SIB. The feedback will be formally submitted to us to consider before it is brought before the Executive. The timeline for publication of the Investment Strategy will be dependent on consensus across departments and Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on plans to demolish two tower blocks in Belvoir, South Belfast.
answer_text: The Housing Executive’s business case recommending demolition of the two tower blocks in Belvoir was approved by my Department in June 2022.There has been good progress in rehousing the tenants and reacquiring the sold flats, and the Housing Executive is still on track to meet its anticipated clearance timescale of June 2025.  Documents have been prepared to procure professional services to develop a demolition scheme and will soon go out to tender.  The Housing Executive remains on track to have the two blocks cleared and demolition started on site in 2025/ 26.It is the intention to redevelop the site for social housing.  A development brief is being prepared for the site.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will publish a formal response to the Truth Recovery Programme, including reference to the legislation recommended by the Truth Recovery Design Panel.
answer_text: The Executive’s formal response to the findings of the Truth Recovery Design Panel’s, “Truth, Acknowledgement and Accountability” Report into Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses from October 2021 was conveyed by way of a Statement in the Assembly on 15 November 2021. We have reaffirmed this commitment in the Chamber in response to Assembly Questions on several occasions since the new Assembly and Executive was restored in February 2024. In addition to confirming the Executive’s intention to implement the five key recommendations in the Report, we have specified our intention to introduce the requisite legislation to establish a statutory Public Inquiry and redress schemes at the earliest opportunity. Additionally, the Preservation of Documents (Historical Institutions) Bill was enacted in May 2022 and has been invaluable in assisting the Public Records Office of Northern Ireland to request historical records from Institutions and the important process of preserving, digitising, and cataloguing the 4,500 records identified from initial survey of private records.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they have taken a position on the desirability and viability of a Public Inquiry into Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.
answer_text: Since the Assembly and Executive’s restoration in February we have reaffirmed our commitment in the House of the previous Executive’s fulsome support and intention to implement the five key recommendations in the Truth Recovery Design Panel’s “Truth, Acknowledgement and Accountability” Report into Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses. This includes establishing an integrated truth investigation, comprising a non-statutory Independent Panel and a statutory Public Inquiry. The first stage of the investigation has begun with the appointment in April 2023 of a 10-person Independent Panel, comprising 7 specialist roles and 3 victim-survivors. It is our intention to launch a public consultation on issues relating to the statutory Public Inquiry and redress schemes as quickly as possible, following which we will finalise the draft legislation for Executive agreement prior to introduction in the Assembly at the earliest opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to improve the public transport routes and frequency to Belvoir Estate.
answer_text: The provision and scheduling of Metro bus services is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration has been given to incorporating Belvoir Estate, or aligned bus links to Belvoir Estate, in the North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route.
answer_text: On 21 October 2022 I announced the North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route. Any decision on a feeder service linking Belvoir Estate would be an operational matter for Translink.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what additional budget will be allocated to enable the Northern Ireland Executive Office in Brussels to assist the Windsor Framework Democratic Scrutiny Committee with its work.
answer_text: Our Office in Brussels has submitted a bid to the Treasury exercise for new costs or requirements for the implementation of the NI Protocol and Windsor Framework.  We are awaiting the outcome of that exercise.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how many Executive Office staff (i) were working on issues arising from the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 during the 2022-23 financial year; (ii) were working on issues arising from the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 during the 2023-24 financial year; and (iii) will be working on issues arising from the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 during the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: The Executive Office is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (the Act) for which it is responsible.During the 2022/2023 and 2023/2024 up to 14 staff were allocated to work on the Act as part of their core duties.Currently (7thth May 2024) 9 staff are allocated to work on the Act as part of their core duties. Future staffing requirements in 24/25 will be dependent on our decisions going forward.Other staff have and will contribute to the implementation of the Act by supporting these colleagues where appropriate, in line with their overall duties.TEO officials have and continue to take forward the necessary preparatory work to implement the Act which will be in line with our decisions.Members will be kept informed on progress.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when an Interim Advocate for victims and survivors of Mother and Baby Institutions, the Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses will be appointed.
answer_text: The Truth Recovery Design Panel Report did not recommend a dedicated Advocate role. Instead, a series of advocacy interventions have included dedicated and specialist support services, the monthly Victim and Survivor Consultation Forum (with an Independent Chair) and the Independent Panel Victim and Survivor Representatives.There is currently a series of workshops underway with Consultation Forum members which include views on advocacy.  There is no immediate plan to appoint a single Advocate role. On completion of this there will be opportunity to consider how the current advocacy model may be enhanced.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they plan to carry out an updated What Matters Most survey.
answer_text: We have no plans to carry out another ‘What Matters Most’ survey at this time.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what advice they have sought on Good Relations policy from the Equality Commission since coming into office.
answer_text: Officials from the Executive Office have engaged the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland as part of the review of the Together: Building a United Community Strategy (T:BUC). Further engagement is planned as this area of work progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether his Department has made a decision on the demolition of the maisonettes in Kilwarlin Walk and Moyle Walk in Belvoir; (ii) whether his Department will be carrying out the relevant repairs to the properties; and (iii) to provide a timeframe for any work to be completed.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:The Housing Executive has prepared a Multi-Element Improvement Scheme and supporting business case for the maisonette blocks in Kilwarlin Walk and Moyle Walk; it will now go through the necessary approval process before submission to the Department for Communities for consideration.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will bring forward a refugee integration strategy.
answer_text: Following consultation, which showed strong support for the proposed Refugee Integration Strategy vision and outcomes, officials are currently entering the final stage of Strategy development. This includes collaboration with Departments and other stakeholders to develop associated ‘Thematic Delivery Plans,’ so it is clear how we will seek to achieve the outcomes and vision.We intend to seek Executive agreement to a final Strategy and the Thematic Delivery Plans in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what work he is undertaking to ensure Child Rights Impact Assessments are applied to policies and legislation relating to climate action.
answer_text: As part of work to develop a draft Climate Action Plan (CAP), my Department has commissioned and is developing a range of impact assessments on the effects of policies and proposals within the CAP. These include a Child Rights Impact Assessment (CRIA) on the draft CAP with the intention of releasing for consultation alongside the CAP consultation. A CRIA has also been completed to assess the impact of the regulations that will set Northern Ireland’s first three carbon budgets covering the period 2023-2037.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what work they are undertaking to address age discrimination, particularly as it concerns the provision of goods and services.
answer_text: Consideration of extending age discrimination legislation to the provision of goods, facilities and services was the subject of consultations in a previous mandate.  A decision was not made in respect of the scope of that legislation. Officials will commence a review of the work previously undertaken to inform advice to Ministers around possible next steps and the potential scope of any legislation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work they have undertaken since taking office to protect children from exploitation by paramilitary and criminal gangs.
answer_text: Through the Executive Office we have a number of initiatives which are working to protect children and young people from exploitation by paramilitary and criminal gangs. The Planned Interventions Programme (PIP) funds community activity to provide positive alternatives for young people at risk of becoming involved in anti-social behaviour, sectarianism, and recruitment from paramilitary gangs, and to address heightened inter and intra community tension around interfaces, or anywhere there is a potential for community tensions to arise. The Programme is delivered annually under wider commitments of the Executive’s ‘Together: Building a United Community (T: BUC)’ Strategy.During 2023/24 70 projects were awarded a total of £400k under the PIP, five of the applications awarded were from the South Belfast constituency and received a total of £29,614.18. The call for applications for 2024/25 opened on 8 March 2024 and closes on 10 April 2024.The Communities in Transition project, which is part of the overall Tackling Paramilitary Activity, Criminality and Organised Crime Programme, is also delivering projects which protect children and young people from exploitation by paramilitary and criminal gangs. The project is currently delivering across the following eight areas:Carrickfergus and Larne (Antiville and Kilwaughter in Larne together with Northland and Castlemara in Carrickfergus)Derry/Londonderry (Brandywell and Creggan)North Down (Kilcooley & Rathgill)Lurgan/Craigavon (Drumgask and Kilwilkie)   West Belfast (Lower Falls, Twinbrook, Poleglass, Upper Springfield, Turf Lodge and Ballymurphy)North Belfast (New Lodge and Greater Ardoyne) Shankill (upper and lower, including Woodvale), andEast Belfast (The Mount and Ballymacarrett).To date CIT has delivered a total of 82 projects with a value of £19 million across the eight Communities in Transition areas. 51 of these projects have been completed, with plans to extend delivery of the remaining 31 contracts into 2024/25.At a recent CIT Showcase event discussion focussed on the following issues:The Impact of Paramilitarism on Health and WellbeingVulnerability to Exploitation: monetary, sexual, and child criminal (CSE/CCE)Vulnerable Youth: lack of aspirations, education, and positive choices, andThe impact of paramilitarism on the physical landscape.The issue of child exploitation however is not new to CIT and there is a range of projects already in existence which are working to protect children from exploitation by paramilitary and criminal gangs. Some examples are as follows.In North Belfast CIT supported the ‘Youth Realities on Stage’ project highlighting the dangers of exploitation and how to make positive life choices. There is also the ‘Youth Empowerment’ project working with disadvantaged youth (14-17 years) who are at risk of exploitation, supporting them to channel their energy and passion into something positive.In Lurgan CIT has supported the roll out of the ‘Pressure Project’, which educat

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what investment is being considered to enhance the role of the community pharmacy network in the provision of frontline healthcare.
answer_text: In the year 2023/24, we invested additional funding (£2.494m) in respect of vaccination services; roll out of the Urinary Tract Infection service; pilot testing the StrepA test and treat service; and, pharmacy collect service for lateral flow devices. The additional recurrent funding invested in 2023/24 has brought the recurrent funding to over £134m at the start of 2024/25. While I await the allocation of a budget for the 2024/25 year, my Department has recently committed to providing additional funding to this opening position in recognition of the service pressures that this sector is facing and within what is affordable. This includes committing an additional £2m per quarter in 2024/25 in respect of ongoing service pressures. Whether this can be made recurrent will be dependent upon the delivery of efficiencies and the budget allocation.A community pharmacy strategic plan will be published in the coming weeks and funding bids will be made to enable the delivery of these service areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given his comments on the inadequacy of his budget, what revenue raising measures he is considering in Health and Social Care during this mandate.
answer_text: You will be aware that my Department is facing unprecedented funding challenges and in recognition of this I’m seeking approval to defer the operational date of the Hospital Parking Charges Act for 2 years. This would enable charging to continue and safeguard some £6m income which is currently used to partially fund the expenditure associated with the operational upkeep and management of car parks. Without this deferral, the costs will need to be met by reductions to other expenditure areas within Health with the corresponding impact on services.  I have said before that difficult choices will need to be made in the face of the financial position and once a budget is confirmed I may need to consider other measures to support our severely constrained funding position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of commitments arising from Reshaping Stroke Care: Saving Lives; Reducing Disability.
answer_text: While capacity and funding constraints have impacted the pace of delivery, progress has nonetheless been made against a range of the priorities set out in the published Stroke Action Plan. This includes the development of a regional model for TIA (mini-stroke); the completion of a Thrombolysis audit; the introduction of Artificial Intelligence software in five hospitals to assist in the interpretation of scans; and the development of a specification for rehabilitation and long-term support.    Work is ongoing on other key priorities. This includes Thrombectomy, where a costed model identifying requirements to support expansion has been developed. In addition, agreement on a revised process to support the identification of a preferred option for Hyperacute Stroke Care has been secured. Officials are currently undertaking preparatory work to support the agreed process. This includes stakeholder mapping, the development of a coproduction plan and the development of outline timescales for the remainder of the project. These will be reviewed by the Project Board when it next meets later this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what discussions she has had with the Executive Office concerning the implementation of ethnic monitoring in the Civil Service by the end of 2025.
answer_text: My officials are represented on an Executive Cross Departmental Working Group to progress implementation of Ethnic Equality Monitoring in the Civil Service. Best practice in relation to ethnic monitoring will be incorporated into the design of the new technology solution under the Integr8 Programme.In the interim, the Civil Service will continue to monitor and report on the composition of its workforce and applicant pools, including ethnic monitoring in accordance with Section 75 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment on whether tarmac surfacing used in road construction is cost effective, given the prevalence of potholes.
answer_text: My Department places high importance on the need to adequately maintain the road network through a properly resourced programme of structural maintenance. The purpose of the programme is to maintain the integrity of the network. Such maintenance includes strengthening, surface dressing, patching and drainage works. It has been independently established that some £192 million (at 22/23 prices) is needed to maintain the structural integrity of the North’s road network. However, for many years the funding available to my Department has fallen far short of this requirement.My Department’s Resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities such as patching and grass cutting, has also been under significant pressure for many years. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety and protecting the transport network. Consequently, only the highest priority defects across the network are currently being repaired. As a result, we have all seen a continued deterioration in our infrastructure with large numbers of potholes and other defects developing across the road network, especially after periods of particularly cold or wet weather.The combination of these factors would indicate that the current issues on the road network are due to sustained underfunding and the recognised inability to upgrade road surfaces which have come to the end of their serviceable life, rather than a need to move to a different type of construction.The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6324/22-27, to detail the considerable progress that has been made by his Department in its work to implement the Cancer Strategy.
answer_text: Pursuant to my response to AQW 6324 we have seen significant progress on implementation of the Cancer Strategy: This includes:The development of Rapid Diagnosis Centres (RDCs) forming the basis for faster diagnosis pathways for people with cancer. Work on redesign proposals for haematological cancer pathways for the 3 main blood cancers (Myeloma, Lymphoma and Acute Leukaemia) has been completed. This work will help to transform the delivery of these services. A capacity and demand model on Systemic Anti-Cancer Therapy (primarily chemotherapy) has been completed and will be used to support the planning of Systemic Anti-Cancer Services.  Work is currently underway to review the delivery of breast services in Northern Ireland. An initial workshop with Trust medical staff and management was held on 12 March, with further engagement with charities and service users planned in coming weeks. A cancer research strategy for Northern Ireland is being developed. It is hoped that a draft strategy will be circulated for consultation by summer 2024.A review of cancer support services for Adolescents and Young Adults (AYA) has been completed, with work underway to develop regional minimum standards for this service.  A mental health advisory group has also been established to find ways of meeting the mental health and wellbeing needs of those living with cancer and their immediate family.  Work to implement an optimal care pathway for pancreatic cancer is underway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions he has taken in relation to hospital car parking since returning to office in February 2024.
answer_text: The Hospital Parking Charges Act, which will abolish hospital car parking charges from 12 May 2024, was passed by the Assembly for the best of intentions. I was, and remain, entirely supportive of those intentions.However, there are logistical issues that make the planned May 2024 starting date for free parking unfeasible. HSC Trusts have advised that the infrastructure required to manage free parking cannot be in place in time. This is due to legal challenges delaying a contract for Automatic Number Plate Recognition technology. As a result, the intended traffic management solution will not be operational by 12th May. In its absence, Trusts would have significant concerns about their ability to maintain safe access to their sites for patients, clients, visitors and staff. Preserving blue light routes would be a particular concern. In addition, it is unfortunately the case that the financial position for our health service has deteriorated in the past two years. A blanket removal of all charges would result in loss of income, resulting in £10million in costs per year in total to control access and maintain carparks.My primary concern as Minister is to protect access to hospitals for legitimate users.  Free parking without traffic management could very quickly lead to a free for all, with serious consequences for traffic not just in hospital sites but in surrounding areas.In light of these challenges I will be asking the Assembly to agree to a 2-year deferral of the Hospital parking Charges Act by accelerated passage, reflecting the looming starting date.  While the total removal of all charges would be deferred, car parking permits for eligible staff would be provided free of charge during the deferral period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 6622/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the work he has undertaken to progress the actions relating to palliative care as set out in the Cancer Strategy.
answer_text: The Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland contains three actions that relate to palliative care. The Palliative Care in Partnership Programme, which is co-led by the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency, is leading the work to progress these actions.    The Partnership brings together a wide range of stakeholders from across the statutory, independent and voluntary and community sectors, including hospices, as well as service users, to support the development and implementation of services for people who are living with palliative and end of life care needs and those important to them. The work to help deliver the palliative care actions in the Cancer Strategy will continue to be taken forward as part of the Palliative Care in Partnership’s workplan for 2023-26 which focuses on 4 priority areas:Early identification of and timely intervention for people with palliative care needs; Co-ordination & Quality Improvement in palliative care services; Palliative care education and training; and A public health approach to palliative care. Key areas that are being taken forward by the Palliative Care in Partnership Programme to support the palliative care actions in the Cancer Strategy include:Development of Regional Syringe Pump Guidance, education and documentation to standardise processes, education and documentation relating to syringe pump usage across all care settings;  Regional subscription to the Palliative Care Formulary has been put in place to provide instant access on a 24/7 basis to medication support for 250 clinicians caring for end of life and palliative care patients;A Quality Improvement project with District Nursing to support the continued development of the Palliative Care Keyworker role;Improving access to Out of Hours specialist palliative care advice available to support generalist HSC professionals caring for palliative care patients in the out of hours period; andScoping and costing options for moving to a 7-day Specialist Palliative Care Nursing service in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding to be distributed to third-sector palliative care providers in order to meet the requirements of the Cancer Strategy.
answer_text: No funding has been distributed directly to third-sector palliative care providers in response to the Cancer Strategy.The Cancer Charities Support Fund was launched in March 2021. This fund, which provided £10.7m to support local charities, this was financed by Department of Health, and administered by Community Foundation. Within this funding there were funds allocated to charities that provide support for patients during palliative care stage of treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will introduce legislation to allow for birth certificates, published before 11 March 2022, to be translated into Irish by the General Register Office.
To ask the Minister of Health what decisions he has made since returning to office in February 2024 about the implementation of the proposals to reconfigure stroke care.
answer_text: Since returning to office, I have been updated on progress on the delivery of the priorities outlined in the Stroke Action Plan. This included progress on the reconfiguration of stroke services which is led by the Hyperacute Stroke Project Board.  At this stage no decisions have been required and my Officials continue to work to deliver the Action Plan priorities. It should be noted, however, that the pace of delivery continues to be impacted by funding constraints.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the performance of Urgent Care Centre services on the Ulster Hospital site.
answer_text: The provision of Urgent Care services remains a key priority for the DoH in the implementation of the Urgent & Emergency Care Review. The South Eastern Trust has received recurrent funding of £6,486,363 from the No More Silos programme, to establish a range of Urgent Care pathways based predominantly on the Ulster Hospital site, that will be accessible via both GPs and the local phone first service. These Urgent Care services include the Enhanced Minor Injuries service, in the former ED, which aligns with the strategic direction of keeping EDs for emergencies only and scheduling unscheduled care.As part of the commissioning of these services, the SPPG Hospital Services and Finance Directorates will be working with the Trust over the coming months to assess the impact of recent investments and the Trust’s performance against its peers.  If South Eastern Trust is an outlier, SPPG will be seeking to understand the reasons for this and any further actions to improve service delivery.  Currently Urgent and emergency care data for clinical care episodes completed on or after 9th November 2023 in SEHSCT are not currently available due to the Trust’s transition to completely digitised health records. It is therefore not possible to provide an assessment of the performance of Urgent Care Services on the Ulster site.All Trust urgent care services form part of the SPPG’s wider Unscheduled Care performance meetings with Trusts, to support the oversight and evidence the continued impact of these services for our local populations. The SPPG Unscheduled Care Team will be monitoring performance against this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on progress on a regional endometriosis support centre.
answer_text: As you may be aware, my Department recently launched the Women’s Health Action Plan for Northern Ireland and we have also published the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Report for Gynaecology – this signals our commitment to working towards improving services for women which includes those suffering from endometriosis. It is our intention to progress the development of Endometriosis Service in the context of the GIRFT implementation plan which will be developed in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department's plans to open the gate at Cherryvale Playing Fields, Belfast, including a timescale for carrying out the relevant works.
answer_text: I can advise that the opening of the gate at Cherryvale Playing Fields onto the Knockbreda Road is a matter for the Belfast City Council (BCC) as the park is owned by them. Council consider that for safety reasons the gate could not be opened to the public unless a controlled crossing was provided across Knockbreda Road.To assist, my Department has been developing proposals for a controlled crossing at this location.  However, the provision of a controlled crossing will have a significant effect on a number of residents as they will no longer be able to park outside their houses due to the crossing itself and the associated zig zags. As such, the Department has recently written to BCC to highlight this and ask that they carry out further consultation with directly affected residents and those within the vicinity.The views expressed during the consultation will be appropriately considered and will inform the way forward in relation to this project.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how they will ensure that ethnic monitoring of the workplace is part of fair employment regulations in place across the public sector workplace by the end of 2025.
answer_text: It is anticipated that the new Race Relations Order legislation will become the legislative vehicle for Ethnic Equality Monitoring (EEM) here.It is worth noting that a level of voluntary workplace ethnicity monitoring already exists within the NICS.  The introduction of the EEM Pilot scheme will strengthen and harmonise the collection of this data across the public sector.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what discussions they have had with the Minister of Finance concerning the implementation of ethnic monitoring across the public sector workforce.
answer_text: The remit of the Cross Departmental Working Group (CDWG) on Ethnic Equality Monitoring (EEM) is to investigate and implement plans for the introduction of EEM. Executive Office Officials, as policy lead and chair of the CDWG on EEM, have been engaging with their Department of Finance counterparts to analyse the costs and benefits of amending existing systems, and/or incorporating EEM into the plan and budgets for the new Integr8 HR programme.  This will then be addressed by the CDWG.It is worth noting that voluntary monitoring of ethnicity already exists in a number of areas and the introduction of EEM will strengthen this and harmonise it across the NICS.  Meanwhile, Officials have been exploring ways to advance public service provision monitoring to ensure progress of the wider pilot.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they propose to bring forward any legislation arising from the Racial Equality Strategy 2015-2025.
answer_text: As part of the Racial Equality Strategy 2015-2025 a review of the current Race Relations (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 has been progressed, including a 12-week public consultation on possible changes which ended in June 2023. Responses and feedback from the consultation have been analysed and a report summarising the findings is under consideration. The report will inform the basis for any legislative changes and will move us a step closer to providing the best possible legal protection against racism and towards a more equal and safe society.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the value of a Racial Equality Strategy in the absence of ethnic monitoring.
answer_text: There is of course value in having a Racial Equality Strategy.  Achieving the aim of racial equality should be a target for us all and it can be progressed through a variety of means.The aim of Ethnic Equality Monitoring (EEM) is to identify and monitor inequalities, develop evidence-based policy, and plan service delivery.  Voluntary monitoring of ethnicity already exists in a number of service areas and the introduction of EEM will strengthen this and harmonise it across the NICS, and wider public sector. This will allow us to monitor the progress of all government policy including the current Racial Equality Strategy and any future strategies.The Racial Equality Strategy has been monitored to date through TEO’s mid-term Stocktake (October 2021); an ongoing independent review; the Racial Equality Indicators Report (most recent publication March 2023); and continuous engagement with the Racial Equality Subgroup and wider minority ethnic sector stakeholders.As acknowledged in the Racial Equality Strategy’s foreword, we are under no illusion about the size of the challenge involved in tackling racial inequalities and that it will require time, effort, and resources. Therefore, we remain committed to the implementation of the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions he is taking to bring down waiting times for the diagnosis of epilepsy.
answer_text: My department is taking forward a Regional Review of Neurology Services to improve services for patients with neurological conditions including epilepsy. The Review, is tasked with identifying the optimum configuration of neurology services for the next 10-15 years, determining the associated investment required and outlining an implementation plan. As part of the Review, workstreams were established to consider key parts of the Neurology pathway including Outpatient referral and long-term support. The Neurology Review Team is now in the final stages of developing priorities for change which, it is anticipated, will drive improvements across Neurology services, including access to Outpatient assessment and diagnosis. A final report is expected later in the Spring.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions he has taken since February 2024 to encourage GPs to return to rural areas where contracts have been handed back, including through public investment in premises as appropriate.
answer_text: Since returning as Health Minister, my immediate priority has been to progress the 2023/24 pay award for GPs.  I recently approved a 6% funding uplift for GPs and their staff in response to the recommendations of the 51st report of the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration (DDRB) published in July 2023.  This was an important step in recognising the important role of GPs and their staff and to acknowledging the increasing pressures and demands faced by General Practice. 	Stabilisation is a key concern and one which my Department is pro-actively taking steps to address.  It is recognised that there has been significant change in general practice since the original UK wide contract introduced in 2004.  My officials are working with the Northern Ireland General Practitioners Committee (NIGPC) to consider if a new model of delivery is appropriate in future years to ensure that services are sustainable in the medium and long term, and that patients will continue to be able to access high quality service and care from their GP now and into the future.  		My Department has also been working with Health and Social Care Trusts and GP Federations to promote stability in vulnerable, rural areas.  I am aware that over the past two years, 24 contracts have been returned however despite this high level of contract hand-back, through working with key stakeholders including HSC Trusts and GP Federations, it has been possible to ensure that no practice has closed and that patients can continue to access GP services in their areas.		Against the background of a challenging financial climate, my Department continues its focus on building the GP workforce which includes funding the Attract, Recruit and Retain scheme, the GP Retainer Scheme and the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme. 	The Attract, Recruit and Retain Scheme, which promotes recruitment in hard to recruit areas, was launched on 4th January 2023 and, as of March 2024, 48 GP Practices have applied to the Scheme and received approval in principle for funding.  23 GP practices have applied to the Scheme and received final approval for payment in respect of a recruited GP.  The annual number of GP training places remains at the increased level of 121 and my Department will be working with the NI Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA) to undertake a review of the GP training programme to consider innovations which will enhance the recruitment and retention of GP trainees in Northern Ireland.  In terms of investment in premises, my Department’s strategic priority is to increase capacity within primary care.  As part of a 10-year capital investment plan, investment is being made into GP hubs and spokes to improve GP accommodation across Northern Ireland.  My Department also continues to engage with all GP practices on premises improvement projects to support the ongoing delivery of GMS services and to create capacity to accommodate Multi-Disciplinary Team staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when a final decision will be made on hyperacute stroke units.
answer_text: While capacity and funding constraints have impacted the pace of delivery, progress has nonetheless been made against a range of the priorities set out in the published Stroke Action Plan. This includes the development of a regional model for TIA (mini-stroke); the completion of a Thrombolysis audit; the introduction of Artificial Intelligence software in five hospitals to assist in the interpretation of scans; and the development of a specification for rehabilitation and long-term support.    Work is ongoing on other key priorities. This includes Thrombectomy, where a costed model identifying requirements to support expansion has been developed. In addition, agreement on a revised process to support the identification of a preferred option for Hyperacute Stroke Care has been secured. Officials are currently undertaking preparatory work to support the agreed process. This includes stakeholder mapping, the development of a coproduction plan and the development of outline timescales for the remainder of the project. These will be reviewed by the Project Board when it next meets later this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she has decided to retain the existing rates cap for the entirety of the 2022-27 mandate.
answer_text: ANSWERThe existing rate cap, known as the maximum capital value cap, will be retained for the 2024/25 rating year. Any future change to the policy would require Executive agreement. This matter was the subject of public consultation which closed on 13 February and my Department is currently reviewing the responses received on this issue to inform next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what consideration she has given to extending rates relief to amateur sports clubs hit by rising utility costs.
answer_text: £4.7 million rate relief support was provided to amateur sports clubs during 2023/24. 80% Sports and Recreation Rate Relief is provided for premises that are:occupied for the purposes of a prescribed recreation;occupied by a not-for-profit club or society; andoccupied by a club or society that does not employ any person to engage in any recreation for reward.This relief applies to the parts of the property used solely for a prescribed recreation.  Community amateur sports clubs (CASCs) which do not have a full licence to sell alcohol, have access to increased relief of up to 100% for the qualifying sport and recreation areas within the club premises.I have no plans at this time to extend the Sports and Recreation Rate Relief.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update of Reshaping Stroke Care.
answer_text: While capacity and funding constraints have impacted the pace of delivery, progress has nonetheless been made against a range of the priorities set out in the published Stroke Action Plan. This includes the development of a regional model for TIA (mini-stroke); the completion of a Thrombolysis audit; the introduction of Artificial Intelligence software in five hospitals to assist in the interpretation of scans; and the development of a specification for rehabilitation and long-term support.    Work is ongoing on other key priorities. This includes Thrombectomy, where a costed model identifying requirements to support expansion has been developed. In addition, agreement on a revised process to support the identification of a preferred option for Hyperacute Stroke Care has been secured. Officials are currently undertaking preparatory work to support the agreed process. This includes stakeholder mapping, the development of a coproduction plan and the development of outline timescales for the remainder of the project. These will be reviewed by the Project Board when it next meets later this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration he is giving to enabling Sport NI to use part of its budget to provide financial support to sports clubs, particularly amateur clubs, affected by rising utility costs.
answer_text: I would refer the member for South Belfast to my previous response to her question regarding consideration for Rates Relief to Sports Clubs (AQW 8318/22-27) which references funding support to address utility costs under the Renewal Energy Fund. The Sport NI Renewable Energy Fund is currently closed however it may reopen next financial year, subject to funding being available.  Sport NI plan to publish advice for sports clubs on its website in the coming months, which can be implemented at minimal costs to clubs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6880/22-27, what public awareness campaigns are being carried out, specifically in relation to the potential to self-refer to a Cancer Genetics service.
answer_text: There are no public awareness campaigns raising potential to self-refer for genetic testing in NI. GPs, surgeons and oncologists are aware that patients with a significant family history of cancer and/or a personal history of cancer suggestive of an inherited predisposition, can be referred to the NI Regional Genetics Service for assessment of their personal risk of carrying a cancer predisposition gene variant.  This remains the most appropriate route of referral for the majority of patients.  Patients seen within the Regional Genetics service, who have been identified as carrying a gene variant which predisposes to cancer, are provided with information on how blood relatives can self-refer to Clinical Genetics for genetic advice and potential testing. There has been recent work to promote family history services via Trust and regional websites which has included information on the regional cancer genetics service. The regional genetics service and website provides clear information for professionals, patients and family members on how to make a referral to the clinical genetics service, and links to a range of leaflets on genetic testing generally and for specific cancers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration he is giving, in discussions with the Minister of Finance and other Executive ministers, to rates relief for sports clubs hit by rising utility costs.
answer_text: Sports Clubs can apply to Land and Property Services for rates relief/exemption under the Sports and Recreation Rate Relief scheme. Qualification is subject to satisfying the criteria set out by the Department of Finance, Land and Property Services.With regard to rising utility costs, I can advise that in 2023 Sport NI, an Arm’s Length Body of my Department, launched a Renewable Energy Fund (pilot scheme), a £1million pound National Lottery funded investment programme specifically aimed at improving environmental impact and reducing energy consumption at sports clubs.  The fund seeks to reduce carbon emissions, produce greener sports clubs, make savings on energy bills, and create a more financially sustainable club sector. This Fund is currently closed however it may reopen next financial year, subject to funding being available.		Sport NI plan to publish advice for sports clubs on its website in the coming months, which can be implemented at minimal costs to clubs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of his Department’s plans for responding to the Independent Neurology Inquiry, as outlined before he was appointed in February 2024.
answer_text: My Department published the overarching Independent Neurology Inquiry (INI) Implementation Plan on 27 July 2023, establishing the themes and workstreams to progress the 76 Independent Neurology Inquiry Report recommendations.Since then, work has progressed to outline the specific actions required against each INI recommendation and further engagement with wider INI stakeholders was completed to support this work.  The INI Action Plan was published on 7 March 2024, accompanied by a Written Assembly Statement. The INI Action Plan outlines a series of coordinated actions across a range of organisations, including the Department of Health, the General Medical Council (GMC), Healthcare Organisations and the Independent Sector. The Plan sets out the actions required to implement the 76 recommendations and outlines the relevant owners and the progress and current status in respect of each recommendation.It also sets out the detail of the Implementation and Assurance Framework and the engagement process which will sit alongside the INI Action Plan.Many of the actions to implement the INI Report are aligned or interdependent with other Departmental work programmes and work being undertaken in parallel by other organisations. Although significant progress has already been made towards implementation of INI recommendations, further coordinated action is required to ensure full implementation of the INI Report.My Department is committed to overseeing the implementation of the INI Implementation Programme, through the Inquiries Implementation Programme Management Board (IIPMB) which is chaired by the Permanent Secretary, and will work in partnership with stakeholders to take forward each of the INI recommendations.The INI Action Plan is available on the INI Implementation Programme webpage and can be accessed at www.health-ni.gov.uk/topics/independent-neurology-inquiry-ini

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what advice his Department is providing to people who want to use private healthcare for diagnoses of autism or ADHD, specifically around how any such diagnosis will be accepted within the public health system.
answer_text: HSCB wrote to private health providers and GP’s in June 2021 of their duty to inform patients that there may be ongoing costs associated with their private health care treatment until such time as they are seen by a HSC clinician.My Officials are currently meeting with Trusts to develop an updated advisory note for those service users accepted onto ASD or ADHD waiting lists explaining the waiting times, and that engaging with a private assessment centre for a diagnosis will not change their position on the waiting list. It will explain that they will be offered an appointment in chronological order of their referral to the appropriate service in line with IEAP guidelines. They will also be advised that they may remain under private health provision, including paying for treatments, such as prescriptions, until they reach their advised appointment date. With a private diagnosis anyone requesting a prescription, or other support through the HSC, will have to be assessed by the HSC prescribing clinician to assess their medical need.  Trusts will assess the private assessment process against compliance with NICE guidelines. If the assessment complies with NICE it will be accepted and ongoing support will be provided. If the assessment does not meet with NICE guidelines a HSC will complete the diagnosis. I hope this information is helpful and provides some clarity to you on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the commissioning of autism diagnosis services for adults.
answer_text: The Department is aware of the extensive waiting lists for adult autism assessment and the challenges that this presents to those people who need support. A Review of Adult Autism Services across the region has been undertaken and the recommendations were presented to the Permanent Secretary.  One of the recommendations is the appointment of a Lead for Adult Autism Services - recruitment of this post is currently underway. This role will be responsible for taking forward the findings emerging from this review; this will include consideration of the commissioning of autism diagnostic services for adults. Each Health & Social Care Trust (HSCT) is also implementing a number of actions to improve access to diagnostic services:Belfast Trust - continues to recruit and train staff within available resources, however demand for Autism diagnosis and subsequent interventions continues to rise. Northern Trust– The Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) Service is currently funded for 6.9 whole time equivalent staff to provide diagnostic assessments and interventions to adults in the Northern Trust area.  Southern Trust – have appointed a new ASD Mental Health Coordinator who is expected to take up post by the end of March 2024. This additional support will enable the ASD Adult Diagnostic service to increase its assessment clinics from once per month to weekly (circa 4 assessments per month from current baseline of 1 per month) commencing from April 2024.  South Eastern Trust– have updated their short-term intervention service model to a predominately group-based intervention which then enables capacity to be redirected to completing diagnostic assessments.  Western Trust – are working to triage all referrals in a timely and comprehensive manner in keeping with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidance. There are regular reviews of the Trust waiting list. The Trust service maintains compliance with both NICE guidance and the Northern Ireland Adult Autism Care Pathway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether analysis has been carried out on the cost of treating people in the health system due to not having been diagnosed with autism or ADHD within a reasonable time period.
answer_text: An analysis of the cost of treating people in the health system has not been carried out. Characteristics of autism and ADHD present in individualised ways, therefore given the differing care pathways and the need for individualised sources of support, this would be an extremely difficult analysis to undertake and would be unlikely to provide an accurate estimate of cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Independent Autism Reviewer will be appointed.
answer_text: I am aware of the responsibility to appoint an Independent Autism Reviewer for Northern Ireland and acknowledge the significant role this post will bring in advocating for autistic people and their families. I have therefore asked officials to provide advice on how this appointment may now be progressed so I can consider this more fully and in a timely manner.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will provide (i) a response; and (ii) an action plan to the independent report of the High Street Task Force, Delivering a 21st Century High Street, published in March 2022.
answer_text: The High Streets Taskforce’s report ‘Delivering a 21st Century High Street’ was presented to former Junior Ministers in March 2022 and includes recommendations that will be of interest to a number of Departments.  It will now be for Executive colleagues to decide how they wish to take forward any findings that may touch upon their own responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what discussions they have had with the Minister for Communities in relation to implementing the recommendations and proposals contained in the independent report of the High Street Task Force, Delivering a 21st Century High Street, published in March 2022.
answer_text: The High Streets Taskforce’s report ‘Delivering a 21st Century High Street’ was presented to former Junior Ministers in March 2022 and includes recommendations that will be of interest to a number of Departments.  It will now be for Executive colleagues to decide how they wish to take forward any findings that may touch upon their own responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what work they intend to undertake during the current financial year, and in the early months of the next financial year, to deliver on the High Street Task Force's vision of sustainable city, town and villages centres which are thriving places for people to do business, socialise, shop and use public services.
answer_text: The High Streets Taskforce’s report ‘Delivering a 21st Century High Street’ was presented to former Junior Ministers in March 2022 and includes recommendations that will be of interest to a number of Departments.  It will now be for Executive colleagues to decide how they wish to take forward any findings that may touch upon their own responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what budget is available during the current mandate to implement the recommendations and proposals contained within the independent report of the High Street Task Force, Delivering a 21st Century High Street, published in March 2022.
answer_text: The High Streets Taskforce’s report ‘Delivering a 21st Century High Street’ was presented to former Junior Ministers in March 2022 and includes recommendations that will be of interest to a number of Departments.  It will now be for Executive colleagues to decide how they wish to take forward any findings that may touch upon their own responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what role the Northern Ireland Bureau in Washington, DC has in promoting Northern Ireland as a location for the screen industry.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Bureau has been an enthusiastic advocate for Northern Ireland as a location for the screen industry.  The Bureau regularly partners with other organisations, including Solus Nua and Cinemagic to showcase the capability of the region in regard to film.  The Bureau established the Norman Houston Short Film award in 2022.  The inaugural winner, “An Irish Goodbye”, went on to win the 2023 Academy Award Best Live Action Short Film at the Oscars.  The Bureau collaborates with Cinemagic and the promotion of its work in the USA.  The Bureau also collaborated with ComcastNBC on the promotion of the film Belfast in Washington DC, including a special event involving Sir Kenneth Branagh to show the film.  The film Young Plato has also been promoted by the Bureau in conjunction with the Irish Consulate in New York City.
