question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the differentiation in status between Northern Ireland Bureaux in China and the United States, and the Northern Ireland Executive Office in Brussels.
answer_text: There is no differentiation in status between the three overseas offices.  All three offices are led at Director level by NICS staff and include a mix of NICS staff, who are diplomatically accredited, and country-based staff, who are employed on local contracts.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether an evaluation has been carried out on the work of the Northern Ireland Bureau in China.
answer_text: The role of all of the overseas offices will be reviewed as a part of the work to develop our new International Relations strategy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the budget allocated to the Northern Ireland Bureau in China (i) in 2023-24; and (ii) for 2024-25.
answer_text: The budget allocated to NIB China in 2023/24 is £276k made up of £170k operating budget and £106k salaries.  The budget for 2024/25 has not yet been allocated.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will provide an apology to the victims and survivors of Historical Institutional Abuse to be read into the record with the Executive in place.
answer_text: The Historical Institutional Abuse Inquiry recommended that an official apology should be delivered by the Executive to victims and survivors, wholeheartedly and unconditionally recognising that they failed to protect children from abuse.  The Inquiry also recommended that this should be done on a single occasion at a suitable venue.The official apology was offered on 11 March 2022 by five Executive Ministers - Michelle McIlveen, Conor Murphy, Nichola Mallon, Robin Swann and Naomi Long.  The apology was a unique occasion, facilitated by the closure of Parliament Buildings to ensure victims and survivors were able to be present in the Assembly Chamber.As stated on 4 March during Oral Answers to Questions to The Executive Office, we wholeheartedly associate ourselves with the apology offered to victims and survivors of historical institutional abuse.  We hope that this acknowledgement on the official report will complement the apology offered on 11 March 2022.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work of the Commission for Victims and Survivors in relation to victims of the Troubles since the start of the 2022-23 financial year.
answer_text: The Commission continues to work to improve the lives of victims and survivors and keep the adequacy of services under review. Since the start of the 2022-23 financial year the Commission has focused on three broad areas of strategic development: Advocacy, History and Education, and Young People. This has included a range of research, policy development, and engagement activities in areas such as legacy, support for the bereaved, and delivery of the House virtual reality project. The Commission has also completed a number of population surveys which will assist in setting baselines for reporting against outcomes for victims and survivors. The Commission has undertaken a replenishment process for new members of the Victims and Survivors Forum, and has piloted a youth panel which it plans to establish on a permanent basis shortly. The Commission continues to play a key role in a number of important areas such as design of the new draft Strategy for Victims and Survivors, development of the Regional Trauma Network and delivery of the Victims Payments Scheme.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when a Commissioner for Victims and Survivors will be appointed.
answer_text: TEO officials are currently working on the administrative preparations and the documentation required to begin the recruitment process for a new Commissioner for Victims and Survivors.The Commission for Victims and Survivors is regulated by the Commissioner for Public Appointments (CPANI) and, as such, the appointment process must comply with the Code of Practice for Ministerial Public Appointments. We hope to launch the appointment competition soon.  It is estimated that it could take up to 6 months to complete the recruitment competition.As a body corporate the Commission continues in legal existence even in the absence of a Commissioner. The Chief Executive Officer of CVS will now be responsible for the day-to-day business of the Commission. CVS will continue to operate on the basis of the work programme which was agreed by the previous Commissioner.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the recommendations contained in Sir Anthony Hart's Report of the Historical Institutional Abuse Inquiry they, or their predecessors, (i) amended; and (ii) rejected.
answer_text: Under the direction of the former Head of the Civil Service (HOCS), draft legislation based wholly on the Hart Report recommendations was developed in 2018.  Following a full 16-week consultation exercise, which received 526 responses, a consultation report, policy position paper and draft legislation were sent to the then Secretary of State.  Thereafter, at the request of the Secretary of State, the former HOCS led a series of discussions with local political parties to agree their position on those policy issues.  The draft Bill to establish the Commissioner for Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse (COSICA) and the HIA Redress Board reflected that political consensus and subsequently the legislation was enacted on 5 November 2019.Among the amendments made to the draft Bill were an increase in the standard award from £7,500 to £10,000 and agreement that no reduction would be made to any application made in respect of a deceased individual. The Hart Report had recommended that the spouse or children of a deceased individual should be able to claim 75% of the compensation that would have been awarded to their spouse or parent.Other amendments included the inclusion of non-judicial members on Redress Board panels and removing the bar on making applications where there had been previous civil proceedings.To date, the following Hart Report recommendations have been implemented - the establishment of both the statutory Commissioner for Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse (COSICA) and the HIA Redress Board; support services for victims and survivors; and delivery of the apology by the State and the responsible institutions on 11 March 2022.  Work continues at pace on the remaining Hart Report recommendations: contributions from the relevant institutions to the cost of redress; and the erection of a memorial in Parliament Buildings to remind legislators and others of what many children experienced in residential homes. We know implementation of this recommendation is important for many victims and survivors. We also recognise that there are wider views on the issue of memorial and so we are considering a wider memorial programme which goes beyond the Hart recommendation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether further improvements are planned to the phasing sequence of the traffic signals at the Finaghy crossroads.
answer_text: The current traffic signal sequencing at Finaghy crossroads is complex, with up to 6 traffic movements and 4 pedestrian movements that need to be controlled efficiently but more importantly, safely, within the limited road space available.The Department has introduced advanced dynamic traffic signal control called MOVA (Microprocessor Optimised Vehicle Actuation).  MOVA gathers flow information, on each movement, from detector loops buried in the carriageway surface and amends signal timing according to the prevailing flow.The benefits of MOVA control vary from site-to-site but delay savings are typically between 10-20%. There are no plans to carry out further work at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will make a final decision on the extension, or otherwise, of the Glider route from Cairnshill to Carryduff.
answer_text: In my previous tenure as Infrastructure Minister I announced the North to South Belfast Glider route from Carryduff in the South, along the Antrim Road to Glengormley.  Officials are currently  finalising feasibility studies to determine the viability of extending the routes into Glengormley and Carryduff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) where right-turn pockets are planned on the A24 Saintfield Road between Cairnshill and Carryduff; and (ii) the timescale for commencing this work.
answer_text: My Department has completed an options study for the provision of a right turn pocket on the A24 Saintfield Road at its junction with Baronscourt Road, which resulted in preliminary design of two road layouts.  However, given the signalisation of the A24 Saintfield Road junction with Manse Road, which provides a signalised right turn lane into Manse Road, from which Baronscourt development can be accessed, my officials have not progressed either option to the detailed design stage at this time. I have asked officials to keep this under review.There are three proposed right turn pockets on the Saintfield Road between Cairnshill and Carryduff, at lands north of 360 Saintfield Road; at 366 Saintfield Road; and at the Knockbracken Road junction.  Each one is associated with committed developments.  As these are developer led requirements, my department is unable to comment on timescales.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a final decision has been made on the route through Belfast city centre of the North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route.
answer_text: In my previous tenure as Infrastructure Minister I announced the North to South Belfast Glider route from Carryduff in the South, along the Antrim Road to Glengormley.  Officials are currently  finalising feasibility studies to determine the viability of extending the routes into Glengormley and Carryduff.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they are planning to ask for an evaluation of the Strategic Investment Board's role in advising on public sector reform.
answer_text: A periodic review of the Strategic Investment Board (SIB) is currently underway.  In line with good practice, the review is examining the current and future need for SIB, its role, responsibilities, performance and effectiveness.  The review has a particular focus on the strategic needs of the Executive and the wider NICS.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the proposal to transfer the Ebrington Barracks site to Derry City and Strabane District Council.
answer_text: TEO officials are working closely with Derry City and Strabane District Council to progress transfer of the Ebrington site.  More detailed work on future costs is ongoing and options for Council support with some initial elements of site management and maintenance are currently being discussed, with a view to new arrangements being put in place in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on what has been progressed at the Maze/Long Kesh site since January 2020.
answer_text: A new board was appointed to the Maze/Long Kesh Development Corporation on 30 October 2023.  We recognise the immense economic, historical and reconciliation potential of the site and look forward to working with the Board to maximise that potential.The Development Corporation have continued to preserve the listed and retained buildings on the site.  They have invested significantly in the repair and restoration of the WW2 hangars occupied by the Ulster Aviation Society, to ensure visitors can continue to enjoy the UAS collection.  The Royal Ulster Agricultural Society have further developed the area of the site which they hold under a Development Agreement.  The addition of the Logan Hall, The Fold and The Annex have contributed to the ongoing success of the Balmoral Show.  The Air Ambulance also operates from the site under licence.  In addition, the previous Board continued to develop its thinking and approach to the potential regeneration of the site.  The new Board will build upon this work.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when the process of recruiting a permanent secretary for the Executive Office will commence.
answer_text: Interviews for the post concluded on Friday 23 February 2024.  Final deliberations are underway, and it is hoped that an appointment will be made in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the provision of residents' parking schemes in the Belfast City Council area.
answer_text: The first residents’ parking scheme in Rugby Road and College Park Avenue in Belfast came into operation during April 2018.The review of residents’ parking schemes has been completed and the report will be published when I have considered the review findings.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what role the Northern Ireland Bureau in North America has as an advocate of Northern Ireland's status as having unique dual market access to both Great Britain and the European Union.
answer_text: As a diplomatic office of the Executive, the Bureau is responsible for building relations and promoting our region to targeted groups throughout North America. A key part of the Bureau’s work in this regard is to work closely with Invest NI in promoting Northern Ireland as a great place in which to invest and from which to trade, including in relation to access.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what research their Department has commissioned concerning victims since the start of the 2017-18 financial year.
answer_text: The HIA Inquiry report was published in January 2017. The focus since then has been on implementation of the recommendations, and we are committed to ensuring that victims and survivors have the acknowledgement, redress, and victim-centred support services they deserve. Under the legislation establishing COSICA, the Commissioner may undertake or commission research into matters concerning the interests of victims and survivors. The Department has not commissioned any research concerning victims and survivors of historical institutional abuse since the start of the 2017-18 financial year.In implementing the HIA Inquiry recommendations, the findings of relevant research have been considered when appropriate, including research by Professor Anne-Marie McAlinden (Apologies, Abuse and Dealing with the Past project at Queen’s University Belfast) which informed preparations for the apology offered to victims and survivors in March 2022. The Commissioner has also referenced research in her advice on a memorial.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether an evaluation has been carried out into their Department's work relating to victims who are not members of a particular victims group.
answer_text: Since the publication of the Hart report in 2017, the focus has been on implementing the recommendations of the HIA Inquiry to ensure that victims and survivors get access to the support, services and redress that they deserve. Throughout this work, we have been conscious that many victims and survivors are not involved with groups. While there has been no formal evaluation of the Department’s work relating to victims who are not members of a representative group, there have been concerted efforts to ensure engagement with as wide a range of victims and survivors as possible.  Officials have engaged with the Commissioner for Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse (COSICA), the Victims and Survivors Service (VSS), and community partners to use their existing channels and relationships to raise awareness of redress and support services.  In March 2023, TEO commenced a sensitive campaign to raise awareness of the advice, support services and financial compensation that is available to victims and survivors. This campaign is aimed at those victims and survivors who we have not yet managed to reach, including those who are not members of a particular victims’ groups. The campaign has included advertisements in newspapers and on social media, the distribution of posters to relevant organisations, doctors’ surgeries, benefit offices and court services, and a leaflet drop to every household. COSICA sought to support the TEO campaign by undertaking a separate parallel initiative to raise awareness among victims and survivors living in other jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) whether the Northern Ireland Bureau in Washington ,DC, has any direction over Invest NI offices on the west coast of the United States; and (ii) to detail the cooperation between the Northern Ireland Bureau in Washington, DC, and these Invest NI offices.
answer_text: Whilst they work closely together, the Washington Bureau has no role in directing the Invest NI offices on the west coast of the United States.   The Bureau collaborates closely with Invest NI at senior level in the US. The Executive Vice President is now based in New York and previously in Boston. Much of the collaboration on projects involving the Bureau takes place on the east coast.  There is occasional engagement with individual offices, for example in passing on potentially valuable connections made or in shaping visits.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the work of the Northern Ireland Bureau, China.
answer_text: The NI Bureau China works to promote our region as a place where people want to live and work, to visit, to study and invest, and to build the relationships that can help us deliver the Executive’s objectives.In the past year, the Bureau has facilitate a number of visits and events to develop mutually beneficial relationships.  These have included:St. Patrick's Day business networking event with Invest NI in Wuhan. Visits to Dalian, Shenyang and Changchun to maintain relationships and exploratory visit to Luoyang.Supporting two visits by officials in Autumn 2023 to meet with relevant Chinese government departments and key stakeholders.Supporting visits from Universities and Schools including facilitating a performance by Ulster University choir at the British Embassy Beijing.In parallel, the Bureau have continued to pro-actively maintain civic networks, facilitating online communication and visits between friendship and sister cities.  This has included supporting officials with the organisation of inward visits from Chinese local government representatives and senior diplomats, and providing early insights into emerging policy and key events in China.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what role the Northern Ireland Bureau, China, has as an advocate of human rights.
answer_text: The role of the Bureau in China is to promote our region as a place where people want to live and work, to visit, to study and invest, and to build the relationships that can help us deliver the Executive’s objectives.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the consultation process which led to a bench being the first consideration for a memorial to the victims of Historical Institutional Abuse.
answer_text: Engagement on a memorial has focused on the Historical Institutional Abuse Inquiry Report’s recommendation that a memorial should be erected to remind legislators and others of what many children experienced in residential homes.  The report, laid before the Assembly on 5 June 2020, recommended that a suitable physical memorial should be erected in Parliament Buildings, or in the grounds of Stormont Estate.Focused engagement with representative groups on the issue of a memorial commenced after the apology was offered to victims and survivors on 11 March 2022.  Our officials found, just as the late Sir Anthony Hart recognised in the Inquiry report, that there remain widely differing views.  Some victims and survivors wish to see the specific Hart recommendation implemented in full, while others would like to see memorials outside Stormont and Belfast, or the implementation of a living memorial.  In the absence of Ministers a further round of engagements was held during October and November 2023 to hear more views of victims and survivors, both those involved with representative groups and those who wished to offer their individual views.  It was positive that there was participation from many survivors who had not previously engaged with officials. Complementing engagement with victims and survivors, officials have also considered advice from Commissioner for Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse, which highlighted that the HIA Inquiry Report recommendation for a physical memorial should be implemented, and consideration should be given to a wider memorial programme to reflect the range of victims and survivors’ views. We will shortly be meeting victims’ and survivors’ representative groups and will consider all proposals to implement this recommendation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will publish a formal departmental response to the recommendations of the Inquiry into Historical Institutional Abuse, including with reference to redress and a memorial.
answer_text: The HIA Inquiry report was published in January 2017. The focus since then has been on implementation of the recommendations.  We are committed to ensuring that victims and survivors have the acknowledgement, redress, and victim-centred support services they deserve.The HIA Redress Board opened to applications on 31 March 2020, with the first payments being made seven weeks later.  As of 29 February 2024, the Redress Board has received 4,333 applications for redress, and Redress Board panels have considered 4,271 of those applications and have made award determinations totalling £84,986,750.Fiona Ryan was appointed as Commissioner in December 2020. In the same month the Victims and Survivors Service and two community partners, WAVE Trauma Centre and Advice NI, commenced the provision of services and support to victims and survivors. In July 2021 an independent facilitator was appointed to take forward negotiations with the relevant institutions as to an appropriate financial contribution to the overall cost of the HIA Redress Board and specialist support services, and this work is progressing.On 11 March 2022 Ministers Michelle McIlveen, Conor Murphy, Nichola Mallon, Robin Swann and Naomi Long offered a public apology to victims and survivors in the Assembly Chamber at Parliament Buildings.  The apology was followed by statements from each of the institutions where systemic failings were found in the Hart Report.A memorial to victims and survivors is the final recommendation of the Inquiry report to be implemented.  The Inquiry recommended that a suitable physical memorial should be erected in Parliament Buildings, or in the grounds of Stormont Estate.  Complementing engagement with victims and survivors, officials have also considered advice from Commissioner for Survivors of Institutional Childhood Abuse, which highlighted that the HIA Inquiry Report recommendation for a physical memorial should be implemented and consideration should be given to a wider memorial programme to reflect the range of victims and survivors’ views. We do not have any plans to publish a formal departmental response to the recommendations of the Inquiry.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what support is being offered to Health and Social Care Trusts in the recruitment of doctors from overseas, given that the General Medical Council says this recruitment will continue to be essential across the UK.
answer_text: International recruitment is a key avenue in addressing our resource issues in the future and ensuring that the health & social care service can deliver the safe and effective service for patients.The international recruits who arrive to work here across our HSC system provide an immensely valuable contribution to the delivery of health and social care services, let alone to our communities. Northern Ireland has a proud reputation of providing excellent pastoral care for newly arrived international recruits, underpinned by adherence to ethical international recruitment principles. The Department is committed to upholding the highest ethical standards in international recruitment.The Department published the new updated Code of Practice for International Recruitment in April 2023 which aims to protect the rights of international recruits across health and social care, including those employed within the independent sector. Since 2016 1,638 international nurses have been recruited regionally across the five HSC Trusts through the well-established and highly successful International Nurse Recruitment project. In addition, over the last 3 years a total of 207 international doctors have been recruited to HSC Trusts.Work is now progressing to develop the potential for international recruitment for other healthcare professions including pharmacy, social work and allied health professionals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on how Health and Social Care Northern Ireland connects to NHS England's Medicines Repurposing Programme.
answer_text: The Medicine Repurposing Programme, hosted by NHS England, identifies and progresses opportunities to use existing medicines in new ways, outside of the current marketing authorisation. Officials from my Department attend regular Medicines Repurposing Programme meetings along with devolved nation counterparts, to keep abreast of the medicines supported by the programme for planning purposes and horizon scanning.Charities, healthcare professionals and pharmaceutical companies across the UK, including Northern Ireland, are welcome to submit candidate proposals to the Medicines Repurposing Programme. The eligibility criteria and proposal form are available on the NHS England website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what discussions he has had with his counterpart in the UK's Department for Health and Social Care concerning opportunities for research organisations and the third sector in Northern Ireland to contribute to the Medicines Repurposing Programme.
answer_text: The Medicine Repurposing Programme, hosted by NHS England, identifies and progresses opportunities to use existing medicines in new ways, outside of the current marketing authorisation. Officials from my Department attend regular Medicines Repurposing Programme meetings along with devolved nation counterparts, to keep abreast of the medicines supported by the programme for planning purposes and horizon scanning.Charities, healthcare professionals and pharmaceutical companies across the UK, including Northern Ireland, are welcome to submit candidate proposals to the Medicines Repurposing Programme. The eligibility criteria and proposal form are available on the NHS England website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to raise awareness of genetic tests to identify women who have most to benefit from preventative drugs which protect against breast cancer.
answer_text: The Regional Breast Cancer Family History Group developed patient resources with representatives across all Trusts including a patient representative. The group developed web and video presentations which included an ‘introduction to the family history resource’, an ‘overview of the breast family history clinic’ and information on the Regional Genetics Service at Belfast City Hospital.  The history information and video resources were launched at the BRCA conference on 19th May 2023. All Trusts have these resources available on their websites, they are also available on the Northern Ireland Cancer website.Information for patients who may be eligible for genetic testing, along with information about genes such as BRCA1 and BRCA2, associated cancer risks and management options for women, is available on the Genetic Medicine Website.  For families where there is a known inherited genetic change in a high-risk cancer gene, relatives can either request a referral from their GP or self-refer to the Cancer Genetics service to discuss genetic testing. Cascade family testing is offered to blood relatives of the index patient to identify those family members who have inherited the variant and are at a higher risk of developing breast, ovarian, and other cancers. This enables the establishment of preventive and surveillance measures such as risk-reducing mastectomy or chemo prevention treatment. The Regional Breast History Group are continuing to explore further avenues for promotion of the regional resource such as primary care and other public websites.Breast surgeons/breast oncologists have access to genetic testing for patients who have a cancer diagnosis, at that stage risk reduction with hormone tablets is not appropriate.  Their genetic status may have an impact on their surgery and, more importantly, on their chemotherapy.  These patients then have their cancer treated as necessary.  Clinicians providing breast services are aware of the eligibility criteria for genetic testing, both in the context of patients diagnosed with breast cancer and patients who have attended breast clinics for assessment in whom there is no evidence of malignancy. Family history is routinely reviewed as part of breast assessment. Patients may be given advice, a review may be arranged for further follow up at the family history clinic, or a referral may be made to the Regional Genetics Service in the Belfast Trust as appropriate.The family members of these cancer patients will be followed up by the Genetics Service and may be referred to BRCA Clinic, or other family history service, depending on assessment of genetic status and risk due to their family history.  They are likely to be offered the full range of interventions from risk reduction surgery to chemoprevention tailored to their individual risk, stage of life and general health status. Family history clinics are embedded within the breast service of each trust.   A patient may also be referred by their GP to a famil

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what support he will make available (i) during the current financial year; and (ii) early in the next financial year, to prevent Housing Associations imposing rent rises in order to meet rising costs.
answer_text: Decisions on rent levels in Housing Association properties are taken by each Housing Association’s Board and my Department has no power to prevent rent increases by Associations.In terms of support for tenants, Housing Associations provide welfare advice, signposting to other support services and proactively work to identify those who are experiencing difficulty paying their rent in order to provide support to ensure that tenancies continue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the GP contract will be reformed and updated.
To ask the Minister of Health, given the rising average age profile for mothers, what adaptation is being made to the number of midwives in training.
answer_text: The age profile of women using maternity services has steadily risen over the last decade. Pregnancy for women who are aged 35 or older is considered a risk factor for adverse maternal and or perinatal outcomes such as preterm labour, chromosomal abnormalities, and labour complications. A well-established workforce analysis tool is used across the HSC to plan the optimum student numbers that are required to maintain and develop a midwifery workforce that can respond to the changing needs of women.My Department currently spends approximately £4.4m per annum on pre-registration training for midwives at Queen’s University Belfast. An all-time high annual intake of 115 training places was achieved in 2022/23.  I will continue to make the case for additional investment to develop our future workforce, in the context of what I expect to be a very challenging budget in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the operation of the Abortion Services (Safe Access Zones) Act (Northern Ireland) 2023.
answer_text: Safe Access Zones were introduced at all health service locations providing abortion services across Northern Ireland from 29 September 2023, under Assembly legislation to protect women and girls accessing abortion treatment services, information, advice and counselling, and to protect staff working at these locations. My Department's website includes a list and maps of Safe Access Zones, FAQs and Operator Guidance.In line with the requirements under Section 8 of the Abortion Services (Safe Access Zones) Act (Northern Ireland) 2023, the Department will publish an annual report setting out whether, in the opinion of the Department, each safe access zone has been effective in protecting the safety and dignity of protected persons.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work he has proposed, on a cross-departmental basis, to improve air quality and, consequently, health outcomes.
answer_text: As outlined in response to AQW 5627/22-27, air quality is the lead responsibility of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA).As set out previously, the Executive’s overarching strategic framework for Public Health, Making Life Better (MLB), recognises that health and wellbeing is influenced by many factors, including the wider physical environment. Numerous studies point to the physical and mental health benefits of access to green spaces and better air quality. MLB includes a commitment to protect and promote good health and wellbeing through working collaboratively to improve air quality to achieve objectives and targets established to protect health.An example of collaboration to date includes work with colleagues in DAERA to ensure that targeted effective air quality advice is issued to the general public. The advice sets out steps which individuals can take to minimise their exposure to pollutants, or to reduce or alleviate any resultant symptoms. This advice is available on the NI Direct website: http://www.nidirect.gov.uk/air-pollution-and-health. The advice also includes a link to DAERA’s air quality website (www.airqualityni.co.uk) where members of the public can find out current or forecast air pollution levels in their area.Going forward, my Department will continue to seek opportunities for collaborative working to help address this important issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to match NHS Health Check, which is available for people aged 40 to 74 in England.
answer_text: I have no current plans to match the NHS England Health Check in Northern Ireland (NI), however, the extract below from the HPSS (General Medical Services Contracts) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 is relevant to patients who have not presented to their GP within 3 years: -Patients not seen within three years5. Where a registered patient who—(a) has attained the age of 16 years but has not attained the age of 75 years; and(b) has attended neither a consultation with, nor a clinic provided by, the contractor within the period of 3 years prior to the date of his request;requests a consultation the contractor shall, in addition and without prejudice to his other obligations in respect of that patient under the contract, provide such a consultation in the course of which it shall make such inquiries and undertake such examinations as appear to him to be appropriate in all the circumstances.Please note that the obligation on the GP practice is to provide a consultation (to patients not seen for 3 years) as opposed to a health check however this would offer the patient an opportunity to discuss ongoing health issues and seek their GP’s advice on the need for diagnostic tests including weight, urine and blood pressure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has for blood pressure and atrial fibrillation awareness campaigns.
answer_text: The Public Health Agency (PHA) has not in the past run specific awareness campaigns on Blood Pressure or Atrial Fibrillation and there are currently no plans to do so. However, the PHA invests in a wide range of health improvement programmes targeting risk factors for high blood pressure which is common in people with Atrial Fibrillation. The PHA also continues to work with a range of other partners to promote activities that will encourage healthy lifestyles that contribute to the prevention of high blood pressure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will be proposing any amendments to the Hospital Car Parking Charges Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the work of the Open Government Implementation Group since September 2021.
answer_text: I am keen to work with Ministerial colleagues to advance open government. The ‘Open Government Implementation Group’ comprised representatives from departments and civic society - the Open Government Network.From 2016 to 2021, it co-created and contributed commitments towards the joint UK Open Government National Action Plans.  In a move towards more ambitious and meaningful Open Government commitments, the Implementation Group agreed to instead focus on Local Action Plans that relate to issues in this jurisdiction. 	The first Local Action Plan has one commitment – to develop our own Open Government Strategy. This work is being led by my department to provide a solid basis for action plans and replace the Implementation Group with a new and more cohesive co-creation structure and process.		My officials have continued to meet with the Open Government Network to co-create and progress the strategy. They also regularly meet with the Open Government Partnership (the international oversight body) as well as UK counterparts to share best practice and build stronger working relationships.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) what provision exists for birth certificates published before 2023 to be translated into Irish; and (ii) how this provision has changed since the beginning of the 2020-21 financial year .
answer_text: ANSWERThe General Register Office currently provides the option for births to be registered with a choice of certificate headings in English, Irish or bi-lingual English/Irish with all content in English. This was introduced on 11 March 2022 with the implementation of The Marriage, Civil Partnership and Civil Registration (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2022. There is currently no provision for birth certificates published before 11 March 2022 to be translated into Irish by the General Register Office.This provision has changed since the beginning of the 2020-21 financial year in that it is now possible for births to be registered with a choice of certificate headings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has for an audit of primary care practice around diagnosis and management of heart conditions, similar to the CVDPREVENT audit elsewhere in the UK.
answer_text: There are currently no plans to implement the ‘CVD Prevent’ programme in Northern Ireland.  However, the management of cardiovascular disease remains the focus of a number of indicators of the Quality and Outcomes Framework (QoF) in General Practice.  QoF rewards contractors for the provision of quality care and helps to standardise improvements in the delivery of primary medical services. QoF activity and reporting is currently suspended. Whilst GPs are currently not required to record activity undertaken in relation to QoF, they are required to continue to deliver care to these patients as clinically appropriate. QoF outcomes continue to be monitored by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the Department of Health (DoH).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he can provide an assurance that the funding plan for the Cancer Strategy for Northern Ireland can be managed within his Department's current budget.
answer_text: Additional funding for the cancer strategy will be considered along with the many demands for extra funding across the health and social care system at this time. It is expected that the overall financial position for all NI departments will remain very challenging for 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on (i) the timescale for the construction of the North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route; and (ii) the likely route of this service.
answer_text: The construction of the North Belfast to South Belfast Glider route is subject to the availability of funding.   The estimated cost is currently in the region of £145m and will be subject to review once the final outline business case is complete. £35m of funding has been secured through Belfast Region City Deal, however the £20m of New Deal funding that had been identified for the project has been paused, As such a considerable shortfall of £110m remains.In my previous tenure as Infrastructure Minister I announced the North to South Belfast Glider route from Carryduff in the South, along the Antrim Road to Glengormley.  Officials are currently finalising feasibility studies to determine the viability of extending the routes into Glengormley and Carryduff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he is considering a review of the effectiveness of Sunday opening hours.
answer_text: The Department for Communities has responsibility for the Shops (Sunday Trading &c.) (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 which governs shop opening hours on Sundays. There are no plans to review the effectiveness of the Sunday opening hours set out in this legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what work is ongoing to assist the housing needs of people with motor neurone disease.
answer_text: The ongoing work to assist the housing needs of people with motor neurone disease is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  		You should, therefore, direct your question to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount spent by his Department on public relations and events from 1 November 2022 to 31 October 2023.
answer_text: Recognising the importance of engaging with stakeholders, my Department has supported numerous events including a range of workshops, education/training seminars, engagement sessions etc. between 1 November 2022 and 31 October 2023. My officials have completed a manual trawl, and the list below provides details of events within the specified period. Spend for these events was in the region of £65,000.The Department also spent £45,000 on public relations activity within the same period. These costs included paid for advertising for ‘Know, Check, Ask’ pharmacy campaign, printed material for covid treatments, the development of an animation to support the introduction of the Integrated Care System and videos to raise awareness of the Children’s Social Care Services Review report.Events:1Adoption Panel Workshop2All Island Rare Disease Interdisciplinary Research Network3CAMHS Low Secure Workshop4Cancer - question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions he has taken on improving checks for haemochromatosis.
answer_text: The process for haemochromatosis patients to donate blood has been improved. Since July 2023, suitable patients deemed medically fit by their referring physicians can proceed directly into the donation process.  The standard practice of treatment for most healthy individuals involves fortnightly removal of a unit of blood until the target ferritin level is achieved. This process may take over a year, at which time the patient transitions into a maintenance phase every three to four months, depending on blood results and tolerability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions he has taken on improving support for patients on Active Monitoring after a diagnosis of leukaemia or prostate cancer.
answer_text: All prostate patients have a key worker or a Clinical Nurse Specialist (CNS). CNS’s offer a Holistic Needs Assessment, (HNA) for all men on their first review following treatment and are involved with men during the whole pathway from investigation, diagnosis through treatment and follow up. The HNA allows men to talk through their needs and concerns with their CNS. The patient and CNS then agree on a plan for care and support needs.Prostate cancer patients on active monitoring are managed in line with an agreed nurse led prostate active surveillance pathway. Nurse led prostate cancer review clinics are established in all 4 of the Trusts where urology patients are managed. These clinics review men undergoing active surveillance, watchful waiting, androgen deprivation and following radiotherapy, brachytherapy and robotic prostatectomy treatments.The majority of men suitable for the Active Surveillance pathway will be followed up for their prostate cancer by a Clinical Nurse Specialist (CNS).  These follow up clinics are a mix of telephone and face to face reviews and take place on a 3-4 monthly basis for each patient. At these appointments, the patient will receive updated PSA blood results and or results from surveillance MRI. Their lower urinary tract symptoms will be discussed and addressed (if required) and they will be given the opportunity to ask questions about their condition. If appropriate, their next MRI scans will be ordered, they may be booked for further surveillance biopsies or represented at the MDT meeting if needed.LeukaemiaLeukaemia patients are managed in line with recognised clinical pathways which includes palliative chemotherapy or supportive care. They are regularly reviewed at outpatient clinics.  Additional review appointments are arranged as and when clinically indicated.  Patients eligible for transplant are referred onto Belfast Trust who provide this service for the region.With the exception of SEHSCT where Leukaemia patients are reviewed as part of consultant led follow-up and patients may contact their key worker between appointments, in other Trusts for patients diagnosed with a haematological malignancy and on active monitoring have regular review carried out by a clinical nurse specialist (CNS).  This pathway supports the patient and facilitates rapid access to the consultant should this be required.  Clinical Nurse Specialists are present at the time of diagnosis and explain the diagnosis and the Plan of Care.  CNS also invite the patient to complete a Holistic Needs Assessment post diagnosis.  In addition, there are Health and Wellbeing sessions for patients on the Active Monitoring Pathway in some Trusts.All cancer patients are signposted or referred to the Trusts Macmillan Health and Wellbeing service which can refer the patients to available relevant support services and groups.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to deliver the commissioning of ADHD services, including diagnostics and treatment pathways.
answer_text: Although there are currently no commissioned services for Adult ADHD, provision has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams. While this has ensured a level of provision is available, the Department is aware that this has also led to inconsistencies between Trusts including pathways and gaps between what can be provided by Trusts in terms of demand and the level of prevalence of adult ADHD, leading to individuals waiting longer than intended for assessment and support.The Health and Social Care Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of patients with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.SPPG alongside Policy colleagues in the Department are keeping the situation regarding the commissioning of Adult ADHD services under review, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning of services, any decision to commission Adult ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how many people were admitted to Emergency Departments in 2022; and (ii) how many of these people waited more than six hours to be admitted.
answer_text: Information on (i) the number of attendances at Emergency Departments (EDs) in Northern Ireland, in 2022, and (ii) how many of those attendances spent more than 6 hours in ED after arrival, before being admitted to hospital, is provided in Table 1 overleaf. Please note that the number of attendances is based on new and unplanned review attendances only and does not equate to the number of patients treated, as a patient may attend emergency care departments more than once during each year.A new attendance refers to the first unplanned attendance by a patient for a new clinical condition at an ED / Minor Injuries Unit (MIU). An unplanned review attendance relates to any patient who returns to an ED / Minor Injuries Unit (MIU) without written instruction, for the same or related clinical condition within 30 days of the first (initial) attendance.The length of time a patient waits refers to the time between entering the ED and being logged in at reception, until leaving the ED via discharge or admission to hospital.Table 1: Number of Attendances (New and Unplanned Reviews Only) at Emergency Departments in Northern Ireland in 2022, and of those, the Number who Spent more than 6 Hours in ED from Arrival, before being Admitted to HospitalYearTotal AttendancesWaits of more than 6 Hours before being Admitted 2022740,492106,761

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of the Podiatry Workforce Review 2019-2029.
answer_text: My Department appointed a new Chief Allied Health Professions Officer, Michelle Tennyson, in January 2024. With this appointment approval has been given to re-activate the Central Advisory Committee for Allied Health Professions (ACAHP). The first meeting is to take place in March 2024. A workforce sub-committee will be established reporting to the Central Advisory Committee for Allied Health Professions. This sub-committee will be responsible for implementing the recommendations from the completed workforce reviews, including the Podiatry Workforce Review 2019-2029. This work will be multi-faceted, and my officials will collaborate closely across my Department on the various priorities arising from the sub-committee.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what discussions he has had with Executive colleagues around reversing the funding cuts which applied to the Healthy Happy Minds Project in March 2023.
answer_text: The Healthy Happy Minds Pilot concluded on 31 March 2023.  An evaluation of the pilot has been carried out and I will assess its findings as I seek to better support the emotional health and wellbeing of learners.  The Independent Counselling Service for Schools (ICSS) remains available to post-primary age pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the research basis for his Department's decisions around prioritisation of mental health support for children of primary school age.
answer_text: Improving mental health support for all children and young people, not just children of primary school age, is a priority for my Department. Firstly, the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 sets out a number of key strategic actions to further improve the emotional wellbeing and mental health of our children and young people over the next decade, from infancy through to older adolescents. This includes increasing Child and Adult Mental Health Services (CAMHS) funding and putting in place a ‘no wrong door’ approach.As well as this, Actions 1 and 2 of the Strategy set out key commitments for promoting mental health through early intervention and prevention, with year-on-year actions covering a whole life approach, reaching from infancy to older age. A three-year Early Intervention and Prevention Plan has been developed under the Strategy. One of the key actions within this plan is to ensure collective responsibility for promoting and supporting the emotional wellbeing of all our infants, children and young people.In addition to the Mental Health Strategy, in February 2021, the Department of Health and the Department of Education jointly launched the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.  The main emphasis of the Framework is to support educational settings to promote emotional health and wellbeing at a universal level, through a holistic, multi-disciplinary approach, building resilience to enable our children and young people to cope with challenges they may face in life and providing early and enhanced support for those who may be at risk or showing signs of needing further help. Services and projects that have been established under the Framework are available across primary and post-primary schools. The above strategies and frameworks were formulated using a wide range of research and evidence bases. Examples of this include: the 2020 Youth Wellbeing Child and Adolescent Prevalence Survey; the ‘Elephant in the Room’ research produced by the NI Youth Forum and Children’s Law Centre; and the ‘Still Waiting’ report produced by the NI Commissioner for Children and Young People.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timescale for bringing forward a regional strategy for maternity services.
answer_text: In light of several reports concerning the safety of maternity services, both locally and nationally, my Department established a Maternity and Neonatal Services Safety Oversight Group to receive assurance on the safety of maternity and neonatal services for the population of Northern Ireland.A broad programme of work is ongoing with an immediate focus on governance and safety issues highlighted across maternity and neonatal services. Collectively, this work will be fundamental to improving maternity services and improving outcomes for mothers, babies and their families in Northern Ireland and will help inform the future strategic direction for maternity and neonatal services and the need for a new maternity strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an outline of actions being taken towards prevention and earlier diagnosis of chronic kidney disease.
answer_text: The management of chronic kidney disease remains the focus of a number of indicators in the Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF) in General Practice.  QOF rewards contractors for the provision of quality care and helps to standardise improvements in the delivery of primary medical services. QOF activity and reporting is currently suspended. Whilst GPs are currently not required to record activity undertaken in relation to QOF, they are required to continue to deliver care to these patients as clinically appropriate. QOF outcomes continue to be monitored by SPPG.For secondary care improved CKD management (e.g., adequate blood pressure management) including use of SGLT-2 inhibitors to reduce cardiovascular events and slow progression of CKD is the key intervention. Patients who develop chronic kidney disease present with many different symptoms many of whom are referred to and cared for by our endocrinology teams. During this care, it is standard practice to work to prevent chronic kidney disease from developing among those living with diabetes.  This includes well established evidence-based care relating to dietary measures, weight and blood pressure control.    Through annual reviews with patients, clinical teams will action in line with early identification and treatments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in addition to the current proposals for reshaping stroke services, what extra actions will be put in place for prevention and community monitoring for hypertension.
answer_text: Risk factors for stroke such as hypertension are managed in primary care through the Quality and Outcomes Framework (QOF) in general practice.  The indicators within the Framework focus on promoting prevention of cardiovascular problems such as stroke through satisfactory blood pressure control of hypertension patients which can be achieved through lifestyle advice or the use of antihypertensive drug treatment. In addition, the Public Health agency invests in a wide range of health improvement programmes aligned to stroke prevention. Prior to the Covid-19 pandemic, a pilot for community pharmacies to test for hypertension was undertaken. Evaluation of the pilot service will inform the future development of this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the consideration of a children's services directorate in his Department.
answer_text: It is assumed that this is a reference to recommendation 15 from the Independent Review of Children's Services in Northern Ireland. The Review report was published in June 2023. This recommendation has been considered in the context of ongoing organisational re-design work being undertaken by my Department and would have implications for the Department as a whole.It is considered that policy, professional and commissioning/performance management parts of the Department have very distinct roles, although it is important that they work in concert and in support of each other. Work is ongoing to identify how all three parts of the Department as they relate to children's social care services work effectively together. A workshop which considered how all three parts of the department work effectively together took place in early February and a further workshop is being planned. In addition, work is ongoing to revise Departmental circulars with the aim of clarifying roles and responsibilities both within the Department and within Health and Social Care Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when multi-disciplinary teams will be present in all primary care settings in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The MDT model remains the key to stabilising and strengthening vital Primary Care services to ensure they can continue to provide high quality care to our people, now and into the future.  Whilst the budget for 2024/25 has not yet been set it is expected that the overall financial position for all NI departments will remain very challenging. My Department remains committed to the further implementation of the MDT model across Northen Ireland, and is developing a plan for rollout over the next number of years subject to the availability of the necessary recurrent funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the development of sign language legislation.
To ask the Minister of Health how Rapid Diagnosis Centres will work alongside Urgent Care Centres with the overall vision of the Health Service operating 24/7.
answer_text: Rapid Diagnosis Centres (RDCs) seek to provide rapid diagnosis pathways for patients who may have cancer. They currently operate during standard working hours.Urgent Care Centres will utilise existing diagnostic services in the same way as they are currently accessed via an Emergency Department (ED). The patients will present to urgent care services rather than EDs but will continue to access the required diagnostic service, post triage and assessment, as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps his Department has taken to address the systematic maladministration reported by the Public Services Ombudsman in June 2023 with regards to communication with patients on waiting lists.
answer_text: In response to the NIPSO report regarding patient communication, the Department of Health has developed an action plan to address the key recommendations in the report. The Department is working with HSC Trusts to progress these actions through a number of service improvement initiatives, including the roll out of a new text messaging service which will help improve patient communication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department has undertaken in response to the finding by the Office for Statistics Regulation, in 2021, that there are limitations in mental health data in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: DoH recognises the importance of having robust data systems in place to ensure that data is accessible, usable and can act as a strategic asset for decision making, policy development and to support the delivery of services.  Reflecting this need, in 2022 DoH published a Data Strategy for Health and Social Care Northern Ireland (HSCNI) 2022-2030, which outlines the vision, mission and objectives for data and information for health and social care up to 2030.  A copy of the Strategy is available on the publications section of the DoH website.  Implementation of this Data Strategy, which is subject to resource availability, will help ensure development of robust datasets across health and social care, including for mental health services, and is aligned to the Office for Statistics Regulation’s recommendation.Action 34 of the Mental Health Strategy commits to the development of a Regional Outcomes Framework in collaboration with service users and professionals, to underpin and drive service development and delivery.  Considerable work has been progressed to develop the Regional Outcomes Framework, informed by input from, and engagement with, a wide range of stakeholders including health care providers, statisticians, academia, carers, and service users, in line with the Mental Health Strategy’s principles for co-production and co-design. The Framework was finalised in October 2022 and implementation is currently underway, aligned to the delivery of the Encompass project.Once fully implemented, the Outcomes Framework will help ensure that the collection of robust data to enable outcomes measurement is firmly embedded in the delivery of mental health services. In the interim, improvements are being taken forward to improve accessibility to mental health data, for example through more effective signposting of mental health information on the DoH website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what share of his Department's budget, including that of its arm's-length bodies, is allocated to youth sport, given the associated long term benefits to both mental and physical health.
answer_text: My Department’s “A Fitter Future for All”, the current obesity prevention strategy for Northern Ireland, seeks to promote greater participation in all forms of physical activity across the life course. Physical activity plays a key role in improving population health and well-being. While Department for Communities leads on the new Sport and Physical Activity Strategy, we continue to work collaboratively with them on this agenda. For example, in 2023/24 the Public Health Agency (PHA) provided circa £210,000 towards youth sport programmes/activities.  However, it is difficult to provide a definitive figure as elements of activity for youth sport is included within wider commissioned programmes/grants, and as such the detail on the youth sport funding element is not readily available. There is also wider support for people of all ages to participate in physical activity, funded by the PHA and the Health and Social Care Trusts.My Department is currently consulting on a new Strategic Framework to prevent the harm caused by obesity, and improve diets and levels of physical activity in Northern Ireland. This will help set out the way forward on this key public health challenge.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will set out an implementation plan with timescales to complete the reshaping of stroke services.
answer_text: In line with the commitment in the Reshaping Stroke Care Action Plan, the Department has established a Hyperacute Stroke Project Board to oversee the stroke reconfiguration work. Whilst there has been a delay in commencing this work due to resource and budget constraints, I can confirm that this Board met on 24th January 2024 to agree a process to identify a preferred option for Hyperacute Stroke Care. With agreement secured, work is currently under way to identify associated timescales and develop a communication and engagement plan to support stakeholder involvement in the process. This work will be reviewed by the Project Board when it next meets in March 2024. It is intended that stakeholders will be updated following that meeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what support his Department is offering to Health and Social Care Trusts to communicate effectively with patients and their families concerned about the transfer of services, notably with regard to unscheduled general surgery, across the hospital system.
answer_text: Communications regarding changes to local services are primarily a matter for individual Health and Social Care Trusts.  Trusts use a variety of communication channels including consultation exercises, direct engagement, on-line information, and media releases to reach their population. In doing so, they take account of their legislative requirements to involve and consult. These local communications are supported by regional messaging as required.With regard to general surgery in particular, my Department continues to work closely with Trusts as they implement the actions from Review of General Surgery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he plans to improve communication of the stroke reconfiguration plans.
answer_text: In line with the commitment in the Reshaping Stroke Care Action Plan, the Department has established a Hyperacute Stroke Project Board to oversee the stroke reconfiguration work. Whilst there has been a delay in commencing this work due to resource and budget constraints, I can confirm that this Board met on 24th January 2024 to agree a process to identify a preferred option for Hyperacute Stroke Care. With agreement secured, work is currently under way to identify associated timescales and develop a communication and engagement plan to support stakeholder involvement in the process. This work will be reviewed by the Project Board when it next meets in March 2024. It is intended that stakeholders will be updated following that meeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration he is giving to a full ban on the sale of energy drinks to people aged under 16, in line with proposals in existence since 2019 for both England and Scotland.
answer_text: In the UK, there are no legal age restrictions on the sale of any caffeine containing food and beverages, including energy drinks. In 2018, the UK government consulted on the introduction of a proposed ban on selling energy drinks to children. To date the results of the consultation have not been published and there has been no further development on this policy. However, many larger retailers and supermarkets have voluntarily stopped selling energy drinks to under 16s.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to provide additional support for GPs to interpret and act on CA125 ultrasound and blood test results to ensure prompt diagnosis of ovarian cancer.
answer_text: Ovarian cancer in women, can present with a wide variety of vague symptoms and there are many other conditions that can cause some symptoms similar to those of ovarian cancer such as fibroids, Irritable Bowel Syndrome, constipation, Coeliac disease, Crohn’s disease or Colitis.It should be noted that GPs don’t definitively diagnose ovarian cancer, the definitive diagnosis of ovarian cancer will be made in secondary care, by a Gynaecologist.  The GP role is to be aware of the possibility that the patients presenting symptoms may be caused by an ovarian cancer, and ensure that  the Ca-125 blood test and referral for ultrasound are part of the work-up, prior to referral.  If the Ca-125 is in any way above the normal range, and/or the ultrasound shows any ovarian pathology, the GP will know that a referral to gynae is required.All GPs would have had teaching and training in clinical diagnosis, as part of their undergraduate training in Medical School and subsequently during their GP training course.  In addition, they have access to regular post-graduate update training, on an annual basis, on many different clinical issues, including gynaecology, and their learning is reviewed and discussed at an annual appraisal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his Department's assessment of the findings from the Queen's University and others' research on the impact of poor air quality on mortality.
answer_text: Air quality is the lead responsibility of the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and we continue to work closely with colleagues in relation to related health outcomes. The Public Health Agency (PHA) is aware of the Northern Ireland pollution dashboard which compares pollution over time with World Health Organization’s (WHO) recommended thresholds. The dashboard can report changes in pollution over time. PHA colleagues are also aware of a range of research in this area, including recent research from Queen’s university and the Royal College of Physicians’ 2016 report “Every breath we take: the lifelong impact of exposure to air pollution” and would support initiatives from DAERA to implement WHO standards to improve the health of the population of Northern Ireland.The Executive’s overarching strategic framework for Public Health, Making Life Better (MLB), recognises that health and wellbeing is influenced by many factors, including the wider physical environment. Numerous studies point to the physical and mental health benefits of access to green spaces and better air quality. MLB includes a commitment to protect and promote good health and wellbeing through working collaboratively to improve air quality to achieve objectives and targets established to protect health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how the use of domiciliary care agencies in the provision of home care packages represents value for money for the Health Service.
answer_text: Within N. Ireland domiciliary care is provided by both statutory and independent sector agencies.  This ranges regionally from an approximate 20 % statutory and 80% independent sector spilt to a 40/60% spilt (respectively) across the region.The five HSCT’s receive baseline funding for domiciliary care from Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG).  This money is devolved down to SPPG from DOH.  SPPG have a role to performance manage the HSCT’s against activity delivered in domiciliary care.The current problem is around the recruitment and retention of domiciliary care staff.  The role of a domiciliary care worker is very different now compared to the past.  The staff are working with people who have more complex needs, who are living longer and are choosing to remain living at home rather than opting for institutional care.  Post covid, family and carers are returning to the work place and therefore have less time to provide care to loved ones which is increasing the demand for domiciliary care.In NI, the current base rate for independent sector domiciliary care is £18.54 per hour, which is at the lower end of the average across the UK. (It should be noted that some Domiciliary Care agencies may cost more than the base rate of £18.54, depending on the service or locality.)This largely allows for a pay rate of £10.82 to £11.33 to domiciliary care staff.  (Employer’s costs such as NIC and travelling expenses for the direct care and support and corporate overheads subsuming the balance).  This hourly rate supports those who need the services to remain in their own home rather than require more costly support such as a residential care home placement, which in NI currently starts from £694 per week (c£36k per annum) for an Independent Sector residential care home.  Or the alternative of a delayed discharge within a hospital setting which costs an average of c£440 per day.  I would direct you to the recent report HOW MUCH DOES IN HOME CARE COST in February 2024 - A Definitive Guide (ukcareguide.co.uk)  which indicates:On average, for the cost of overnight care at home in the UK, or day care, you can expect your home help costs to be anywhere between £15 and £30 per hour;The average cost for a live-in carer in the UK is between £800-£1500 per week The research shows that average care home fees range from £27k to £39k per annum for a residential care home.  Strategic Planning and Performance Group are currently working with the 5 HSCT’s on a pilot that is designed to bring some of the previously allocated domiciliary care hours back into the system by ensuring timely reassessment of need post hospital discharge, thereby ensuring efficiency of the baseline resource. It is very clear that domiciliary care provides value for money over other options available within the health care system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his long-term plan for publicly funded dentistry.
answer_text: A key part of the access issues facing patients are the rates being paid to our dentists for treatment.  The absence of Executive Ministers has meant that it has not been possible to progress the recommended pay uplifts for staffing groups for 2023/24.  This is now my immediate priority, and I am meeting with Trade Unions in early course to take this forward. Officials have already been making arrangements in anticipation of funding being made available to ensure that uplifts can be implemented as quickly as possible.I know that an additional £95m has been invested in General Dental Services via the Financial Support Scheme and Rebuilding Support Scheme since April 2020. This came to an end in July 2023 due to the severe financial pressures arising from the Department’s 2023/24 budget settlement.A pilot scheme is currently running to provide appointments to unregistered patients who require emergency dental treatment during the working week. If a patient is not registered with a dentist, requires emergency dental treatment, and has been unable to access an appointment, they can contact their local Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) office for advice.I am also conscious of the progress that has been made in the rest of the UK on dental reform and I will be discussing these issues with officials as soon as possible, to ensure that the sector here in NI is supported in delivering important care to patients. More immediately, officials have been developing investment proposals to further support General Dental Services and I will consider them in early course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when the Public Health Agency will update its advice on bedsharing with infants to be in line with NHS advice; and (ii) what the timescale is for the removal of previous advice.
answer_text: The current Public Health Agency (PHA) guidance, revised in October 2022, is in line with NHS guidance.  The information contained in the books “Birth to Five”  (2023) and “The Pregnancy Book” (2023), which are issued to all expectant and new parents also contain the same advice relating to safer bedsharing as the recently published NHS guidelines.However, following the recent media publicity relating to safer bedsharing, the PHA is planning to review and further update its guidance within the next few weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will outline how he is moving forward on the reform of adult social care, including a budget for the reforms.
answer_text: Following the publication of an Evaluation and Summary Report on the Reform of Adult Social Care Consultation, in May 2023, the Department has established the Social Care Collaborative Forum.The purpose of the Collaborative Forum, is to provide a formal mechanism to support opportunities for greater co-operation, co-production and joined up working between the Department, the social care sector, and across Government, to implement the proposals arising out of the Consultation.Many of the purposed reforms are subject to confirmation of funding and will therefore require prioritisation and planning, and longer-term budget stability to ensure realistic delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people were seen for an urgent cancer check during 2023.
answer_text: Information on the number of people seen for an urgent cancer check during 2023 is not collected centrally in the Department. This information has been sourced from each of the five Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts and is presented in table 1 below. This is the number of people seen in their first consultant-led outpatient appointment after being referred with a suspect cancer. Not all of these patients will have a confirmed cancer diagnosis following the appointment.Table 1: The number of people seen for an urgent cancer check in each HSC Trust during 2023HSC TrustNumber SeenBelfast15,946Northern18,872South Eastern19,087Southern12,551Western13,537Total79,993Source: HSC Trusts

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment has been made of the impact a vacancy in the ministerial office post 28 October 2022 will have on departmental (i) decision making; and (ii) spending.
answer_text: While this remains a hypothetical situation, it is clear that any period without Departmental Ministers will significantly hamper decision making and spending.  Standing Order SO19 2(e) refers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on listing of the former Bank building at 364 Lisburn Road, Belfast.
answer_text: The former bank building at 364 Lisburn Road Belfast was listed at Grade B2 on 23 November 2021. Its listed building reference is HB 26/18/065.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how social value is being incorporated into contracts for parking enforcement.
answer_text: The current parking enforcement contract does not include Social Value clauses as it was awarded in November 2012, prior to this being a contract issue. You will be interested to know that my Department is in the process of procuring a new parking enforcement contract which will include Social Value clauses, as from 1 June 2022 Procurement Policy requires that tenders must include a minimum of 10% of the total award criteria to score Social Value.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what inquiries he has received seeking access to the Northern Ireland Electronic Care Record from hospitals that are currently unable to access it, since the last Assembly Election.
