answer_text: All healthcare professionals employed by Health and Social Care organisations (HSC) have access to the information held in the NI Emergency Care Record (NIECR) appropriate to their role.Independent healthcare providers under contract to the HSC also have access to health and social care information, appropriate to the services provided.Access to personal, sensitive information held on HSC systems is strictly governed. Access to patient information held on the NIECR by organisations not engaged by the HSC will require those organisations to demonstrate their ability to adhere to the strict cyber security standards, clinical governance standards and information governance standards in force within the HSC. The Independent Neurology Inquiry highlighted the opportunities to enhance patient safety by encouraging those operating in the independent sector to maintain their records to the high standard obtained in the HSC and where possible to contribute to a single patient record. The report also recognised that it would be both equitable and necessary for the independent sector to contribute to costs of providing the NIECR system.My Department has received initial overtures from independent health and social care providers on access to information held on the NIECR. Initial consideration on technical, clinical governance, information governance, cyber security requirements and potential access costs are under way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health on what date will people under the age of 22 gain access to the drug Kuvan, which has been approved for use by the National Institute for Clinical Excellence for use against Phenylketonuria and is available elsewhere in the UK.
answer_text: Sapropterin (Kuvan) for treating hyperphenylalaninaemia in phenylketonuria was recommended for use by NICE through TA 729 on 22 September 2021 and has been available as commissioned therapy since that date.In late September 2021 the HSCB met with clinical and management team in the Belfast Trust to discuss the arrangements for implementation.  Trust colleagues highlighted some of the challenges with the delivery of this therapy associated primarily with a complex patient assessment pathway involving intensive monitoring arrangements and testing, and all of which needs to be completed to ensure that treatment is appropriate.Funding to allow recruitment of significant additional staffing infrastructure investment to enable full implementation of the service, has now been made available to the Trust. The Trust is now actively undertaking the assessment and testing of patients and will provide treatment where appropriate to patients up to the age of 22, in line with the NICE Recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether appropriate access to the Northern Ireland Electronic Care Record will be granted to all hospitals in Northern Ireland, in line with the Independent Neurology Inquiry; and (ii) from what date this access will be granted.
answer_text: All healthcare professionals employed by Health and Social Care organisations (HSC) have access to the information held in the NI Emergency Care Record (NIECR) appropriate to their role.Independent healthcare providers under contract to the HSC also have access to health and social care information, appropriate to the services provided.Access to personal, sensitive information held on HSC systems is strictly governed. Access to patient information held on the NIECR by organisations not engaged by the HSC will require those organisations to demonstrate their ability to adhere to the strict cyber security standards, clinical governance standards and information governance standards in force within the HSC. The Independent Neurology Inquiry highlighted the opportunities to enhance patient safety by encouraging those operating in the independent sector to maintain their records to the high standard obtained in the HSC and where possible to contribute to a single patient record. The report also recognised that it would be both equitable and necessary for the independent sector to contribute to costs of providing the NIECR system.My Department has received initial overtures from independent health and social care providers on access to information held on the NIECR. Initial consideration on technical, clinical governance, information governance, cyber security requirements and potential access costs are under way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the availability of drugs against Phenylketonuria in people of all ages, given the relatively high prevalence in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The treatment for (Phenylketonuria) PKU is a lifelong, very strict low protein diet with amino acid supplements. Many people with PKU only tolerate 4g or 5g of protein per day which is equivalent to the protein found in one slice of bread. Specialist low protein and protein substitute dietary products are required; specified products are supplied by GP prescription through Advisory Committee on Borderline Substance arrangements and can be found at the following link to the Northern Ireland Drug Tariff:https://hscbusiness.hscni.net/pdf/DT_PART_10a-2210.pdfSapropterin (Kuvan®) is the only drug licensed in the UK for treatment of PKU, but is subject to managed entry in Northern Ireland.  Managed Entry Decisions | NI Formulary (hscni.net). Sapropterin (Kuvan) for treating hyperphenylalaninaemia in phenylketonuria was recommended for use by NICE through TA 729 on 22 September 2021 and has been available as commissioned therapy since that dateIn late September 2021 the HSCB met with clinical and management team in the Belfast Trust to discuss the arrangements for implementation.  Trust colleagues highlighted some of the challenges with the delivery of this therapy associated primarily with a complex patient assessment pathway involving intensive monitoring arrangements and testing, and all of which needs to be completed to ensure that treatment is appropriate.Funding to allow recruitment of significant additional staffing infrastructure investment to enable full implementation of the service, has now been made available to the Trust. The Trust is now actively undertaking the assessment and testing of patients and will provide treatment where appropriate to patients up to the age of 22, in line with the NICE Recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the risks of not granting appropriate access to the Northern Ireland Electronic Care Record to all hospitals, given the involvement of both private and public facilities in the breaches of standards which led to the Independent Neurology Inquiry.
answer_text: All healthcare professionals employed by Health and Social Care organisations (HSC) have access to the information held in the NI Emergency Care Record (NIECR) appropriate to their role.Independent healthcare providers under contract to the HSC also have access to health and social care information, appropriate to the services provided.Access to personal, sensitive information held on HSC systems is strictly governed. Access to patient information held on the NIECR by organisations not engaged by the HSC will require those organisations to demonstrate their ability to adhere to the strict cyber security standards, clinical governance standards and information governance standards in force within the HSC. The Independent Neurology Inquiry highlighted the opportunities to enhance patient safety by encouraging those operating in the independent sector to maintain their records to the high standard obtained in the HSC and where possible to contribute to a single patient record. The report also recognised that it would be both equitable and necessary for the independent sector to contribute to costs of providing the NIECR system.My Department has received initial overtures from independent health and social care providers on access to information held on the NIECR. Initial consideration on technical, clinical governance, information governance, cyber security requirements and potential access costs are under way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the measures set out in the UK Government’s Our Plan for Patients policy paper, to outline what action he will take to correct pension scheme rules regarding inflation, where the difference in Consumer Price Inflation between the previous year and the current year is considered as pension growth.
answer_text: My Department is currently in discussions with DoF to address the issue of rapidly increasing inflation and its effect on pension growth. The scope of the technical adjustments to legislation which may be required to facilitate any adjustments to handling are now being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what support is available for women arriving in Northern Ireland seeking asylum who have experienced domestic violence and sexual abuse in their country of origin and/or on their journey to our country.
answer_text: Issues around immigration, including support for asylum seekers, are excepted matters here. Access to healthcare in Northern Ireland is provided under the Health and Personal Social Services Provision of Health Services to Persons Not Ordinarily Resident Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 (“the Regulations”). The Regulations provide that all asylum seekers are entitled to access publicly funded healthcare in Northern Ireland.Health and Social Care Trusts provide an adult protection response to adults who are subject to domestic and sexual abuse (including asylum seekers) who are unable to protect themselves without intervention and who are resident in the Trust area. This includes referrals to PSNI and to Multi-Agency Risk Assessment Conferences for high-risk cases. Children’s Gateway services will also be involved if there are children present. Local Domestic and Sexual Violence Partnerships work in partnership with agencies regarding prevention, early intervention and support for victims and children who have experienced domestic and sexual violence, including Women’s Aid, Nexus, the Rowan Sexual Assault Referral Centre and Rape Crisis Centre. Partnerships promote access to all services for those residing in their area regardless of race, religion or ethnic background and services are also available for those hard to reach or minority groups.  In the Belfast HSC Trust, the Specialist Health Visitor in the NI New Entrants Service (NINES) offers Routine Enquiry regarding domestic violence (whenever it is safe/possible to do so), as part of all family health assessments with pregnant women, postnatal women and movement in families.  All asylum seeker families are offered Health Visiting Services. In addition, NINES staff will enquire about service users’ safety and FGM, as this would be relevant to many service users on the caseload. Support is also considered from Sure Start, Family Support Hub and the Education Authority and, if there was an issue with trafficking, Flourish NI and Belfast and Lisburn Women’s Aid.Asylum seekers can represent some of the most vulnerable people in our society and it is important that those who are affected by domestic abuse reach out to seek help from support services. The regional Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline is also available to support victims and survivors of domestic abuse. The Helpline is a confidential, Freephone service, funded jointly by my Department, the Department of Justice and the Department for Communities. Fully trained Helpline staff are there to listen and offer emotional support 24 hours a day, 365 days per year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, following the measures set out in the UK Government’s Our Plan for Patients policy paper, to set out what action he will take in Northern Ireland to introduce partial retirement from the 1995 scheme.
answer_text: My Department is considering this issue. Any proposals will be subject to consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what action his Department is taking to address loss making and inadequate fees for dental treatments such as dentures, fillings and extractions, following evidence provided by BDA; and (ii) for his assessment of dentists having to provide specific treatments at a financial loss.
answer_text: I have considered and accepted the recommendations made by the Review Body on Doctors' and Dentists' Remuneration (DDRB) in their 50th report that would provide a 4.5% pay uplift to GDPs for 2022/23.  Whilst elements of the pay uplift are outwith the control of the Department of Health, particularly this year, I will do everything I can to implement as soon as possible.My Department is also currently exploring options to compensate for the most extremely impacted treatments that require lab work, to ensure that vital services can be maintained.Support being provided to dental practices will remain under ongoing review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to prioritising an independent Cost of Service review as an essential step to recalibrating dental fees that are fair, reasonable and workable at practice and practitioner level.
answer_text: The current focus of my Department is to rebuild General Dental Services so that the immediate oral health needs of the public are met in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic. In parallel the Dental Contract Reform Group is exploring options to ensure that Health Service dentistry is sustainable and delivers for both patients and the profession. Undertaking a Cost of Service Review will be an important part of any new approach.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to address morale among Health Service committed dental practitioners.
answer_text: The impact of the pandemic on the practice of dentistry has been particularly severe and I am very grateful for the commitment of local dentists who have continued to provide an essential services under very difficult circumstances. To help sustain General Dental Services (GDS) in the face of the impact of the pandemic, my Department provided £76m in financial support to independent General Dental Practitioners (GDPs) over the last 2 years.Further funding was provided during the first half of 2022/23, with Item of Service (IoS) payments being enhanced by 25% as part of the Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS), to reflect the ongoing pandemic pressures practices are facing. This support has been extended until the end of 2022/23 with a 10% enhancement on IoS payments. This is further investment, projected to be a further £2m, in a key service that has received significant support since the outset of the pandemic.  In addition, I have considered and accepted the recommendations made by the Review Body on Doctors' and Dentists' Remuneration (DDRB) in their 50th report that would provide a 4.5% pay uplift to GDPs for 2022/23.  Whilst elements of the pay uplift are outwith the control of the Department of Health, particularly this year, I will do everything I can to implement as soon as possible.My Department continues to engage with the dental industry via the British Dental Association (BDA).  In 2021 the Contract Reform Group was established to work with representatives from the BDA in a collaborative approach, to examine how to make improvements in the dental contract in the short and longer term to ensure that Health Service dentistry is sustainable and delivers for both patients and the profession.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his decision to cut interim dental funding through the Rebuilding Support Scheme from 25 per cent to 10 per cent enhancement from October can help address the dental access crisis in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The General Dental Services (GDS) Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS) was introduced in April 2022 to support dental practitioners with the impact of the pandemic. With the impact of the pandemic significantly reducing over the past few months and most restrictions now removed, the justification for ongoing support has also reduced. However, it is acknowledged that the impacts have not entirely receded with inefficiencies created by increased “Did Not Attends” and associated staff absences. It is for this reason that I committed to continuing support to the end of the financial year.To ensure that those patients most in need are able to obtain a dental appointment, any General Dental Practitioner signed up to the RSS is required to treat patients with an urgent or emergency dental condition based on clinical need, regardless of their registration status.In addition, any patient who has an urgent or emergency dental need, including those on pension credit or those entitled to free dental treatment, is able to make use of an Emergency Dental Clinic during weekends or Public Holidays. There the patient will be triaged remotely, and appropriate advice given or treatment arranged.To further help those in most need, on 3 October 2022, my Department commenced a scheme throughout Northern Ireland to provide access to non-registered patients who are experiencing an emergency or urgent dental condition.Support being provided to dental practices will remain under ongoing review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what the average waiting time was for (i) paediatric; and (ii) adult cystic fibrosis patients in Northern Ireland to be referred to (a) a clinical psychologist; and (b) a social worker, for each year since 2015.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely collect figures on waiting times for patients with cystic fibrosis to see a clinical psychologist or social worker; therefore, the information in this response has been provided by the Belfast Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust who are responsible for the service.Part a:To obtain the average waiting time for paediatric patients with cystic fibrosis to be referred to a clinical psychologist for each year since 2015 would require a manual trawl of records and could only be provided at disproportionate cost.The average waiting time for adult cystic fibrosis patients to access psychology services is not available; however, the longest waiting timeframe for each year are presented in Table 1.Table 1: Longest waiting times (weeks) for adults with cystic fibrosis to access psychology services in Northern Ireland (2015-2022)Year(January-December)Longest wait timeframe(weeks)20159 – 1320169 – 1320179 – 1320189 – 13201913 – 182020>522021>522022*13 – 18Source: Belfast HSC Trust.* Information for 2022 relates from January to September.Part b:To obtain the average waiting time for paediatric patients with cystic fibrosis to be referred to a social worker for each year since 2015 would require a manual trawl of records and could only be provided at disproportionate cost.For adults with cystic fibrosis, the social work service is part of a multi-disciplinary team service available to all patients who consent and does not require a waiting list. Therefore, information on waiting times is not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health on what date he will make a final decision on how to move forward with the Duty of Candour.
answer_text: Following the completion of a public consultation in 2021, the Duty of Candour Workstream provided its assessment to me in February 2022.  In response, the Department is continuing to develop the policy for a "Being Open Framework" for our health and social care system. Initial work on the “Being Open Framework” is focusing on the development of a pilot exercise in a Trust.The pilot exercise will initially focus on a diagnostic exercise to establish an understanding of the:perceptions of openness;barriers to openness;levers of power in relation to openness;enablers of openness.Interviews will be undertaken over the coming months to establish baseline data, providing evidence upon which to build a strategic plan for engagement.  The initial interviews will be held with the Trust Senior Management Team, Board and senior clinical leaders; senior clinicians; legal advisers and professional representative bodies.My officials are also carrying out further study on how a duty of candour might work in practice, including additional analysis of the impact of such a duty on the health and care service, both legally, and in workforce terms.  I should also point out my view that the introduction of any statutory duty of candour would be of such significance as to require referral to the Executive under the Ministerial Code. This will require the Assembly and Executive to be restored.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce support for anyone having a miscarriage, immediately upon the first miscarriage.
answer_text: There is already support available for women after a first miscarriage.  All Trusts in Northern Ireland have a bereavement midwife, early pregnancy unit and services available for women who have experienced miscarriage.Primary care services, counselling and other supports located in the community and voluntary sector are available to women who experience miscarriage. Furthermore, the National Institute of Health and Care Excellence Guideline NG 126, ‘Ectopic pregnancy and miscarriage: diagnosis and initial management’, was endorsed by the Department in June 2019. This guideline aims to improve how early pregnancy loss is diagnosed, and the support women are given, to limit the psychological impact of their loss.  It was updated in November 2021 to reflect new recommendations on the use of progesterone in cases of threatened miscarriage.  It is expected that the HSC will also implement these new recommendations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many and what percentage of (i) children; and (ii) adults, in Northern Ireland with cystic fibrosis saw (a) a clinical psychologist; and (b) a social worker at their annual review, for each year since 2015.
answer_text: My Department does not routinely collect figures on the number of patients with cystic fibrosis who see a clinical psychologist or social worker at their annual review; therefore, the information in this response has been provided by the Belfast Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust who are responsible for the service.Part a:To obtain figures for the number and percentage of children and adults who saw a clinical psychologist at their annual review since 2015 is not available as it would require a manual trawl of patient files.Part b: To provide the numbers and percentage of children who saw a social worker at their annual review each year from 2015 to August 2020 would require a manual trawl of files at disproportionate cost. Between 1 September 2020 to 31 March 2021, 71 (33%) children with cystic fibrosis were seen by a hospital social worker at their annual review. For year ending 31 March 2022, 106 (53%) children with cystic fibrosis were seen by a hospital social worker.Figures for the number and percentage of adults who had a social worker at their annual review each year since 2015 would require a manual trawl of a significant number of patient files at disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when a national pregnancy screening programme will be put in place.
answer_text: Currently in Northern Ireland, every pregnant woman is offered blood tests to screen for infectious diseases, including HIV; hepatitis B; syphilis, and susceptibility to rubella at their antenatal booking appointment (usually 10-12 weeks of pregnancy). This is as part of the Infectious Diseases in Pregnancy Screening programme – a managed population screening programme, working to meet nationally agreed standards as recommended by the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC). As part of routine antenatal care in Northern Ireland all pregnant women are also offered a detailed fetal anomaly scan, typically at 18-21 weeks of pregnancy.  This is to check for structural abnormalities and rare conditions, and to allow referral to fetal medicine services for further assessment and follow-up as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health following the merger of NHS England and NHS Improvement, what equivalent organisation exists within Health and Social Care NI to drive improvement.
answer_text: Health and Social Care (HSC) improvement is overseen by two strategic boards:The HSC Performance and Transformation Executive Board; andThe HSC Improvement and Transformation Advisory Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs in relation to Paragraphs 26 to 36 of the Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act 2011, what consideration he has given to amending the legislation particularly around fines and restrictions on the purchase of aerosols.
answer_text: The Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (CNE Act) gives an authorised officer of a district council the power to issue Fixed Penalty Notices of £80 in respect of certain graffiti and fly-posting offences under: Article 33 of the Road Traffic Regulation (NI) Order 1997 (interference with, or damage to, traffic signs), which involves only an act of defacement; Article 87(1) of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 (painting, making marks or displaying advertisements on roads); and Section 175(2) of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 (enforcement of advertisement control).	Article 18 of the Local Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) (NI) Order 1985 includes powers for district councils to tackle graffiti. The formal guidance associated with the Order stipulates that councils should use these powers in the first instance (as opposed to the provisions in the CNE Act) so that they can claim the costs of removal from the perpetrators and pursue offenders. Reflecting the Department for Communities’ policy responsibility in this area, this legislation was transferred from the former Department of the Environment to the Department for Communities (DfC) under the Departments (Transfer of Functions) Order (NI) 2016 (Schedule 5, Part 1).		Whilst DAERA is currently responsible for the overarching CNE Act itself, it would appear that policy responsibility for graffiti (including the sale of aerosols) resides with DfC, as per Article 18 of the Local Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) (NI) Order 1985. As such, I have not given consideration to amending the legislation in respect of fines and restrictions on the sale of aerosols, as I believe that this is more appropriately a policy matter for the DfC Minister.		DAERA will be seeking to transfer Part 4 of the CNE Act (which includes Section 37 – sale of aerosols to children) in its entirety (Sections 26 through to 39) to DfC, subject to the agreement of the DfC Minister, as part of a future Transfer of Functions Order.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department is taking to mitigate the impact of soaring expenses for dental practices, when fees to provide Health Service dentistry are fixed by his Department.
answer_text: I have considered and accepted the recommendations made by the Review Body on Doctors' and Dentists' Remuneration (DDRB) in their 50th report that would provide a 4.5% pay uplift to GDPs for 2022/23.  Whilst elements of the pay uplift are outwith the control of the Department of Health, particularly this year, I will do everything I can to implement as soon as possible.  This process will also address increases in expenses.Additional support is also being provided to the sector via the GDS Rebuilding Support Scheme, which has paid a 25% enhancement on all Items of Service undertaken in the first two quarters of 2022/23, and will continue with a 10% enhancement throughout Q3 and Q4.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on what date will the final version of the George Best Belfast City Airport Environmental Noise Directive Round 3 Action Plan 2019-24 be published.
answer_text: Competent Authorities have a duty to draw up, review and revise action plans under the Environmental Noise Regulations 2006. I can advise that in compliance with these noise regulations George Best Belfast City Airport has already submitted an Action Plan to my department which has been independently appraised by an external contractor.  My officials have and continue to work and engage with senior airport officials to agree this plan which once finalised will be published.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what advice he has sought from regulators over the capping of agency fees.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline what plans his Department has for a Women's Health Platform, similar to that in place in Scotland, to provide support and information to women suffering from low mood, anxiety, stress and signposting for help during menstruation.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline the amount spent on outpatient mental health treatment per patient, broken down by Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to match the five per cent pay rise offered to Agenda for Change staff in Scotland, in order to prevent losing staff to a neighbouring jurisdiction.
answer_text: I recently publicly acknowledged the need to ensure all staff are appropriately paid, when highlighting the financial pressures and budgetary uncertainty facing our health service. I must stress that our pay award decisions can also only be taken in the context of a wider public sector pay policy set by the Department of Finance. Any pay increase will also require to be fully funded by the Executive, given the financial outlook facing my Department in 2022/23.I further issued a statement on 20th July, in which I set out that I have considered the recommendations made by the NHSPRB and DDRB, and would be content to recommend acceptance of the relevant awards. In relation to the Agenda for Change pay award, this would maintain the previous commitments made by the Executive to maintain pay parity with England. The NHSPRB was not asked to make recommendations for Agenda for Change staff in Scotland, instead the Scottish Government decided to negotiate directly with Trades Unions. The offer of a 5% award was rejected. While AfC colleagues in Scotland may have slightly higher rates of pay, Scottish Government benefits from tax-raising powers, which sets the rate of Income Tax at 21% for earnings over £25,689 and at 41% for earnings over £43,663.Unlike my colleagues in other parts of the United Kingdom, I am unable to announce the immediate implementation of pay awards locally as Northern Ireland still does not have an agreed Executive Budget for 2022/23. Equally, I welcome and support the recent letter from the three devolved Finance Ministers asking the previous Chancellor to increase budgets to address pay and other pressures, including in the Health Service.The current cost of living crisis impacts on us all, yet Departments cannot address all issues in isolation – we need a functioning Executive to deliver appropriate support across our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the reasons behind the growing waiting list for treatment for prostate cancer.
answer_text: A number of factors have contributed to growing waits for prostate cancer.  We have seen an 9% increase in urology referrals for 21/22 when compared to pre-pandemic levels (2019/20).  Referrals for April-September 2022 suggest that 2022/23 will see further growth in the region of 3%.This increase in referrals has come at a time when services are still recovering from the impact of COVID-19.  The need to introduce enhanced infection prevention and control measures and to redeploy staff  had a profound impact across the pathway, from reductions in outpatient capacity, through to a reduction in day case and inpatient care due to reduced theatre access.  While steps were taken to minimise the impact of COVID (e.g. commissioning of additional capacity in the independent sector and regional prioritisation of surgery), the system was unable to deliver at pre-pandemic levels.  In addition, the system is working with workforce constraints due to vacancies in nursing and consultant vacancies across a number of specialities including urology and pathology.  Steps are being taken to actively recruit to vacancies and to invest in skills mix.  The DoH continues to work to expand urology capacity and funding has recently been announced to allow the development of 4 regional day surgery lists on the Omagh site. This capacity will allow additional Transurethral Resection of the Prostate (TURP) and trans urethral resection of bladder tumour (TURBT) procedures to be undertaken to support the equalisation of waits. Consultant Urologists work across all urology pathways therefore efficiencies in these diagnostis and treatment procedures will improve flow for patients overall, to include prostate cancer.While these developments combined with the gradual return to pre-COVID activity levels should improve the position over the coming months, ultimately, delivering on sustainable improvements in cancer waiting times in the face of increasing demand will require sustainable investment in workforce and diagnostic capacity in particular.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the planning and procurement for Malone Integrated College within the Capital Works Programme.
answer_text: The Education Authority is in the process of procuring Integrated Consultancy Teams (ICTs) for the major capital works that I announced on 21 March 2022. It is anticipated that the ICT contracts will be awarded in spring 2023. Following appointment, the ICTs will undertake technical feasibility studies, which will inform the business cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the planning and procurement for Hunterhouse College within the Capital Works Programme.
answer_text: The Education Authority is in the process of procuring Integrated Consultancy Teams (ICTs) for the major capital works that I announced on 21 March 2022. It is anticipated that the ICT contracts will be awarded in spring 2023. Following appointment, the ICTs will undertake technical feasibility studies, which will inform the business cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many patients currently registered with GPs are not funded; and (ii) how much this figure has risen by during the current financial year.
answer_text: All patients registered with GP Practices across Northern Ireland are funded via the Global Sum.  Global sum payments are based on various elements of the Carr-Hill formula that has been used as the basis of core funding for GMS practices across the United Kingdom since the inception of the GMS contract in 2004.  The distribution of the Global Sum payment to each GP Practice is based on the practices current registered patient list size, adjusted for a number of factors, including patient gender, patient age, rurality, market forces, unemployment levels, number of patients in a nursing home, number of new patient registrations etc.  The global sum amount is reviewed quarterly to account for changes to the practice’s registered patient population.The Global sum funding is currently £148m, based on the 21/22 value, an uplift for 2022/23 has not been agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans there are to improve cycling infrastructure on the route between the Ormeau Road and Ravenhill Road junction and Forestside Shopping Centre.
answer_text: My Department released details of the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan (BCNDP) 202231 in March 2022 following on from ‘Making Belfast an Active City – Belfast Cycling Network 2021’ which was published in June 2021.I can confirm that cycling infrastructure improvements on the route between the Ormeau Road and Ravenhill Road junction and Forestside Shopping Centre are identified within the long term plan of the BCNDP which can be viewed via the following link Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan 2022 – 31 (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk) .Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the planning and procurement for St Joseph's College within the Capital Works Programme.
answer_text: The Education Authority is in the process of procuring Integrated Consultancy Teams (ICTs) for the major capital works that I announced on 21 March 2022. It is anticipated that the ICT contracts will be awarded in spring 2023. Following appointment, the ICTs will undertake technical feasibility studies, which will inform the business cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when her Department will publish an assessment of the impact of the Child Poverty Strategy which was extended to May 2022.
answer_text: Work on the evaluation of the Child Poverty Strategy is ongoing.  Publication of an assessment of the impact of the Strategy would be subject to Executive approval and therefore cannot be published in the absence of a functioning Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how much money is assigned to Northern Ireland's Long Covid fund.
answer_text: The overall level of funding made available is £1m for 2021/22 and £2.5m for 2022/23.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans there are to refresh and emphasise the cycle lane markings along the A24 Ormeau Road and Saintfield Road.
answer_text: My officials recently inspected the cycle lane markings along the A24 Ormeau Road and Saintfield Road. They found the lane markings to be in reasonable condition, however it is acknowledged that the advanced stop lines and associated green boxes need to be refreshed.  A works order to provide new green surfacing and bicycle symbols will issue to the contractor as soon as the necessary resources are available.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work is ongoing to deliver care pathways for men living with prostate cancer.
answer_text: My Department continues to work to expand urology capacity and have recently approved the expansion of two new regional Transperineal biopsy lists and four flexible cystoscopy lists. This additional capacity will be used to help manage those red flag urology patients requiring diagnoses and treatment and this approach will benefit those patients requiring prostate cancer treatment.In addition, funding has recently been announced to allow the development of four regional day surgery lists on the Omagh site. This capacity will allow additional TURP (transurethral resection of the prostate) and TURBT (trans urethral resection of bladder tumour) procedures, a part of the bladder cancer pathway, to be undertaken to support the equalisation of waits.  Health and Social Care Trusts across NI provide a holistic needs assessment for all men requiring prostate cancer treatment at their first review and during the whole treatment pathway from investigation, diagnosis and follow up. Nurse led prostate cancer review clinics have been established in the Health and Social Care Trusts that provide prostate cancer care (from 2018 the Western Trust provided prostate care service to NHSCT patients), with prostate cancer patients assigned a key worker or clinical nurse specialist.  Nurse led clinics review prostate cancer patients undergoing active surveillance, watchful waiting, androgen deprivation and following radiotherapy, brachytherapy and robotic prostatectomy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on whether the rise in travel rates for domiciliary care workers will apply to all workers employed in Northern Ireland, or only to those employed by Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the continuing impact that the increase in living costs and fuel prices is having across Northern Ireland, particularly in relation to those who use their vehicles to deliver services as part of their day to day roles.As noted, I was recently pleased to be able to ensure a mileage rate increase for Agenda for Change (AfC) staff, including Trust employed domiciliary care workers and district nurses. I have approved a 50% increase to the secondary rate on annual mileage over 3,500 miles to bring it up to 30p per mile. This measure, which will be for an initial six month period, to be reviewed, will benefit those who are required to use their vehicles most frequently. However, this particular increase does not apply to the independent health and social care sector.Work has been ongoing to deliver additional funding to the independent sector, both in-year and on a recurrent basis, to try to alleviate the ongoing pressures on the system. The £23m funding package which I approved in November 2021, was intended to stabilise and enhance capacity within the independent domiciliary care and wider social care sector. While the Department cannot mandate pay or mileage rates offered by private employers, this investment has allowed providers to deliver enhanced rates of pay and improve other terms and conditions, which may also include increased mileage rates, at individual employers’ discretion. This additional funding secured an increase in the hours of domiciliary care delivered by the independent sector and helped to stabilise the care sector during a very difficult period. Despite a very challenging financial context I have ensured that funding was secured to ensure this uplift was carried through to the current year and has been made recurrent. I recognise that the independent sector continues to face significant difficulties. My officials continue to engage with the sector on ongoing challenges, including those arising from travel and the cost of living. However, given the financial pressures and budgetary uncertainty, there remains limited room for manoeuvre in terms of additional funding.   You may be aware that a public consultation on the Reform of Adult Social Care recently closed. Proposals contained within this consultation will see wider ranging changes to the delivery of social care, including the consideration of recognition and reward of staff. However, this reform package will require sustained additional investment and budgetary certainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6065/17-22, whether he intends to gather data relating to the estimated number of people eligible for pulmonary rehabilitation.
answer_text: Pulmonary rehabilitation is offered to people living with lung disease after a hospital admission and is provided by individual health and social care trusts in community, hospital and often online settings. It is not a mandatory treatment but it is part of standard after-care as recommended by National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). It should be noted that not all people living with lung disease may benefit from or be able to undertake the programme. Additionally, due to lifestyle factors and personal inclination it is very much up to the individual as to decide whether they want to partake in the programme. The important issue is that the offer will be made and that the service has the capacity to manage all referrals. Pulmonary rehabilitation is an element of community respiratory services of which a review was conducted in 2017/18 and no issues of concern were reported during this process. The admissions data that was supplied in 19/20 is helpful in giving a very rough estimate of how many people might be ‘eligible’ but the HSC does not record this exact data as standard. There are no current plans to review this position. Further review of lung disease services will be subject to the demands and priorities of the respiratory work programme and will be regularly reviewed as part of the joint DoH SPPG/PHA approach going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what is the trigger point for when Lateral Flow Tests for the Coronavirus would again be recommended for the entire population.
answer_text: Future decisions regarding arrangements for COVID-19 testing, including consideration of proportionate use of lateral flow testing, will be informed by a range of factors. These include for example the prevailing COVID-19 disease prevalence, trajectory and impact and the wider public health risk assessment. My Department continues to closely monitor Covid-19 surveillance and epidemiological data. All advice in relation to testing will continue to be kept under review and a range of measures may be necessary into the future should the current risk assessment change and in order to mitigate against severe illness for the most vulnerable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why there is currently a higher number of children on the Child Protection Register in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust area than prior to the Pandemic, when the number is relatively stable in other Trusts.
answer_text: During the COVID-19 pandemic, the Department established a weekly (now monthly) temporary data collection for children’s social care services in order to monitor and respond to emerging trends. This temporary data collection uses figures from the Quarterly Child Protection Statistics for quarter ending December 2019 as a pre-COVID comparison.  Whilst there are currently more children on the Child Protection Register in the Belfast Health & Social Care Trust than prior to the pandemic, when considered against historical annual trends it would appear that the pre-COVID figure is lower than average rather than the current figure being higher than average. This drop in the rate of child protection referrals was identified and drawn out in the Children’s Social Care Statistics for Northern Ireland 2019/20 annual publication. However, the reason for this drop is not known. The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust has the second lowest number of children on the Register across the region. The average number of monthly child protection referrals is the same as the pre-covid monthly average (46) and there has been a small increase in the monthly average of child protection registrations between the pre-covid period and August 2022 i.e. 27 compared to 21.The decision to register a child or young person on the Child Protection Register is a professional decision requiring multi-agency input and oversight through the Child Protection Case Conference process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what budget he has allocated during the current financial year to enable GPs to tackle Opioid Use Disorder in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The SPPG recognises that Opioid Dependency is a growing concern across the region as well as in the Belfast area. Some aspects of the totality of care for patients with opioid dependency are provided by GPs as 'core GMS'. However, the SPPG have also designed and commissioned a bespoke NI Local Enhanced Service to bolster care and understanding of patients dealing with these issues.The Substitute Prescribing NILES aims to provide a local, evidence based substitute prescribing service to all patients assessed as being in need of treatment for addiction as part of an overall package of treatment. The service was also designed to reduce the use of illicit drugs and the social, legal and medical problems associated with it including drug related deaths and overdose alongside providing information and advice to patients and nominated family members on methods of reducing harm and managing overdose.The funding for this NILES is allocated regionally, not by each Trust area. The total funding for this service across the region in 2022/23 is £100k.In 21/22 the Belfast GP Practice spend for the service was £17,598.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what proposals his Department and the Trusts have to revise how people who are hard of hearing are provided for in Emergency Departments.
answer_text: On 16 March this year, I launched a public consultation on the Review of Urgent & Emergency Care Services in Northern Ireland, which closed on 1 July.  The draft Equality Impact Assessment, published as part of the suite of consultation documents, recognises that service users with disabilities may have particular needs in relation to the way services are organised, and this includes information and communication support needs. The issue of accessibility for the deaf community has been raised at the public consultation workshops and this feedback will be considered as part of the implementation of the overall reform of urgent and emergency care services.  The proposed reforms aim to ensure that all citizens in Northern Ireland have equal access to safe urgent and emergency care services, tailored to their specific needs, at the right time and in the right place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the cost of hospital treatment for children diagnosed with autism during the financial year 2021/22.
answer_text: Children receive hospital treatment based on need, an autism diagnosis may or may not be recorded as part of these hospital records.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation and funding of the Cancer Strategy.
answer_text: Initial work on the implementation of the Cancer Strategy is progressing according to plan. Work has started on a number of actions, including scoping enhanced support for teenagers and young adults, developing new targets, enhancing clinical trials and developing a cancer workforce plan. I recently approved governance structures, which includes the creation of a Cancer Programme Board and a Cancer Steering Group to provide the strategic direction, oversight and ensure effective decision making for the implementation of the Cancer Strategy. I have also approved the allocation of £2.3m budget this year to fund the implementation of year 1 cancer strategy actions line with the funding plan to support implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what proposals he has to limit the promotion and consumption of junk food in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department’s ‘A Fitter Future for All’ obesity prevention strategic framework includes outcomes to advocate for further restrictions of advertising products with high fat, salt, and sugar (HFSS) to children and young people. The policy also advocates for Councils to consider and be supported to undertake Health Impact Assessments on relevant planning applications related to unhealthy take away or fast-food establishments near schools or other child-focussed settings. In addition, through the all-Island START campaign led by Safefood, consistent regional messages are provided to the public to raise public awareness of evidence-based healthy eating and reduce consumption of HFSS foods.Minimum Nutrition Standards for catering and vending in Health and Social Care settings have been recently reviewed and full implementation for staff and visitors is underway. Work is also being taken forward to promote the roll-out of these standards in local government and wider public sector.The Food Standards Agency lead on a number of food-related outcomes in ‘A Fitter Future for All’. In collaboration with retailers, they are working to consider consumers accessibility to healthier foods sold on promotion of foods high in fat, sugar, salt and calories. At the same time, they are working across the UK on Front Of Pack nutrition labelling. In addition, they are supporting voluntary calorie labelling and portion control in the out of home sector through the Calorie Wise scheme to help business and consumers make healthier and more informed food choices.Finally, my Department is currently developing a new obesity strategy to take over from ‘A Fitter Future for All’ in 2023, through co-design and in partnership with stakeholders across all sectors. This new policy intends to build on the outcomes and evidence already identified, to establish new short, medium and long-term outcomes to tackle the promotion and consumption of HFSS foods across the population, with the goal of making healthier choices easier choices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many fines have been issued under Part 4 of the Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, since the legislation came into operation.
answer_text: The Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 gives an authorised officer of a district council the power to issue Fixed Penalty Notices of £80 in respect of certain graffiti and fly-posting offences under: Article 33 of the Road Traffic Regulation (NI) Order 1997 (interference with, or damage to, traffic signs), which involves only an act of defacement; Article 87(1) of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 (painting, making marks or displaying advertisements on roads); and Section 175(2) of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 (enforcement of advertisement control). The Department does not hold information in relation to the number of FPNs issued by district councils in respect of these offences.	Article 18 of the Local Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) (NI) Order 1985 includes powers for district councils to tackle graffiti. The formal guidance stipulates that councils should use these powers in the first instance so that they can claim the costs of removal from the perpetrators and pursue offenders. Reflecting the Department for Communities’ policy responsibility in this area, this legislation was transferred from the former Department of the Environment to the Department for Communities under the Departments (Transfer of Functions) Order (NI) 2016 (Schedule 5, Part 1).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs given the prevalence of ongoing defacement of public property at locations such as railway bridges, what plans he has to amend the guidance to district councils under Paragraph 33 of the Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: The Clean Neighbourhoods & Environment Act (NI) 2011 strengthened the powers available to district councils to deal with a range of local issues including graffiti, posters and placards. Section 33 relates to guidance in respect of sections 31 and 32 (Defacement Removal Notices - DRNs), which I currently have no plans to amend. However, DRNs are not intended to be used to deal with new / ongoing cases of graffiti, rather to enable district councils to clear surfaces that were defaced by graffiti over time.		Article 18 of the Local Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) (NI) Order 1985 includes powers for district councils to tackle graffiti, placards and posters. The formal guidance stipulates that councils should use these powers in the first 	instance so that they can claim the costs of removal from the perpetrators and pursue offenders. Reflecting the Department for Communities’ policy responsibility for this area, this legislation was transferred from the former Department of the Environment to the Department for Communities under the Departments (Transfer of Functions) Order (NI) 2016 (Schedule 5, Part 1).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what budget there is for monitoring and assisting Councils with issuing penalty notices and the removal of graffiti from his Department’s property under Paragraphs 26 to 36 of the Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act 2011.
answer_text: My Department has powers under the Roads (NI) Order 1993 to remove graffiti, however, given the significant financial pressure my department is under this work is considered lower priority than other functions, many of which are road safety related, that are funded from my Department’s finite resource allocation. As such my Department would currently not be in a position to support Councils in their application of such measures which I understand they are empowered to carry out under the Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (NI) 2011.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance why the proposed cost for the North-South Glider route is in excess, as of May 2022, of the initially proposed cost at the time of the Belfast City Deal.
answer_text: The initial cost estimate, submitted to Belfast Region City Deal in September 2018, was based on very early assumptions. This estimate was subsequently revised during the development of the interim Outline Business Case. The estimate is currently being reviewed as part of the finalisation of the final Outline Business Case.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will introduce official measurements of the number of miscarriages taking place across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As I have previously advised the member, the recording of miscarriage is a complex area.  There is no central recording of pregnancy loss numbers or rates.  Estimates are often used and applied to population figures to provide a population estimate as not all pregnancy losses will be known to healthcare providers. Pregnancy loss may be recorded on a number of information systems including Trust Patient Administration Systems (PAS), GP clinical systems and NIMATS (Northern Ireland Maternity Systems).  The Trust PAS will capture people who require hospital admission for management of pregnancy loss or ectopic pregnancy.  Early Pregnancy Clinics will record attendance at a clinic but not the outcomes and not all those who attend will have a pregnancy loss confirmed. GP clinical information systems will record pregnancy loss – but only if the GP knows about it and this may not always be the case. Any statistic could not therefore be reflective of the true number of miscarriages occurring and consequently I have no plans to introduce any official measurements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on a target date for making Northern Ireland tobacco-free.
answer_text: My Department’s 10-year Tobacco Control Strategy for Northern Ireland has the overall aim of a tobacco-free society. An end review of the strategy is currently underway and the Department will consider the appropriateness of a tobacco-free target date for Northern Ireland in the development of any future strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration is being given to a full Mesh Surgery Reimbursement Fund.
answer_text: There are no plans at this time to establish a full Mesh Surgery Reimbursement Fund in Northern Ireland. My Department’s position remains that patients requiring full mesh removal surgery have access to local services. Consultants at the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust are trained in full mesh removal. In exceptional circumstances where local management is not appropriate, patients may be offered access specialist treatment options through the extra-contractual referral process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the advice informing the current decision not to offer fourth/booster vaccines against the Coronavirus to those aged under 50.
answer_text: The COVID-19 vaccination programme in NI and across the rest of the UK has been guided by the advice and recommendations of the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI), an independent expert advisory committee.  JCVI consider all the available scientific evidence, both published and unpublished, before offering their advice to the four UK Health Ministers.As we transition from a period of pandemic emergency response to pandemic recovery, the focus of the vaccination programme will increasingly be on protecting those in society who continue to be more at risk of severe COVID-19. Based on JCVI advice, a booster dose will be offered from September to all residents and staff in care homes, everyone aged 50 years and over and those aged 5 to 49 years of age who are in a clinical risk group, including all pregnant women.  In addition, a booster will also be available for frontline health and social care staff, carers aged 16-49 years of age as well those aged 5-49 years of age who are the household contacts of people who are immunosuppressed.The primary objective for the autumn booster programme is to boost immunity in those considered by JCVI to be at higher risk from serious illness if they catch COVID-19. The autumn booster dose will optimise protection against severe COVID-19, and is expected to help reduce their risk of hospitalisation and death from COVID-19 during the winter of 2022/23.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there will be any differentiation in eligibility for flu vaccines in Northern Ireland versus the rest of the UK for the 2022/23 vaccination programme.
answer_text: Eligible cohorts for the flu vaccination programme are based on the advice of the Joint Committee on Vaccinations and Immunisations (JCVI). The programme aims to provide direct protection to those who are at higher risk of influenza associated morbidity and mortality and to reduce transmission to all age groups through the vaccination of children. This year vaccination will also be offered to those aged 50 to 64 years.Based on JCVI advice, each UK nation decides what cohorts to vaccinate in the flu programme.  During 2022/23, the other UK nations will vaccinate similar cohorts to Northern Ireland. Their flu policy letters can be found via the following links:Wales: https://gov.wales/national-influenza-immunisation-programme-2022-2023-whc202216England: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/national-flu-immunisation-programme-plan/national-flu-immunisation-programme-2022-to-2023-letterScotland: https://www.sehd.scot.nhs.uk/cmo/CMO(2022)27.pdfEligibility for the Health and Social Care flu vaccine programme in 2022/23 is as follows:all preschool children aged 2 to 4 years on 1 September 2022all primary and secondary (up to and including year 12) school children those aged six months to under 65 years in clinical risk groups (as defined by the influenza chapter in ‘Immunisation against infectious disease’ (the ‘Green Book’)those aged 50 years and over on 31 March 2023 (i.e. born on or before 31 March 1973)pregnant womenthose in long-stay residential care homescarersclose contacts of immunocompromised individualsfrontline health and social care workers (HSCWs) employed by:Health and Social Care Trusts including NIAScommunity HSC providers including GP practices, pharmacies, dentists, optometristsregistered independent sector residential care or nursing homeregistered domiciliary care providersvoluntary managed hospice providers

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what work has been carried out by his Department on a Palliative Care Strategy.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of quality palliative care in supporting people with life-limiting conditions to live well and achieve the best quality of life as their illness progresses.  The Palliative Care in Partnership programme continues to oversee the implementation of the Department’s ‘Living Matters:Dying Matters palliative and end of life care strategy for adults’ and the development and improvement of palliative and end of life care across the region.In terms of any new palliative care strategy, it would be appropriate to wait for the Programme for Government process to be progressed, as this will provide the strategic driver for the future development of palliative and end of life care.  In the meantime, a number of initiatives are being progressed which I believe will further improve services and support in relation to both palliative care and bereavement.  These include my Department’s new Cancer Strategy, the work being taken forward to develop the Advance Care Planning policy for adults and the work of the new regional Bereavement Network to enhance bereavement support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6065/17-22, whether any data is now available with regard to how many patients have been referred to pulmonary rehabilitation programmes by GPs.
answer_text: Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) in the Department has been collating data on the total number of referrals to pulmonary rehabilitation services as part of Post Covid Syndrome Services, established across each trust. The overall number of referrals to wider pulmonary rehabilitation services unrelated to Covid is not collected centrally.Between April 2021 and the 31 July 2022, there was a total of 683 referrals to pulmonary rehabilitation services as part of Post Covid Syndrome Services across Northern Ireland, of which, 132 were reported as coming from General Practitioners. Please note that the Western Trust has not reported to the Department for this period.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what budget has been allocated during the current financial year to GP services for people without a permanent address.
answer_text: As per the GMS contract, the SPPG provide funding to support practices that provide GMS care to patients deemed to be ‘temporary residents’.GP Practices may, if their list is open, accept a person as a temporary resident if they are satisfied that the person is temporarily resident away from his normal place of residence and is not being provided with essential services (or their equivalent) under any other arrangement in the place where he is temporarily residing.A person shall be regarded as temporarily resident in a place if, when they arrive in that place, they intend to stay there for more than 24 hours but not more than three months. The regional budget set for GP Practices to provide services to Temporary Patients in 2022/23 is £722k.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the Department’s plans for managing pressures in the health and social care system, emerging from the likely elevated risk of influenza over the 2022/23 winter period.
answer_text: Trusts have submitted Service Delivery Plans for 2022/23 which require them to return to pre pandemic levels of service based on what they delivered on a month by month basis in 2019/20. These levels of activity would have been provided throughout the Winter period and so factor in seasonal pressures such as influenza, other unscheduled pressures and focusing on maximising delivery of elective care. The high impact indicators that the Trusts are being monitored against are consistent with the approach taken by NHS England to manage seasonal pressures and include measures to reduce hospital occupancy, ensure timely discharge, provide better support for people at home, improve community response for urgent and emergency care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the timescale under which he will implement the Review of General Surgery in Northern Ireland, published on 30 June 2022.
To ask the Minister of Health what action is currently being taken by his Department to identify cases of Crohn's disease and Colitis.
answer_text: Patients with symptoms which are indicative to Crohn’s disease or Colitis can be referred by their GP or a doctor in secondary care (including Emergency Departments to any Health & Social Care Trust in Northern Ireland.  These patients will be triaged and seen by a consultant and, subsequently, appropriate diagnostic procedures will be arranged and carried out.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-Cell Therapy for multiple myeloma will become operational in Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister of Health when a departmental lead on inflammatory bowel disease will be appointed.
answer_text: Crohns and Colitis are forms of Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) which is managed mostly under the speciality of Gastroenterology but patients may also be referred from General Medicine or General Surgery.  Regional commissioning through the Department of Health serves as the means of ensuring a consistent approach to prioritisation and delivery across Trusts and primary care, in line with relevant clinical standards and population needs. There is already a close network across Primary Care, Voluntary groups, HSC Trusts and specialities to manage the care of patients. Regional commissioning through the Department of Health serves as the means of ensuring a consistent approach to prioritisation and delivery across all Trusts and primary care, in line with relevant clinical standards and population needs. Diagnosis is supported by regionally agreed primary care pathways.  Once diagnosed, patients are cared for by specialists including clinicians and IBD nurses in each Trust area.  A number of Trusts have appointed pharmacy support for IBD patients who attend in their Emergency Department.  There is also a regional IBD interest group which shares good practice and learning across the region. The Department of Health in recent years has increased funding on an annual basis to ensure that all IBD patients, assessed as benefiting from NICE approved biologic technologies, receive treatment regardless of where they live. Patients across NI are able access these medicines within a waiting time of 13 weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given rising food costs, what considerations are provided in the welfare benefits system to people living with medical conditions, such as Cystic Fibrosis, which require the person specifically to eat a high calorie diet.
answer_text: I have written to Thérèse Coffey MP, Secretary of State for Work and Pensions requesting an uplift to benefits in line with inflation.I am deeply frustrated that the lack of a functioning Executive and agreed budget prevents any further cost of living support measures being introduced. However, I remain determined that my Department will continue to deliver support for those who are in need and I have tasked my officials to fully explore and expedite all options to address both the immediate needs of, and medium to longer term solutions to, the cost of living crisis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what financial assistance his Department is able to provide to care providers who are unable to recruit or retain staff because of rising fuel and travel costs.
answer_text: The support package of up to £23m which I announced in November 2021 allowed independent domiciliary care providers to improve recruitment, employee retention, and Terms and Conditions for staff. The Department cannot mandate what levels of pay, or terms and conditions private employers should offer, including in regards to mileage or travel costs. However this investment has allowed providers to deliver enhanced rates of pay and improve other terms and conditions, which may also include increased mileage rates, at individual employers’ discretion. Despite a very challenging financial climate I have ensured that sufficient funding was provided to Trusts to ensure this uplift was carried through to the current year and made recurrent.I am acutely aware of the continuing impact that the increase in living costs and fuel prices is having across Northern Ireland, and particularly in relation to those who use their vehicles to deliver services as part of their day to day roles, whether that be in the statutory or the independent sector.I have tasked officials to continue engaging with the independent sector on ongoing challenges including the travel and other cost of living pressures facing staff. However, given the financial pressures and budgetary uncertainty, the room for manoeuvre in terms of additional funding is limited.As you may be aware, a public consultation recently closed which sought views relating to long term proposals on the Reform of Adult Social Care. Whilst the proposals on the Reform of Adult Social Care will see wide ranging changes to the delivery of social care, the reform package will require sustained additional investment and budgetary certainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what work was carried out by her Department to facilitate collaboration between Forge Integrated Primary School, Knockbreda and St. Bernard’s Primary School, South Belfast, on future educational provision at the former Knockbreda High School site.
answer_text: The new design proposal for the Forge Integrated primary school on the former Knockbreda High School site is progressing well, with the design team preparing to make a full planning application in summer 2022. Forge Integrated Primary School, Knockbreda and St. Bernard’s Primary School, have experience of collaborative working though Shared Education, but not directly with one another. With both schools involved in Shared Education partnerships the schools recognise the benefits of collaborative working that not only helps to improve relations between communities, but also delivers a range of educational benefits, and the sharing of ideas and good practice between education providers. The EA has advised that in the 2018/19 school year there were initial conversations around accessibility for all when the site was being discussed as this was an issue that was raised by the other schools.  The EA has also advised that there were some early conversations with the Principal of Forge Integrated Primary School and the Authority’s Infrastructure and Capital Development Division around a Shared Campus “lite” option, but COVID-19 put a halt on conversation.  There is no current area planning activity in respect of Forge Integrated Primary School, Knockbreda and St. Bernard’s Primary School, South Belfast.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the plan for the next stage of Coronavirus booster vaccines, with specific regard to carers for clinically vulnerable people.
answer_text: The COVID-19 vaccination programme in the UK has been guided by the advice and recommendations of the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI), an independent expert advisory committee.  JCVI consider all the available scientific evidence, both published and unpublished, before offering their advice to the four UK Health Ministers.  Interim guidance from JCVI in relation to an autumn programme has been published and this guidance recommends that a booster vaccine should be offered to –Residents in a care home for older adults and staffFrontline health and social care workers All those aged 65 and overAdults aged 16 to 64 who are in a clinical risk groupA final JCVI statement is expected shortly.  I fully recognise the important and vital role carers provide looking after vulnerable members of society and with this in mind my Department has written to JCVI encouraging them to look favourably on including carers in their final statement regarding the autumn booster.In the meantime the Public Health Agency are developing plans for the implementation of the autumn booster in Northern Ireland and these will be announced following the release of JCVI’s final statement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is giving consideration to specific financial support for caring providers operating in rural areas, where the comparative impact of rising fuel costs is greater.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the continuing impact that the increase in living costs and fuel prices is having across Northern Ireland, and particularly in relation to those who use their vehicles to deliver services in rural areas as part of their day to day roles.I was pleased to recently be able to announce a mileage rate increase for Agenda for Change (AfC) staff, including Trust employed domiciliary care workers and district nurses, however this particular increase does not apply to the independent health and social care sector. Unlike the statutory sector, there is no regional rate for mileage in regards to independent sector providers, which is part of the terms and conditions offered by individual employers. The Department cannot mandate what levels of pay, or terms and conditions private employers should offer, including in regards to mileage or travel costs.However, you will be aware that on 24 November I announced a support package of up to £23m, which allowed independent domiciliary care providers to improve recruitment, employee retention, and Terms and Conditions for staff. This investment has already allowed providers to deliver enhanced rates of pay and improve other terms and conditions, which may also include increased mileage rates, at individual employers’ discretion. Despite a very challenging financial climate I have ensured that sufficient funding was provided to Trusts to ensure this uplift was carried through to the current year and made recurrent.I have tasked officials to continue engaging with the independent sector on ongoing challenges including the travel and other cost of living pressures facing staff. However, given the financial pressures and budgetary uncertainty, the room for manoeuvre in terms of additional funding is limited.Proposals currently out for consultation on the Reform of Adult Social Care will see wider ranging changes to the delivery of social care. However, this reform package will require sustained additional investment and budgetary certainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what funding has been allocated for capital works at St Bernard’s Primary School, South Belfast, since the start of the 2003/4 financial year.
answer_text: In adherence to records management data retention policies, my Department is only required to retain financial records for a period of six years following the end of a full financial year.  Therefore the information provided below is for the financial years 2015-16 to 2021-22. Please also note that my Department does not allocate capital funding to individual school budgets, rather it grant-aids capital works it has approved at non-controlled schools.The total amount of accrual expenditure from the 1 April 2015 to the 31 March 2022 relating to capital works at St Bernard’s Primary School is £138,885.91. This includes expenditure incurred by the Education Authority in relation to capital furniture and equipment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any concerns had been raised with his Department about the Logan Wellbeing and Medical clinic prior to the BBC Spotlight programme on 29 March 2022.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department will take to ensure that flags and posters placed on Departmental property are flown in line with legislation and with the Code of Practice on the Respectful Display of Flags as proposed in the final report of the Commission on Flags, Identity, Culture and Tradition.
answer_text: My Department does not permit the attachment of flags to its property.  Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 makes it an offence to attach unauthorised signs or advertisements to structures such as lamp posts. One of my Department’s primary considerations is the safety of the public and where unauthorised flags or attachments pose a risk to road users, my Department will seek to remove that hazard. Where there is no such danger, my Department will liaise closely with other key stakeholders and seek to provide a solution.I note the commitments to respect for different cultures and identities contained in the New Decade New Approach.
