Finding a sustainable solution to the ongoing problem of illegally erected flags and emblems requires leadership and a united approach. I can assure you that I am committed to working with Executive colleagues and all stakeholders to deal with this issue comprehensively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what involvement the Department and its agencies have had into the investigation of the Logan Wellbeing and Medical clinic, following the BBC Spotlight programme on 29 March 2022.
To ask the Minister of Health what powers the Department has to regulate the practices of private clinics offering medical and quasi medical treatments.
answer_text: The Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) is responsible for the registration and inspection of independent clinics in accordance with the Independent Health Care Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005.The Regulations require the establishment to have a written statement of purpose and patient’s guide and to have arrangements for regular review. The registered provider/organisation and manager must be able to demonstrate that they are fit to perform their responsibilities and possess the training and skills necessary for this purpose. The Regulations also address the quality of treatment and service provision, the care and welfare of patients, the numbers, qualification and experience of staff and their fitness to perform their work, professional practice, records, staff views, complaints, fitness of premises, financial viability and notification of events.Terms and conditions of employment between individuals working in these areas and their employers would be private.  Health Professionals such as Doctors, Dentist, Nurses working in these areas are subject to their regulatory bodies for example the General Medical Council, General Dental Council and Nursing and Midwifery Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action the Department intends to take to regulate and protect therapists working in businesses which claim to be medical clinics.
answer_text: The Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) is responsible for the registration and inspection of independent clinics in accordance with the Independent Health Care Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005.The Regulations require the establishment to have a written statement of purpose and patient’s guide and to have arrangements for regular review. The registered provider/organisation and manager must be able to demonstrate that they are fit to perform their responsibilities and possess the training and skills necessary for this purpose. The Regulations also address the quality of treatment and service provision, the care and welfare of patients, the numbers, qualification and experience of staff and their fitness to perform their work, professional practice, records, staff views, complaints, fitness of premises, financial viability and notification of events.Terms and conditions of employment between individuals working in these areas and their employers would be private.  Health Professionals such as Doctors, Dentist, Nurses working in these areas are subject to their regulatory bodies for example the General Medical Council, General Dental Council and Nursing and Midwifery Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assistance is in place for former clients of Logan Healthcare to obtain copies of their treatment records from the clinic, including records of IV drips and prescription medication administered.
answer_text: Under Article 15 of UK GDPR legislation individuals have the right to access and receive a copy of their personal data held by private companies. This includes medical records held by private healthcare providers. Such a requests is commonly referred to as a Subject Access Request (SAR). Compliance with such requests is regulated by the Information Commissioner’s Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what provision or treatment pathways have been put in place for former clients of Logan Healthcare to receive counselling and mental health support following the BBC Spotlight programme on 29 March 2022.

To ask the Minister of Health on how many occasions in the last 12 months, have facilities under the responsibility of Health and Social Care Trusts engaged the Police Service of Northern Ireland in response to a child in their care being suspected of having run away or gone missing.
To ask the Minister of Health what action he is taking to improve mental well-being among GPs and those working in multi-disciplinary teams in primary care.
answer_text: I am very aware of the hard work and dedication of all those in our health service who provide services day by day often in the face of significant challenges.  It is very important that those who care for us also receive appropriate support to aid their own mental wellbeing.  Those working in primary care service delivery can access direct psychological support via occupational health services.  These services are provided via the HSC Trusts and can be accessed via self-referral/Departmental (SPPG) referral.	In addition, the individual professions delivering care in general practice settings may access support via their own professional bodies.  For example, the BMA provides wellbeing support services which include counselling and peer support services and similarly the BASW provides a Social Work Professional Support Service which offers peer to peer support to talk through issues and develop their career. 	Support is also available to GPs through the Northern Ireland General Practitioner Mentoring Scheme which aims to help GPs to address challenges they are facing. All of the mentors in the scheme are GPs with a broad range of experience. Further details of this scheme are available at: https://www.nimdta.gov.uk/general-practice/gp-mentoring/ .  At a longer term strategic level, my Department also continues, subject to available funding, to build upon the reforms that are ongoing to support primary care and general practice.   By working to create a wider skills mix profile, we can help to support practitioners in delivering care and also ensure that patients are receiving care from the most appropriate professional.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the reasons for withdrawing subjects at Stranmillis University College and focusing life-long learning courses on subjects likely to lead to employability, to the exclusion of those promoting well-being.
answer_text: My Department has no role in the direct funding, nor setting, of the annual progamme of life long learning courses within Stranmillis University College.  However, in response to your question I asked my officials to seek a response from the College. Their position is set out below:		Lifelong learning courses at Stranmillis University College consist of a broad range of short, non-examined, full-cost-recovery programmes. These programmes are not funded by DfE and, in order to be offered, the College must determine if they are financially viable through student fee income. 		Every year, the entire portfolio of programmes is reviewed, by the College, in preparation for the forthcoming academic year and relevant prospectus. Lifelong learning programmes will be introduced and removed as part of this annual process based on course viability as well as availability of teaching staff.		In preparation for the coming year, existing Lifelong Learning Tutors have been contacted and proposals for courses in the 2022-2023 academic year are being sought. In addition, a new model of delivery is  being piloted which seeks to increase the number of health and wellbeing courses being offered on the College campus next year, by allowing tutors/instructors to hire out rooms in the College. The College hopes that this model will offer greater flexibility for tutors, whilst also allowing the College to continue promoting the use of its facilities and campus to the benefit of the wider community. 		In responding to the 10x Vision, the College is currently reviewing its provision to identify if it is possible to further align to the 10x Vision.  Any new provision emerging from this review will aim to complement existing provision, which continues to be viable and relevant. 		The Lifelong Learning prospectus for this coming year has not yet been published and the final provision has not yet been agreed by College management.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to provide a written statement on plans and advice for managing COVID-19 during the autumn and winter.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether plans for the North and South Belfast Glider route recognises the desirability for the route to operate directly from City Hall and along Royal Avenue as part of a green urban bus corridor, rather than more peripheral routes.
answer_text: Route considerations are at an advanced stage with the Department seeking to ensure BRT2 provides an accessible and connected Glider service within the core of the city centre.Shortlisted route options published for public consultation propose operation along Donegall Place and Royal Avenue. However, in taking decisions on the preferred option, consideration will be given to alignment of the route with A Bolder Vision for Belfast and balancing public transport accessibility with the creation of a people-focussed Civic Spine.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he intends to extend funding for the Sustrans' Active School Travel Programme into the 2022/23 academic year.
answer_text: Officials are currently considering options for the Active School Travel Programme along with the Public Health Agency and I expect to receive an update imminently.I fully support the benefits of the Programme in setting early habits of active travel in young people that will contribute into healthy outcomes in their adult lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients (i) have attended; and (ii) are on the waiting list for, their first appointment following referral to a long COVID clinic.
answer_text: Information on the number of people who have attended or are on the waiting list for their first appointment at a long COVID clinic is not available centrally in the Department.  The following information has been provided by the Health and Social Care Trusts.Northern and Western Health and Social Care Trusts were contacted for input but were unable to provide a response within the required timeframe.Table 1: Number of patients who have attended their first appointment at a long COVID clinicHSC TrustNumber of attendancesBelfast189South Eastern264Southern79Source: HSC TrustsTable 2: Number of patients waiting for their first appointment at a long COVID clinicHSC TrustNumber of patients waitingBelfast133South Eastern202Southern156Source: HSC Trusts

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the commissioning of abortion services.
answer_text: My Department has developed a draft commissioning specification to support the implementation of abortion services in Northern Ireland, in line with the requirements of the Abortion Regulations (No.2) 2020.  I am currently awaiting legal advice on the implications of the recently introduced Abortion (NI) Regulations 2022 and updated Directions before considering the next steps regarding the commissioning of services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the waiting times for a patient to receive their first appointment following referral to a long COVID clinic.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on improved travel allowances for District Nurses and other health professionals who require a car to perform their duties.
To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 818/22-27, why no eligible patients have yet had access to Kuvan, even on a trial basis.
answer_text: Sapropterin (Kuvan) for treating hyperphenylalaninaemia in phenylketonuria was recommended for use by NICE through TA729 on 22 September 2021 and has been available as commissioned therapy since that date. The Health and Social Care Board (HSCB) met with clinical and management team in the Belfast Trust on 27 September 2021 to discuss the arrangements for implementation.  At this time Trust colleagues set out some of the challenges with the delivery of this therapy associated primarily with a complex patient assessment pathway involving intensive monitoring arrangements and testing – all of which needs to be completed to ensure the treatment is appropriate. It was understood that as a result of this, implementation would take considerably longer and would require significant investment in staffing infrastructure to fully deliver the service.  Opportunities to reduce the timeline for progress were explored including additional resourcing or developing links with other tertiary GB centres to provide support were considered.  The Trust however advised that the timeline for implementation was broadly similar to other GB services and had no concerns that Northern Ireland was in any way different from other units taking forward the sapropterin pathway. The HSCB explicitly confirmed at that time that as a NICE TA recommended therapy, implementation would be fully supported by the commissioner. There is therefore no impediment to progress in relation to resourcing from a commissioning perspective.The Trust was required to submit a business case to inform the level of resourcing required for the staffing components, which are in addition to the drug costs.  This was received by the Department’s Strategic Performance and Performance Group (SPPG) on 6 June 2022 and an allocation for the full costs as requested was issued on 23 June 2022.The SPPG (previously HSCB) has received five individual requests for the funding of sapropterin (Kuvan) for the treatment of hyperphenylalaninaemia in phenylketonuria between October 2020 and May 2022. All five requests were approved for funding via the Individual Funding Request (IFR) process or Cost per Case (CPC) process.  However it should be noted that the SPPG does not hold information on whether the individuals subsequently received treatment. The treatment continues to be made available on a cost-per-case basis should a requesting clinician make application to the SPPG demonstrating that the relevant criteria have been met.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) whether there will be an Engage III Programme in the 2022-23 academic year; and (ii) when will school principals be advised to allow for appropriate planning and adjustments.
answer_text: The Engage programme has been extremely successful to date, with more than £24 million provided to 1,700 educational settings this academic year.  Engage has ensured that our children and young people have been provided with a range of support to reduce the impact which the Covid-19 pandemic has had on their educational experience and learning. I issued a Direction to my officials for the Engage programme to continue to December 2022.  The Department of Finance approved that Direction on 6 July 2022, and I am delighted that I have been able to allocate £10million to continue the programme until the end of December 2022.   I have recently written to schools and settings to inform them.It is my intention that the programme will continue until the end of the academic year. However, as this remains subject to the necessary funding being made available, settings are not able to commit to expenditure beyond December 2022.  I will update schools on the budget position for the rest of the academic year as soon as I am in a position to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the last review of the relocation of, or additional, halts on current railway lines was carried out.
answer_text: A recent review of the railway network in 2021 did not indicate a requirement for any additional new halts on the existing rail network in the short/medium term. Translink is however progressing with developing a business case for a new halt at Lisburn West (Knockmore) which was identified in a previous study. Translink is currently undertaking a feasibility study for potential additional railway halts in the North West. This will consider the demand forecast and implications for the wider network operations and timetable.  It is hoped to conclude this initial study by end of the summer 2022.With the Climate Act now in place the importance of an accessible rail network comes into new focus and I believe that will include the need for new rail links across the island with new rail halts to ensure as many people as possible can easily access a rail network that meets their everyday work and leisure needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans he has to appoint a small businesses champion.
answer_text: There are no plans to make such an appointment at present. However, I recognise the significance of small businesses to the Northern Ireland economy. This is reflected in the regular engagement I and my Department have with the Federation of Small Businesses and other organisations that represent small businesses.	My officials have also engaged with the UK Small Business Commissioner, who has a particular role in tackling the problem of late payments to small businesses and is very willing to champion businesses in Northern Ireland. They have discussed promoting her work in championing the rights of small businesses in our economy, and encouraging the development of digital education amongst small business owners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his plans for legislation to promote financial assistance to farmers based on sustainable land management.
answer_text: My officials have already started work on scoping the necessary powers needed to enact all 54 decisions on future agricultural support I announced in March this year.  I envisage a number of Secondary Legislation Regulations will be required to go forward with individual measures aimed at promoting and incentivising sustainable land management on farms.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the scale of new housing developments, what further options are being looked at to improve public transport along the Newtownbreda-Carryduff corridor.
answer_text: In order to accommodate any additional Park and Ride demand on the Saintfield Road corridor, my officials are currently in the process of acquiring land from the Department of Health to provide an additional 350 parking spaces at the Cairnshill site.My Department will also be bringing forward the Belfast Metropolitan Transport Plan which will consider the wider public transport availability and provision on this corridor in the context of future development proposals. As part of this study, further discussion and assessment will be undertaken to examine Public Transport on the Newtownbreda – Carryduff corridor. Translink recognise that they need to review this provision against the expected growth in demand from the new developments. They have started the process of planning their service offering and will update local political representatives and the community as this progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Review of Active Travel Pop-Up Cycle Lanes will be completed.
answer_text: A review into the pop-up cycle lanes was completed last year and findings published on the Department’s website in December 2021.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-active-travel-pop-cycle-lanes

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Review of Active Travel Pop-Up Cycle Lanes, including the Dublin Road, Belfast.
answer_text: The review into the pop-up cycle lanes was completed last year and published in December.  The main findings of the review was that the cycle lanes would be maintained while also creating an additional bus lane along the Dublin Road to further improve sustainable travel options, subject to consultation and legislative processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progress of the north-south Glider route and the timescale for the selection of the final route.
answer_text: Development of an outline business case, which will help inform selection of the preferred route, is well advanced. At this stage, it is too early to provide a timeline for the preferred route announcement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what representations she is making to the UK Home Office concerning improved accommodation for asylum seekers.
answer_text: Issues around immigration, including accommodation and support for asylum seekers, are excepted matters and do not fall within my Department’s remit.  The Home Office is the operational and policy lead on these matters.I have written to the Permanent Secretary within the Executive Office to raise concerns that have been raised with me, and enquire about the approach currently being adopted by Mears, the Home Office’s local contractor, to the sourcing of appropriate accommodation for asylum seekers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 199/22-27, how he intends to match the £150 support package for families announced by the Chancellor of the Exchequer in February 2022, currently being provided in England by Council Tax rebates.
answer_text: The Executive received a Barnett consequential of £100 million in relation to the Council Tax rebate late in the 2021-22 financial year and Treasury subsequently permitted the carry forward of that funding into 2022-23.  In the absence of an Executive it is not possible to allocate this funding. Any decisions on how that funding is allocated will be for an incoming Executive to agree in line with local needs and priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on reform of the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority, including the appointment of key staff and board members and its functions.
answer_text: RQIA’s restructuring into three operational Directorates is well underway with three Directors appointed on a permanent basis.  RQIA’s Chief Executive was appointed in July 2021, followed by a Director of Hospital Services, Independent Healthcare, Reviews and Audit Programmes and a Director of Mental Health, Learning Disability, Children’s Services Prison Healthcare appointments in December 2021. A Director of Adult Care Services was appointed in January 2022The public appointments process for RQIA's Non-Executive board members is underway and appointments are anticipated during this coming year.  The RQIA Board is operating its committees including its Audit and Risk Assurance Committee with proper arrangements for risk management and financial management in place.  RQIA is functioning as a properly constituted arms length body of the Department of Health, with appropriate accountability arrangements in place, including mechanisms for internal and external audit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the timescale for the publication of revised targets for progressing the reshaping of stroke care.
answer_text: My Department received in excess of 19,000 responses to the ‘Reshaping Stroke Care Consultation’. In the time since the consultation closed, I have been giving careful consideration to the consultation analysis, the evidence base for reform and emerging evidence on stroke treatments. Having done so, I can advise that it is my intention to set out the way forward for the reform of stroke care in the coming weeks and that the Reshaping Stroke Care consultation analysis will be published alongside this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what changes have been made to pest control in health facilities over the last year.
answer_text: HSCTs have confirmed that there has been no change to the provision of pest control in health care facilities over the last year.  A regional contract for the Monitoring and Management of Environmental Services, including pest control provision across health and social care settings, has been in place since 2017. This contract is managed by BSO, Procurement and Logistics Service and is due for renewal later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when Northern Ireland will switch to using HPV testing rather than cytological tests to check for cervical cancer.
answer_text: The Cancer Strategy launched in March 2022 indicated that primary HPV testing would be introduced into the Cervical Screening Programme in 2022/23. My Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) is currently working with the Public Health Agency and Regional Pathology Network to progress this action.  It is anticipated that this will be partially completed within the next 12 months and will be dependent on confirmation of budgets to support this service change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an assessment of first dose COVID vaccination rates among 5-11 year olds in Northern Ireland, compared to the rest of the UK.
To ask the Minister of Health, given that the consultation on Reshaping Stroke Care closed in August 2019, when he will provide a response to that consultation with proposed next steps.
answer_text: My Department received in excess of 19,000 responses to the ‘Reshaping Stroke Care Consultation’. In the time since the consultation closed, I have been giving careful consideration to the consultation analysis, the evidence base for reform and emerging evidence on stroke treatments. Having done so, I can advise that it is my intention to set out the way forward for the reform of stroke care in the coming weeks and that the Reshaping Stroke Care consultation analysis will be published alongside this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the timescale for a Sexual Health Strategy.
answer_text: There are no immediate plans to bring forward a new Sexual Health Strategy. My current focus remains on prioritising limited resources on the post-pandemic rebuild of services, including sexual health services. In the absence of a Strategy, work is underway to renew our sexual health improvement action plan. This will be developed alongside stakeholders and finalised we hope by the year end. The need for a new strategy will be kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the timescale for making Evusheld available in Northern Ireland as a treatment to prevent COVID-19.
answer_text: The drug Evusheld (tixagevimab/cilgavimab), was recently given a Conditional Marketing Authorisation for use in Great Britain by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA). The European Medicines Agency (EMA) have also granted Evusheld a Marketing Authorisation under the Centralised Procedure for the authorisation of new medicines, and this licence applies in Northern Ireland. Evusheld is authorised to be used before being exposed to COVID-19 infection in order to prevent disease. At present there is not a supply of the drug Evusheld available in the UK.Evusheld is being developed as a potential preventative treatment, with AstraZeneca announcing positive trial data. However, this trial took place before the emergence of the Omicron variant. The UK’s Health Security Agency (UKHSA) is carrying out further testing on the treatment’s effectiveness against the Omicron variant. The MHRA approval outlines that the duration of protection against Omicron is not yet known and a higher dose may need to be used to protect against Omicron. We therefore need an understanding of these issues before any procurement can take place. The Department of Health and Social Care in England have asked clinical experts to advise on the most appropriate option for the Health Service in line with all available data, the public health situation and alongside the suite of other treatments available. The assessment of the most appropriate patient groups which could potentially benefit, as well as the deployment and administration process for pre-exposure prophylaxis, are being considered in parallel to inform any next steps. This is a crucial step, as we need to ascertain how it could be used and under what circumstances, to ensure that we know who could benefit the most. Any deployment would be dependent on data showing that Evusheld sufficiently neutralises Omicron variants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider publishing the statistics of the number of patients waiting for planned orthopaedic surgery in each Health and Socal Care Trust within the Waiting Times Statistics.
answer_text: Inpatient and day case waiting list data provided to the Department of Health contain limited information on the specific surgical procedures for which patients are waiting.Waiting lists are not managed by trained clinical coding staff and any procedure codes noted in these data may not fully detail all procedures that are planned or later performed. It is therefore not possible to count the number of patients waiting for orthopaedic surgery specifically.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what lessons have been learned from contact tracing for the Novel Coronavirus that can be applied to restricting the spread of monkeypox.
answer_text: Contact tracing is an established part of the public health response to the management and control of communicable diseases and is part of the wider Public Health Agency (PHA) Health Protection function. The Contact Tracing Service that was established in response to COVID-19 was on a much larger scale than would be required as part of a public health response to the management and control of other communicable diseases including the recently identified Monkeypox virus. There are however a number of relevant lessons learnt from the approach to contact tracing deployed during the pandemic that are being appropriately considered and applied, where required, to the restricting of the spread of Monkeypox. These include the flexing of the contact tracing staff resource in response to variations in positive case numbers; and, the optimisation of digital solutions for accessing and tracing positive cases and their contacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update when the commencement of the trialling of Kuvan on patients in Northern Ireland will take place.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with NICE under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC).I can advise that Sapropterin (Kuvan) for treating hyperphenylalaninaemia in phenylketonuria was endorsed by my Department on 20 October 2021 and is available in Northern Ireland in line with the current NICE guidelines (TA729). Further information on the guidance is available from: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta729

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the provision of a pedestrian and cycle bridge from the Gasworks to Ormeau Embankment, Belfast.
answer_text: The statutory processes for this project have been completed and, following its inclusion in the Belfast Region City Deal, my officials have commenced preparations for the procurement phase. It is estimated that it will take approximately 3.5 years to complete the procurement, detailed design and construction phases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 201/22-27, whether there are any plans to provide a service or guidance for the translation of existing birth certificates into Irish.
answer_text: The Marriage, Civil Partnership and Civil Registration (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2022 enable certificates for life events registered after the introduction of the legislation to be issued with headings in Irish and bi-lingual English and Irish.  The legislation relates to new registrations only and does not apply retrospectively to life events already registered. I have asked officials to consider how existing birth certificates could be reissued into Irish.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the increase in the Universities Hardship Fund and Student Support Fund in Northern Ireland, to ensure it is in line with other parts of the UK.
answer_text: Higher education students who are experiencing genuine financial hardship can apply for additional financial help through Support Funds. The Funds are administered on my Department’s behalf by the five higher education institutions in Northern Ireland and each institution is responsible for assessing students’ needs and issuing support. In academic year 2021/22 a total of £4,809,000 has been made available to the local higher education providers in Northern Ireland. As of 30th April 2022, approximately £3,566,000 had been awarded to students.Similar schemes are in place across the UK, with each administration providing support to meet the needs of its own student population. I remain committed to ensuring that the Support Funds made available by my Department continue to meet the needs of our student population. Officials from my Department closely monitor the demand for this support and the current budget allocated to the local institutions is currently sufficient to address demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he intends to progress plans for a Park and Ride facility in Carryduff.
answer_text: My Department currently has no plans for a new Park and Ride facility in Carryduff.In order to accommodate any additional Park and Ride demand on the Saintfield Road corridor, my officials are currently in the process of acquiring land from the Department of Health to provide an additional 350 parking spaces at the Cairnshill site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the scoping exercise concerning travel costs paid to district nurses and other medical professionals who have to travel by car for work.
answer_text: In light of the recent significant increases in fuel costs, my Department, Employers and Trade Unions have been involved in national and local discussions regarding a potential review of the current Agenda for Change (AfC) Mileage Rates.The national discussions have been led through the NHS Staff Council and have been exploring the options in relation to mileage reimbursement rates. Unfortunately it has not been possible to conclude a National agreement on an increase in fuel reimbursement rates.In light of no nationally agreed position, temporary increases have been agreed in Scotland and Wales until the end of July and June respectively. At the time of those agreements, no decision could be made locally due to election period restrictions. My Department is, however, considering options for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what the timescale is for Departmental staff to return to work full-time in the Causeway Exchange building.
answer_text: My Department is complying with the current Executive guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what information his Department holds on the difference in overall grocery prices between Northern Ireland and Great Britain.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy purchases grocery category prices for Northern Ireland and Great Britain produced by the retail analysts Kantar. Kantar monitors the prices of items purchased by a panel of shoppers in NI and GB and uses these prices to create an average/composite price per pack for certain grocery categories. They also produce an average total grocery price, which refers to all grocery items purchased in a given period and is a combination of all categories.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when she will sign off on the Northern Ireland Housing Executive's implementation of the National Joint Council's pay award of 1.75 per cent; and whether the award will be back-dated to cover the 2021/22 financial year.
answer_text: Housing Executive 2021/22 Pay Remits have been approved

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the reason for the delay in the implementation of the Housing Executive's new Leasehold Extension policy and procedures.
answer_text: Whilst the proposal to introduce a new Leasehold Extension Policy was approved by the Housing Executive’s Board in October 2020 the development of the policy has proven to be technically and legally complex. This resulted in the policy taking longer to develop than the Housing Executive had originally anticipated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what provision exists for the translation of birth certificates into the Irish language; and for his assessment of the awareness of this provision.
answer_text: The Marriage, Civil Partnership and Civil Registration (Amendment) Regulations (NI) 2022 which came into operation on 11 March 2022 enables a person registering a birth to select the language for the registration in either English, Irish or Bi-lingual English & Irish.  This enables the headings on the birth certificate to be produced in the language selected, with the content, the details regarding the birth, in English.  Once the selection is made all future birth certificates are produced in the language selected at the time of registration.   The NI Direct website provides details of the options available and the parents are given the option to select the language when they are completing the registration of birth form (GRO 4) prior to the registration of the birth.  Registration staff, based in local district council offices, make all informants registering a birth aware of the language choice as this must be selected before the registration can proceed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) why pupils with a Statement of Special Educational Needs will not be notified of their post-primary school on 21 May 2022 along with other pupils; and (ii) what assessment has been made of the impact of this decision.
answer_text: The transfer process for Primary 7 children with a statement of special educational needs (SEN) is a statutory process managed entirely separately, to ensure that each child receives a suitable placement that will support and cater to their individual needs.   Education Authority (EA) SEN Link Officers work closely with schools, parents/carers and educational psychologists to review the individual needs of each child on a case by case basis and families are informed once a school placement has been finalised, rather than on a fixed date. The EA SEN Team is working hard to confirm places as quickly as possible. No assessment has been made of the impact of this decision. Parents of children with a statement of SEN with any concerns are encouraged to contact their Link Officer directly or the SEN Helpline which is open Monday – Friday from 9am – 5pm on 028 9598 5960.  Further information is available on the EA website:  www.eani.org.uk/parents/special-educational-needs-sen/sen-contact-details

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how he intends to implement rates relief in Northern Ireland, equivalent to that administered by councils in Great Britain.
answer_text: I have taken your query to refer to the domestic rates sector, since the majority of the non domestic sector has been in receipt of rate holidays since April 2020.There is no direct read across between the domestic rating system here, and that in Britain where Local Authorities have discretion to implement their own relief schemes. A comparison of average rate bills here with council tax bills of households in Britain shows that bills for households here are lower than their counterparts, which also face additional, separate charges for water and sewage.There are already significant Domestic rate reliefs in place here which include the Rate Rebate Scheme, Housing Benefit, Low Income Rate Relief, Disabled Person’s Allowance, Lone Pensioner Allowance, Farmhouse relief, Clergymen’s Homes relief, and exclusions from empty property rates.  Any change in regard to reliefs would necessitate legislative change, and this cannot be progressed without a functioning Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities on what date the Housing Executive's new Leasehold Extension policy and procedures will be implemented.
answer_text: The Housing Executive’s draft Leasehold Extension Policy was approved by its Board in April 2022. As the policy requires Departmental approval, it is now under active consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there is scope to amend the eligibility criteria for home to school transport to be flexible in relation to the qualifying distance.
answer_text: The statutory walking distances, for the purposes of Home to School Transport, are set out in The Education and Libraries (NI) Order 1986.  No changes to the legislation can be considered until a review of the current transport policy is undertaken and any changes to that policy will need to follow due process, including a public consultation. A review of the current policy is on hold pending the outcome of the Independent Review of Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action is she taking to ensure substitute teachers are paid for extra public and bank holidays.
answer_text: Substitute teachers are only paid for the days they actually work in schools and do not receive payment for bank or public holidays.  An exception has been made for the additional bank holiday which has been allocated in respect of the Platinum Jubilee.  Substitute teachers who are already contracted to work in a school at the time of the additional bank holiday on Friday 3 June 2022 and who, as part of their contract, would be working in the school on that day had it not been designated as an optional additional closure date, will receive a day’s pay.  Where this is the case, schools have been directed to book the substitute teacher on the NI Substitute Teacher Register on that date to ensure they receive payment.The Department’s response to the additional bank holiday for the Platinum Jubilee is in line with the precedent set for the Diamond Jubilee in 2012.  It ensures that the substitute teacher does not suffer any detriment and that the day is counted as reckonable service for any entitlement purposes, including incremental progression and future pension calculations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on what action he is taking to increase the Maintenance Loan for Northern Ireland-resident students travelling to study in Great Britain.
answer_text: For the current 2021/22 academic year my Department continues to provide student support to eligible higher education students in the form of a means tested Maintenance Loan of up to £4,840 for students living away from home, £6,780 for students living in London, and a non-repayable means tested Maintenance Grant of up to £3,475 to help with students’ living costs.  In all likelihood the earliest any changes could be made to the student support package as it currently stands would be academic year 2023/24 given lead in times and the lack of an agreed Executive Budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what options are available to ensure that substitute teachers, unable to work on 1 June, are paid for the Jubilee bank holiday.
answer_text: Substitute teachers are only paid for the days they actually work in schools and do not receive payment for bank or public holidays.  An exception has been made for the additional bank holiday which has been allocated in respect of the Platinum Jubilee.  Substitute teachers who are already contracted to work in a school at the time of the additional bank holiday on Friday 3 June 2022 and who, as part of their contract, would be working in the school on that day had it not been designated as an optional additional closure date, will receive a day’s pay.  Where this is the case, schools have been directed to book the substitute teacher on the NI Substitute Teacher Register on that date to ensure they receive payment.The Department’s response to the additional bank holiday for the Platinum Jubilee is in line with the precedent set for the Diamond Jubilee in 2012.  It ensures that the substitute teacher does not suffer any detriment and that the day is counted as reckonable service for any entitlement purposes, including incremental progression and future pension calculations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to ensure that weight loss surgery is available from Health and Social Care NI, similar to NHS provision elsewhere in the UK.
answer_text: The Department is currently working on the development of a new Obesity Strategy for Northern Ireland which is due to be published in 2023. The strategy will outline policy on both the prevention and treatment of obesity, including surgical intervention.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work he is doing to mitigate the impact of inflation on doctors' pensions.
answer_text: The current high rate of inflation is having a significant impact on all staff employed in the HSC as well those who have now retired.  This high impact is also reflected across all areas of society.One of the significant benefits of the HSC Pension Scheme is that pensions in payment to retired members, as well as accrued benefits under the current scheme, are uplifted in line with inflation every April.HSC pensions in payment, and deferred benefits under the current scheme, are revalued based on the Consumer Price Index (CPI) figure from the previous September.Accrued current scheme benefits for active members are revalued based on the same CPI figure plus 1.5%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how respite services will be expanded and fully restored over the current financial year.
answer_text: In February 2022, the Chief Social Work Officer wrote to Trusts to outline my decision to remobilise respite services. To date, significant efforts have been made by Trusts to implement the Public Health Agency Pathway to manage risk, while increasing service provision on a phased basis, throughout a period of high community prevalence of Covid-19. The latest position reported by Trusts shows that the majority have increased availability in adult day care to at least 70% of pre-pandemic capacity. However, this has not been reflected in the volume of attendances as a number of service users have been unable to attend due to health protection guidance that outlines that close contacts should not attend a Health & Social Care setting for a period of 10 days. Short Breaks provision across statutory health & social care settings has significantly increased. However, overall capacity has reduced as a number of respite beds available in care homes have been withdrawn by independent sector providers. Transport continues to be provided on a reduced basis as there is limited scope to increase capacity while adhering to 1m physical distancing guidelines, aligned to recommendations by the World Health Organisation.While it is clear that the Pathway has been an enabler to remobilise services, there remains further adjustments to public health guidance required before services can be fully restore to pre-pandemic levels. Therefore, my officials have written to the PHA asking them to commence an urgent formal review of the Pathway and associated health protection guidelines. Aligned to this process, the Department has requested performance and capacity data across all Trusts, which will be published on the Department’s website in June 2022. I have clearly outlined that this data will be collected and published on a regular basis to monitor remobilisation against rebuild timeframes. Furthermore, I will commit to issuing a periodic communication to all MLAs to outline progress made by the Trusts. It is regrettable that services have yet to be fully restored to pre-pandemic capacity and I sincerely apologise to those families that continue to receive a partial service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how families with children with learning disabilities will be prioritised for respite services following the reduction in services during the pandemic.
answer_text: Respite services play a critical role in supporting disabled children, young people and their families to stay together and reduce the need for intensive late interventions by Health & Social Care. Due to increasing complexity presented by children with disabilities, there has been growing demand for short breaks and long term residential care placements, which has been further impacted by the Covid-19 pandemic. Respite services for families with children with learning disabilities continue to be prioritised on the assessed needs of the individual and their family. There is a recognition at a regional and departmental level that there are insufficient short breaks and residential care placements to meet the growing complexity of Children with Disabilities (CwD).To meet the future service needs, the HSCB (now the Department’s Strategic Planning & Performance Group) developed a Regional Framework for Children’s Disability Services, which proposes a new overarching model of care for children with a disability in Northern Ireland. This draft framework outlines a new model of practice based on the principles of: (i) early intervention; (ii) collaboration and multi-agency working; (iii) co-design and decision making with families; and (iv) person centred care.The implementation of the framework will be underpinned by four strategic work streams, which will require significant transformation across Health & Social Care services:Improving our early offer of help and community based supports; Developing additional effective residential short breaks, fostering and intensive family support;Rethinking our approach to residential provision and how we support CwD effectively in out of home placements; andGetting Transition right The CwD draft framework is currently under consideration by the Department.  It should however it should be noted that the recommendations set out in the framework will require significant re-design of existing services, workforce development and investment.
